outliers.hook: I wake up at 5am each morning.
outliers.text:
I wake up at 5am each morning.

I drink 2 cups of coffee.

Do a DuoLingo lesson. Load my LinkedIn content.

Read for 20 minutes. Take a 3-mile walk. Shower.

Listen to a podcast before my workday starts.

I have 3-4 client meetings lined up.
Make some big decisions.
Drive a key KPI forward.

Call my parents around 4pm.

Peloton.
Shower again.

My wife and I have a healthy dinner.
30 more min of reading.
A cup of tea.

In bed by 9:30p. Couldn't have accomplished more.

Smile as I nod off.

Feeling amazing. That's the perfect day.

Happens MAYBE once a month??
_____

Most days I wake up at 6a, scramble to get through Duolingo while brushing my teeth.

Create my content on the spot. I'm not happy with it. 

Forget to load it. Sh*t. 

No podcast. Just listening to some hip hop. Trying to get motivated.

Scrolling Instagram or LinkedIn. Feeling bad about the state of the world. 

Don't call my parents. Skip the peloton to watch TV. Drink beer. 

Order in delivery food.

Scroll Twitter in bed. 

BUT...

I still sleep well. 

Smiling as I nod off.

A reminder that every day doesn't have to be perfect. You don't have to be on your "A" game every day of every week.

Try your hardest. But, don't beat yourself up when you fail.

Sleep soundly to see another day.

Success is a marathon.

✌🏻

outliers.hook: The 9 to 5 is getting pummeled.
outliers.text:
The 9 to 5 is getting pummeled. 

The great resignation is growing faster than ever.

And I love it. Why?

Because the workforce is tired.

Tired of 3% raises and 7% inflation.
Tired of being treated unfairly.
Tired of long commutes.
Tired of bad bosses.

So they are saying no to the old normal.

And learning to make money on their own...

Coaching.
Teaching.
Helping.
Selling.

They're moving out of high-cost cities and into lower-cost, tax-friendly areas.

Realizing they can support themselves.

Armed with nothing but an internet connection and their own knowledge.

And they won't be going back...

Because they don't want dream jobs.

They want dream lives.

✌🏻

outliers.hook: If you're in your 20's...
outliers.text:
If you're in your 20's...

And you're optimizing for money...

IMO, you're doing it wrong. 

The likelihood of you reaching your earnings ceiling in your 20's is low. 

In your 30's, 40's and 50's???

MUCH higher.

So what should you be doing in your 20s? 

Optimize for experience. 
Optimize for knowledge.
Optimize for people.
Optimize for fun!

Find a company you LOVE. 

A product you're so proud of. 

Work your tail off. 

Work SO hard. 

Gobble up as much knowledge as you can.

Ask everyone you admire for their knowledge.

Find a mentor. 

Build an incredible network of smart people.

Future titans of your industry.

People who LIFT YOU UP. 

Drop them in a Google Sheet

Call/text/email/meetup regularly.

ALSO. 

Don't forget to blow off some steam!

Travel to cool places.
Hang with great friends.
Eat awesome food.
Drink amazing wine.

If you treat your 20s like this...

...the 30s (and I hope) the 40s have a better chance of being lovely.

✌🏻

#travel #networking #health

outliers.hook: Relationships aren't transactions. The faster you figure this out?
outliers.text:
Relationships aren't transactions. 
 
The faster you figure this out? 
 
The bigger, better, and stronger network you'll build. 
 
And if you're going to build your own business, a network of strong, meaningful relationships is a requirement. 
 
So, don't be this person: 
 
"Hey, man - long time, no talk. Wondering if you could help me out..." 
 
Instead, be this person: 
 
"Hey! Just reaching out to see how you and your family are doing. Would love to catch up and hear what's new." 
 
Invest in non-transactional relationships over the next decade. 
 
When you decide to launch your business? 
 
You won't even have to ask for help.

P.S. Networking doesn’t have to be hard.

Here’s how the best do it: https://lnkd.in/ekYTEG5R

outliers.hook: I paid a guy $130 to move a treadmill in 30 minutes.
outliers.text:
I paid a guy $130 to move a treadmill in 30 minutes.

Said he had 8 moves that day.

Do the math.

Have you made $1,040 in one day?

Everyone wants entrepreneurship to be fancy. 

Sometimes it's just movin' treadmills.

Maybe your idea is fancy.

Maybe it isn't.

Either way...go for it.

outliers.hook: I escaped the rat race 2.5 years ago.
outliers.text:
I escaped the rat race 2.5 years ago.

My secret sauce is less ambition.

I don't:

- want to change the world.
- want to build the next unicorn.
- want to be featured on any lists.
- want to get the highest valuation.

Instead:

- I want to spend my time working on things I enjoy with people I enjoy.
- I want to be able to travel wherever I want to, whenever I want to.
- I want to spend way more time with my friends and family.
- I want to stop doing things I don't like doing.

Thinking about what you actually want in life can force different behavior.

Take you down a different path.

A path towards living more intentionally.

Are you copying someone else's life?

Or designing your own?

✌🏻

outliers.hook: Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.
outliers.text:
Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.

The perfect idea.
The right economy.
More money in the bank.
Better market conditions.

But here’s what I’ve learned after building multiple companies:

There is no perfect moment.

The economy will always be shaky.
Your idea will always feel unfinished.
Your savings will always feel too small.
The market will never be perfectly timed.

The people who win don’t wait.

They start.
They launch early.
Sell before it’s polished.
Figure it out as they go.

Because they understand something most people ignore:

You don’t build confidence by thinking.
You build it by moving.

Action creates feedback.
Feedback sharpens your direction.
And direction is what creates traction.

That’s exactly how I built my business.

Not with a perfect plan.

But by showing up every day with consistent motion.

That system became the foundation of everything I run today.

It’s how I turned my social content into:

• $10.5M in revenue
• 42,000 customers
• All without a single sales call

Want to make the same move?

Start here → https://lnkd.in/gBnHYrjW

outliers.hook: You can't build a one-person business.
outliers.text:
You can't build a one-person business.

People won't pay for your work.

Making it as a creator is impossible...

How wrong they were.

Here's what I've learned over 6 years:

The loudest critics are the most afraid.

The biggest skeptics are the least accomplished.

The harshest words come from those who never tried.

The truth?

Growth forces people to question their own choices.

Achievement challenges their limiting beliefs.

Success makes others uncomfortable.

So they try to stop you.

Not because you can't.
But because they're scared you WILL.

I built my business anyway.

You should too.

I recorded exactly how I did it inside of The Creator MBA.

111 lessons on how I built a profitable one-person business.

(mostly here on LinkedIn...)

You'll learn:

→ Real strategies that actually work
→ Systems to scale without burning out
→ How to turn your expertise into income

Ready to try?

Learn more: https://lnkd.in/gBnHYrjW

outliers.hook: I wake up at 4:30 am every morning.
outliers.text:
I wake up at 4:30 am every morning.

Write an entire week's worth of content.

Read for 30 minutes. Take a 6-mile walk. Shower.

Listen to a podcast before my workday starts.

- Look over an investment opportunity.
- I have 2-3 client meetings lined up.
- Complete a business project.

Every afternoon I call my parents at 4 pm on the nose.

Then I Peloton.
Shower again.

My wife and I have a super healthy dinner.
30 more min of reading.
A quick meditation.
A cup of tea.

In bed by 9:45p. Feeling awesome.

Smile as I nod off.

The perfect day.

-----

It's also not true.

It's just a silly made-up fairytale. 

Every time you read something like this, just remember that human beings weren't designed to be productivity machines.

In fact, one of my favorite "productivity hacks" is to stop trying to be productive all of the time.

So...

Sleep in.
Eat a donut.
Drink a beer.
Take a day off.
Skip working out.
Don't write that day.

Whatever.

Life, entrepreneurship, and literally anything else worth doing are a marathon.

Not 10,000 "perfect morning routines" strung together.

With this in mind, I hope you have a fun week.

outliers.hook: You can lose the job. Lose the business.
outliers.text:
You can lose the job. 
Lose the business. 
Lose the money.

But you *cannot* afford to lose yourself.

So why do so many people ignore the one thing they'll live with forever?

Themselves.

I see people do it all the time:

They'll spend 10 hours grinding at work.
Zero minutes asking, "Who am I becoming?"

Plan their weeks down to the minute.
Haven't thought about their life in years.

That was 2018 me.

I thought "winning" meant optimizing everything around me:

- The ideal productivity system
- The perfect morning routine
- Bigger title, more money
- Crazy company growth

Meanwhile, I wasn't living my life mission.

That's when it hit me:

The only asset that matters is the person you become.

So I started treating myself like the #1 project that matters.

My whole life changed after that.

Most people invest in everything except themselves.

They'll drop $5k on a watch but won't spend $50 for self-improvement.

That's backward.

Start with you.
Everything else follows.

Interested in following a similar path?

I documented everything I learned quitting my job in 2019, then going from $0 to eight-figures in 6 years.

(without losing myself in the process)

It's a 100% free read.
↓
https://buff.ly/taCFAUc 

Is today the day you go all in on yourself?

outliers.hook: You're probably 2-3 years away from living the wildest life of your dreams.
outliers.text:
You're probably 2-3 years away from living the wildest life of your dreams.

The big question is:

Do you have the patience?

Nearly every successful person I know decided, in advance, that they were going to do something big with their life.

But they also gave themselves a long runway to achieve it because all incredible things take time.

So here's some advice:

Decide what you want, give yourself a decent window to achieve it, and don't abandon the plan when things are moving slowly.

Because, at some point, it will feel slow.

There will be times when nothing seems to be working, and that goal feels a lot further away than when you started.

I can promise you - that's not necessarily a sign you're failing.

That's just what the middle looks like sometimes.
Keep going anyway.

The people living their wildest lives didn't get lucky.

They just hung around a lot longer than everyone else.

If you're interested in living differently, I write a short Saturday essay each week to help people rethink how they work, earn, and live. 

5 minutes and no marketing spam.

Join 175,000+ here: https://buff.ly/ZMGy5w7

outliers.hook: People think they need a business plan.
outliers.text:
People think they need a business plan.

They don't.

People think they need permission to pivot their career.

They don't.

People think they need approval to build something new.

They don't.

The biggest lie we tell ourselves?

"I need to wait for the right moment."
"I need someone to tell me it's okay."
"I need more experience first."

Here's the truth:

You already have everything you need to start.

The work isn't waiting for permission.
The work is doing the thing.

How many people do you know who spend months planning instead of building?

How many people ask everyone else what they should do instead of just trying?

The most successful business owners I know didn't wait.

They started messy.
They figured it out along the way.
They gave themselves permission to begin.

Your competition isn't other people.

It's your own hesitation.

Stop asking if you can.
Start doing what you want.

You can borrow my entire playbook, which I've built over the last six years.

Watch it here: https://lnkd.in/gBnHYrjW

outliers.hook: The most successful people I know are kinda delusional.
outliers.text:
The most successful people I know are kinda delusional.

They believe they can build anything.
They know their weird idea will work.
They think they'll compete with $1B+ companies...

Everyone else calls them crazy.

"That market is already too crowded."
"You need experience in that industry first."
"You can't start a business without funding."

But here's the thing about "crazy" people:

They don't listen to the noise.

They start anyway.
They build anyway.
They work anyway.

While rational people are making spreadsheets and conducting market research, crazy people are already selling their first product.

The business world needs more delusional optimists.

People who know they can win.
Who believe their voice matters.
Who are willing to look silly while building.

Every major company started with someone who was "crazy enough" to think they could do it better.

It all started with people who ignored the experts telling them it wouldn't work.

Your "crazy" idea might be exactly what the world needs.

Stop listening to reasonable people giving you reasonable advice.

Start building something unreasonable.

Ready to turn your "crazy" idea into a profitable business?

I've helped 45,000+ people start talking about and marketing their ideas right here on LinkedIn.

Here's the entire playbook: https://buff.ly/wJvIV7r

Once you watch it, go promote that new idea and see what happens.

outliers.hook: Don't trade your sanity for a paycheck.
outliers.text:
Don't trade your sanity for a paycheck. 
 
You'll be forever regretful. 
 
All of the money in the world won't save a crushed mind. 
 
Take it from me: 
 
- Find your dream position 
- Prioritize your mental well-being 
- Invest time & energy in passion projects 
- Turn them into your main source of income 
 
You don't need to climb the corporate ladder to be wealthy. 

But you do need a hedge against the instability of the current job market.

Here are 2 ways I'd build one:

1. A simple service business
2. A digital product business

If you want to choose one and get started, here's a guide on how to do it: https://lnkd.in/eKn39rhH
 
Remember to aim for comfortable wealth & freedom. 
 
A more blissful life.

outliers.hook: Parkinson's Law: Work expands to fill the time given for its completion.
outliers.text:
Parkinson's Law: 
 
Work expands to fill the time given for its completion. 
 
That means, as long as you have time to work, there will be work to do. 
 
You can't ever finish everything. 
 
It's a neverending game of more stuff to do. 
 
It doesn't matter how much you eliminate, simplify, automate, or delegate. 
 
You'll never get to the end of emails, projects, meetings, deliverables, etc. 
 
So don't try. 
 
Instead, choose the 20% of important things that move your business forward and quit doing everything else.  
 
Instead, live your life: 
 
- Take walks 
- Go to the gym 
- Read a good book 
- Do something in your community 
 
Work to live. 
 
Don't live to work.

P.S. I'm working on a guide called LIVING: Thoughts on Living With intention.

A breakdown of how to build Life-Work balance.

If that sounds like something you'd enjoy, then join 85,000+ Solopreneurs on my email list: https://lnkd.in/eXv2Mxfg

I'll send it to you when it's done.

That "ASAP" request? It's not life-or-death.
That after-hours email? Tomorrow exists.

Don't sacrifice your mental health because of someone else's poor planning.

You're a human being.
Not a productivity machine.

It's in escaping this situation.

Want to learn how I turned my expertise into a business on my own terms?

It started here on LinkedIn.

outliers.text:

They're going to change the world.

But the *actual* truth is a lot different.

How do I know?

Because I've lived it many times over.

And no matter how much you enjoy work, how much money you make, what kind of impact you create, and how famous and adored you are...

Life sucks if you're neglecting your friends, family, and community.

So for those of you out there grinding because you think someone else's life is worth living...

Don't build someone else's idea of a great life.

Build your own.

outliers.hook: The smartest people I know are the most teachable.
outliers.text:
The smartest people I know are the most teachable.

That sounds backward.

You'd think the more you know, the less you need to learn.

But it works the opposite way.

The people who are really good at something?

They're constantly asking questions.
Admitting what they don't know.
Changing their minds when evidence changes.

The people who are stuck?

They already have all the answers.

I've been running my own business for almost 7 years now.

I still read stuff from people who are just getting started.

Still change my approach when something isn't working.

Still get things wrong every single day.

That's not a weakness.
That's part of the game of entrepreneurship.

The moment you decide you've figured it out is the moment you stop growing.

And in a world that changes this fast, standing still is the same as moving in reverse.

Be the person who's always learning.

Not because you're insecure about what you know.
Because you're curious about what you don't.

I write a weekly newsletter to 180K+ entrepreneurs who want to continuously get better.

Every week I share what's working, what's not, and what I'm still figuring out.

As a bonus, you'll also get my 2026 LinkedIn growth guide free when you sign up. 

It's the playbook I used to go from 2,000 to 815,000 followers.

outliers.hook: You can lose the job. Lose the business.
outliers.text:
You can lose the job. 
Lose the business. 
Lose the money.

But you 𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗼𝘁 afford to lose yourself.

So why do so many people ignore the one thing they'll live with forever?

Themselves.

I see people do it all the time:

They'll spend 10 hours grinding at work.
Zero minutes asking "Who am I becoming?"

Plan their weeks down to the minute.
Haven't thought about their life in years.

That was 2018 me.

I thought "winning" meant optimizing everything around me:

- The ideal productivity system
- The perfect morning routine
- Bigger title, more money
- Crazy company growth

Meanwhile, I wasn't living my life mission.

That's when it hit me:

The only asset that matters is the person you become.

So I started treating myself like the #1 project that matters.

My whole life changed after that.

Most people invest in everything except themselves.

They'll drop $5k on a watch but won't spend $50 for self-improvement.

That's backward.

Start with you.
Everything else follows.

Interested in following a similar path?

I documented everything I learned quitting my job in 2019, then going from $0 to $10.3M in 6 years.

(without losing myself in the process)

outliers.hook: Only foolish people dismiss hard work as "luck."
outliers.text:
Only foolish people dismiss hard work as "luck."

"They're lucky they can work from home."
"They're lucky their business took off."
"They're lucky they have that lifestyle."

Luck?

Behind every "overnight success" is years of work.

Pitching dozens of clients before landing the first one.

Failing at multiple business ideas before finding one that works.

Learning how to market, sell, and deliver value to people.

Reinvesting every dollar back into growing instead of buying stuff.

That's not luck. 
That's called persistence.

Most people see the result, not the process.

Luck is when preparation meets opportunity.

And you create your own luck by showing up consistently, learning from failures, and pushing on when things get hard.

The only difference between people who "get lucky" and people who don't?

The lucky ones keep going when everyone else gives up.

Ready to create your own business luck?

Here's a guide I wrote that breaks down how I built mine from $0 to 8-figures in 5.5 years.

Read it free: https://lnkd.in/ecdsCRDm

outliers.hook: Your brain is sick and tired of content.
outliers.text:
Your brain is sick and tired of content.

It wants you to be creative.

Over the past 6.5 years, I've written:

- 3,559 LinkedIn posts
- 4,107 Tweets
- 789 Instagram posts
- 456 Threads
- 278 Substack notes
- 266 newsletter issues

And here's what I've learned:

1. The more you create, the happier you are:

On the days I'm sharing my creations, my mood is incredibly elevated. There's nothing better than learning something and sharing it with other people.

2. The more you consume, the more pessimistic you become:

Whenever I go down a rabbit hole on LinkedIn or X, I get bummed out. Culture wars, fighting, lack of empathy for our fellow humans. It's gross.

3. The longer you write, the better at your craft you become:

If you write every single day for a long time, you will eventually become a relatively decent writer. And there's no better way to communicate your thoughts.

4. The more you outsource creative work to AI, the dumber you become:

The more AI does your creative work, the more you forget how to be creative and original. You sound like everyone else. You blend in. You're not interesting.

TL;DR:

1. Create daily
2. Consume less
3. Write until you're good
4. Don't outsource creative

I teach 200,000+ creative entrepreneurs how to:

- Get attention
- Keep attention
- Monetize attention

I'll show you how when you join my weekly newsletter: https://buff.ly/iKAl3na

(→ You'll also get my free 2026 LinkedIn writing guide instantly.)

outliers.hook: I lived through Crying CEO Guy.
outliers.text:
I lived through Crying CEO Guy.

I didn't take a mocking selfie. 
I didn't blast him in the comments.
I didn't go to his Instagram and make fun of him.

Want to know why?

Because humiliating people isn't my thing.

And it never will be.

Because I've done and said a lot of really stupid things over the course of my 41 years here on this planet.

And when people have gone out of their way to humiliate me, it sucks.

So a few years ago, I made it a habit to remember that even the silliest mistakes are made by someone's son or daughter, spouse, parent, or sibling.

I've found that to be a helpful thought to revisit when I feel like piling on.

Look...I obviously hope crying selfies don't become the norm.

But, I hope humiliating people on LinkedIn doesn't either.

outliers.hook: Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office?
outliers.text:
Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office? 
 
Or spend all day at your desk? 
 
Me too.  
 
Sure...I understand the camaraderie.  
 
And some things are more easily done in person than virtually. I get that. 
 
But here's something else I think: 
 
Success is not about long hours in the office. Or sitting at your desk from 9 to 5.  
 
It's about good ideas and sharp execution. 
 
And sometimes, the best ideas come when you're away from your desk.  
 
Like at the dinner table, on a run, or playing with your kids.  
 
Spending time with family isn't just nice, it helps us think better. 
 
Don't get stuck on the old idea that more hours at work means more success.  
 
Try something different.  
 
Spend more time living life, and you might find you're better at your job. 
 
Take a step back and think about this.  
 
Maybe a new way to measure success is in the quality of our ideas, and how we execute on them - not the quantity of our hours.  
 
Your desk isn't going anywhere, but your life is.

Build life-work balance. Not the other way around.

More here: https://lnkd.in/eukQAFD5
 
Repost ♻️ if you think being forced back to the office & desk is silly.

outliers.hook: Two years is nothing.
outliers.text:
Two years is nothing.

And also everything!

I started at 38 with zero followers and absolutely no idea what I was doing.

Two years later?

I reached 100,000 followers and achieved $1 million in revenue.

I never could have predicted that on day one.

In fact...it felt like the complete opposite was bound to happen.

The only reason I kept going was because it was fun and interesting.

Here's what most people don't understand:

You can't see the finish line at the start.
You have to trust the process & enjoy the ride.

Build when no one's buying.
Publish when no one's watching.
Keep going when everyone's quitting.

Because two years is both nothing and everything.

Nothing in the grand scheme of the work you'll do.
Everything in terms of what it can change.

Two years of consistent action?

That's 730 days of compounding.
730 chances to get better.

And at the end of those two years?

You won't recognize your life.

New income. 
New freedom. 
New options.

All because you didn't quit when it felt slow.

So if you're just starting or feeling stuck, remember this:

Two years from now, you'll wish you had started today.

Six years ago, I couldn't imagine this...

$11.4M+ business, 800K+ followers, freedom to spend my time as I please.

All because I was "dumb" enough to keep trying.

Your turn. 

Want the complete system that helped me build it?

Join 7,500+ entrepreneurs here: https://buff.ly/EFNO1Of 

(It's 75% off for early Black Friday)

outliers.hook: Yesterday I turned 40.
outliers.text:
Yesterday I turned 40.

Here are 20 helpful lessons I've learned during my life.

👇🏻

1. The 2nd biggest difference between success & failure is persistence.
2. The biggest difference between success & failure is getting started.
3. 100% of people are out for themselves, even if it doesn't appear so.
4. Comparing yourself to others is the easiest way to get distracted.
5. It's difficult to build a work ethic without the right environment.
6. The most important skill to learn is how to learn on your own.
7. If you have a 10-year plan, you'll outperform most people.
8. Getting 1% better at something means leaping millions.
9. Everyone has knowledge that other people will pay for.
10. There is absolutely nothing wrong with being selfish.
11. 10% of people will hate what you do no matter what.
12. If you can't teach yourself, you'll always be a liability.
13. Don't spend time with people who don't reciprocate.
14. You won't do anything special without risk.
15. Divorce from anyone who drains you.
16. Time is the single greatest asset.
17. Money is not the root of all evil. 
18. You can't say yes to everyone.
19. Consistency trumps all traits. 
20. Nearly everyone is winging it.

While I'm bummed to leave my 30s, my wife always reminds me:

"Not everyone gets to be 40."

✌🏻

outliers.hook: I got FIRED 3 times before I was 28.🤦🏼‍♂️
outliers.text:
I got FIRED 3 times before I was 28.🤦🏼‍♂️

I NEVER hit quota in my first 4 sales jobs. 

At 28, I took a job as one of the first salespeople at a tech company in NYC. 

I'll spare you the details on how I got the job.

I moved to NYC and was paid $40k as I turned 29. 

I slept on my friend Mara's couch for 5 months. (Thanks!)

Something funny happened though: An intersection, I'll call it.

The intersection of finding a product, team, culture and city I freaking LOVED.

I got energized. Pumped. Every day. 

I worked NON-STOP. 

I won every award possible.

I got promoted 5 times. 

By 33 I became an Executive. 

I look back and cannot remember the old me. I simply can't fathom it. 

Moral of the story? Find an intersection of some thing, some people, some vibe and some place you love.

Hell, 3 out of 4 will do.

But, when they ALL intersect...well...?

Thats's when you get dangerous 🔥🔥You forget the OLD you. 

Have fun out there today. Go find the NEW you. 🤜🏼

UPDATE: This took off, and I have 1,100 unread messages in my inbox. Very unexpected. I am humbled. Wildly appreciative. Feel free to sign up to get this type of stuff regularly from me: www.justindwelsh.com

Your business isn't a life sentence.

And those seasons change.

outliers.hook: Working for yourself is really hard.
outliers.text:
Working for yourself is really hard.

It often involves:

1. Wrestling with self-doubt, questioning if you're cut out for this.

2. Battling impostor syndrome, feeling like you don’t belong.

3. Juggling multiple responsibilities, trying to keep all balls in the air.

4. Navigating financial challenges, which can be scary as hell.

Yes, it's hard. 

But truth be told, every path is hard.

So, as we roll toward 2024, give yourself some time to think:

Are the challenges you're working on in your current career fulfilling?

If the answer is no, it's never too late to build something new. Something small that may, someday, be something big. 

The beginning of a new year is the perfect time to embrace change and choose the 'right hard' for your journey.

Are you?

~~~

If you found this helpful, consider resharing ♻️ and follow me Justin Welsh for more content like this.

outliers.hook: The people winning aren't smarter.
outliers.text:
The people winning aren't smarter.

They just never stopped learning.

I see this pattern everywhere.

The person who thinks they know it all?

Stuck at the same level for years.

The person who's constantly curious?

Rising fast.

Here's why:

The world changes every day.

What worked last year doesn't work now.
What you learned in school is already outdated.
What got you here won't get you there.

So you've got two choices:

Keep learning.
Or get left behind.

I'm 44 and I still spend hours every week learning.

Reading books.
Testing new strategies.
Staying curious.

Not because I have to.

Because that's what keeps me relevant.

The moment you think you've figured it all out is the moment you start falling behind.

Your competition isn't standing still.
The market isn't standing still.
Technology isn't standing still.

So why would you?

Stay sharp.
Stay curious.
Stay growing.

That's how you keep rising.

If you want to join over 175,000 online entrepreneurs lifting their audience and business-building skills each week, consider joining my newsletter, The Saturday Solopreneur.

outliers.hook: Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office?
outliers.text:
Ever wonder why companies want you to come back to the office? 
 
Or spend all day at your desk? 
 
Me too. 
 
Sure...I understand the camaraderie. 
 
And some things are more easily done in person than virtually. I get that. 
 
But here's something else I think: 
 
Success is not about long hours in the office. Or sitting at your desk from 9 to 5. 
 
It's about good ideas and sharp execution. 
 
And sometimes, the best ideas come when you're away from your desk. 
 
Like at the dinner table, on a run, or playing with your kids. 
 
Spending time with family isn't just nice, it helps us think better. 
 
Don't get stuck on the old idea that more hours at work means more success. 
 
Try something different. 
 
Spend more time living life, and you might find you're better at your job. 
 
Take a step back and think about this. 
 
Maybe a new way to measure success is in the quality of our ideas, and how we execute on them - not the quantity of our hours. 
 
Your desk isn't going anywhere, but your life is. 
 
Build life-work balance. Not the other way around.

Here’s how: https://lnkd.in/ewg6y-i5
 
Repost ♻️ if you think being forced back to the office & desk is silly.

outliers.hook: I noticed something funny about decision-making.
outliers.text:
I noticed something funny about decision-making.

When I'm agitated?

Every option seems important and urgent. 

I quickly jump between choices, convinced each one could be the best or worst decision of my life.

But when I deliberately slow down — take a walk, sit quietly, sleep on it — the noise fades.

The complicated becomes simple. 
The options reduce to two or three. 
The unimportant falls away.

The calmer I am, the more clearly I can see what matters and what doesn't.

This works for everything: business decisions, creative problems, and even relationship issues.

So, next time you're stuck or overwhelmed, try this experiment: 

Wait. 

Do nothing for a day. 
Let your mind settle.
Then decide.

Don't be fooled by the feeling that urgency equals importance. 

Often, it's the opposite.

The best decisions come from a quiet mind.

P.S. If you enjoyed this writing, learn how I built an audience of 700K+ writing for 84.7M+ impressions monthly here: https://lnkd.in/eh9pVVuf

Image credit: pathsofstoicism

outliers.hook: Your words build a prison or a palace.
outliers.text:
Your words build a prison or a palace.

It's your choice.

People spend years telling themselves:

"I'm stuck."
"I'm exhausted."
"I can't figure it out."

Guess what happens?

They stay stuck, exhausted, and confused.

Words aren't just words.
They're blueprints.

Every "I'm tired" reinforces fatigue.
Every "I can't figure it out" keeps you stuck.
Every "I'm not good enough" proves itself right.

Change your vocabulary and you'll change your life.

Start saying:

"I'm learning."
"I'm building."
"I'm growing."

It might feel fake at first.

But your brain will start looking for evidence to support your new story.

You'll find opportunities instead of obstacles.
Lessons instead of losses.
Progress instead of problems.

Your reality follows your words.

Change your words.
Change your world.

If you want to change your story from "stuck employee" to "successful entrepreneur," consider watching my masterclass.

outliers.hook: 50 pieces of advice after 4.5 years as an entrepreneur:
outliers.text:
50 pieces of advice after 4.5 years as an entrepreneur: 
 
1. Nobody is coming to save you. 
2. Respect people's time. 
3. Learn to teach yourself. 
4. You learn 10x more trying than reading. 
5. Learn to sell or you're going to struggle. 
6. Thick skin is a requirement. 
7. Support people, don't tear them down. 
8. Someone will always hate you. 
9. Price your offer for access. 
10. A good network speeds you up. 
11. A bad network slows you down. 
12. Start figuring stuff out on your own. 
13. If people root against you, cut them out. 
14. Always over-communicate. 
15. You can learn from everyone. 
16. Everyone you talk to is also full of sh*t. 
17. Your business heroes are normal. 
18. If you can make $1 you can make $1k. 
19. And $1k means you can make $10k. 
20. Talk to your customers. 
21. You can't 'hope' a business to life. 
22. Do interesting things & attract mentors. 
23. Promote yourself online every day. 
24. Write daily for at least 30 minutes. 
25. Ignore haters. 
26. Help others. 
27. Go to therapy and be honest. 
28. Drink less alcohol. 
29. Start with one revenue stream.  
30. Diversify later to survice. 
31. Systems beat motivation. 
32. Quit focusing on tools and tech.  
33. Experiment often. 
34. Use data to create better content. 
35. Never plagiarize people but borrow. 
36. Stop trying to be cool. Be successful. 
37. You're not clever. Be clear. 
38. Be on time for important things. 
39. Pessimists are often right.  
40. Optimists are wealthy. 
41. Don't be arrogant, but be strong. 
42. Try a lot of different things. 
43. Don't argue with your customers. 
44. Everyone has the same 24 hours. 
45. Don't study Elon if you're making $0. 
46. You can't control your past. 
47. Arguing online is a waste of energy. 
48. Starting a business > reading biz books. 
49. Anyone selling 'instant results' is a con. 
50. You're 100% responsible for your future. 
 
Take every piece of advice here with a grain of salt. 
 
I'm just some random guy on the internet sharing what I've learned on my very specific journey, so it might not translate 100% for you. 
 
But, if there is one takeaway, it's this:  
 
Get started. 
 
That's how you'll figure out your own journey. 
 
I hope this list helps you avoid some of the mistakes I made over the last 4.5 years. 

P.S. I put every lesson I know about online entrepreneurship inside of The Creator MBA. 14 Chapters, 111 Lessons, and 19 hours of instructional video that will teach you how to build a lean, profitable internet business (even if you're just starting or don't have an idea).

You can learn more here: https://lnkd.in/egPdqha6

Have a great Wednesday.
 
~~~ 
 
✍️ Which is your favorite? 
♻️ Reshare if this hit home.

outliers.hook: You need delusion.
outliers.text:
You need delusion.

You need audacity.

Sanity is actually a liability.

Every entrepreneur I know was told they were being unrealistic.

"That won't work."
"You don't have the experience."
"Why would anyone pay you for that?"

The "realistic" people said all of this.

And the realistic people are still exactly where they were five years ago.

Meanwhile, the delusional ones?

They built the thing anyway.

They launched before they were ready.
They ignored people who said it wouldn't work.
They believed in something no one else could see.

Remember:

You cannot build something new while thinking like everyone else.

The timing will never be perfect.
The odds will never be in your favor.
The math will never make sense at the start.

You have to be a little delusional to hit "publish" anyway.

A little audacious to charge money for your ideas.

A little insane to bet on yourself when no one else will.

The people who play it safe don't build anything.

They just watch and wonder.

If you're ready to stop wondering and start building, I just launched a free 30-day challenge that helps you earn $1,000 from your expertise.

Then you learn why.

Wasted time on the wrong strategies.

But every failure taught me something I couldn't learn any other way.

That's how you get good.

Not by avoiding mistakes.
By making them faster and learning from them.

The test comes first.
The lesson comes after.
That's the deal.

outliers.text:
A stupid simple way to stand out in business (and life):

Be extremely easy to work with.

Be an optimist.
Be a pleasant person.
Respond quickly to requests.
Always do what you said you'd do.

That's the basics.
That's really the whole strategy.

An alarming number of people are bad at all of these things.

They're pessimistic.
They're miserable to be around.
They take weeks or months to respond.
They drop the ball on every major project.

Then, when that eventually catches up to them (it always does), they tell the whole world that everyone else is the problem.

I've worked with a lot of incredible people over 23 years.

The ones I stay in touch with had a few things in common:

They were excellent at their job.
They made my life (and MY job) easier.

Want to build a better life and business?

Clear that bar.

It's a competitive advantage that costs you nothing but a little time and extra energy.

outliers.hook: True wealth isn't in your wallet.
outliers.text:
True wealth isn't in your wallet.

It's the time you spend living life.

I've downgraded my life several times:

Fewer possessions.
Simpler business.
Easier routine.

Each subtraction added more luxury.

What I gained:

- Days with room to think.
- Time to think without interruption.
- Sleep that actually feels like sleep.
- The ability to be present with my wife.

But, we're sold the opposite: 

More meetings, faster responses, constant updates, bigger everything.

But here's the thing:

I might make less money when I create less content.

I might miss "opportunities" when I don't respond to DMs.

I might fall behind the new trends when I read books, not feeds.

But I don't care.

I'm not interested in being busy and important. 

I want the freedom *not* to be.

What small thing could you subtract today to add a little luxury to your life?

Delete it.

outliers.hook: 1,012 tech companies have laid off workers in 2023.
outliers.text:
1,012 tech companies have laid off workers in 2023. 
 
21,000 Meta employees - cut. 
 
12,000 Google employees - gone. 
 
The "safe" job might not be as safe as you think it is. 
 
And some people are pissed off. 
 
But behind the anger, there's a bigger opportunity. 
 
An opportunity to prepare. 
 
Because when profit is down, shareholders are panicked, and board members are upset, layoffs are the next thing coming. 
 
One moment you're a valuable piece of the "family", and then suddenly you aren't. 
 
That sucks. 
 
No matter how loved you feel, no matter how close you are to your boss, no matter how much they rolled out the red carpet...in the end? 
 
It's a math decision. 
 
You're a number on a spreadsheet. 
 
I've been there. 
 
Is it fair? Maybe not. 
Is it true? 100% 
 
The earlier you realize this in your career, the better off you are. 
 
So start future-proofing yourself. 
 
If you're fortunate enough to be employed, start building out your online brand, share your thoughts & learnings, and engage with other talented people. 
 
Create small side hustles and projects. 
 
Network like crazy. Make yourself impossible to forget. 
 
Become synonymous with something that can generate a little income should you need it. Anything. 
 
If you've been let go from your job, don't put the "Open for Work" badge on your profile. 
 
Instead, go create something: 
 
- Attention 
- Content 
- Software 
- A newsletter 
- A blog 
- Audio 
- Video 
 
Whatever. Just create! 
 
Don't tell people you're open for work. SHOW them you're worth working with. 
 
Stand out. 
Make noise. 
Be different. 
Be in as many places as you can be. 
 
And when you get hired again, please don't stop. 
 
Always have a plan B. 
 
Always be prepared.

Here’s 2 ways to start: https://lnkd.in/eGzFY6Tg

outliers.hook: Energy doesn't lie.
outliers.text:
Energy doesn't lie.

Listen to it.

When something is right for you, it will give you energy.

That's how you know.

Not because it's easy.
Not because it's comfortable.

But because it lights you up!

Most people ignore this.

They chase things that drain them.

The job that pays well but kills their soul...
The project that looks good but feels heavy...

And they wonder why they're exhausted.

Energy is the signal.

When I started writing online, it gave me energy.

Not because it was easy. It wasn't.
Not because I was good at it. I wasn't.

But it felt right.

Every piece of content, even the bad ones, made me want to write the next one.

That's how I knew.

Compare that to corporate.

Every email felt heavy.
Every meeting drained me.
Every project took more than it gave.

Same effort. 
Different energy.

One was right for me. One wasn't.

If you're doing work that drains you, maybe it's not your work.

If you're ready to build something that gives you energy instead of taking it, 45,000+ students (including 200+ LinkedIn Top Voices) used the LinkedIn Operating System to find work that feels right, talk about it, and grow an audience that cares about it.

You can watch it here: https://buff.ly/soidgrf

Energy doesn't lie.

Follow it.

outliers.text:
The longer you focus on things outside of your control? 
 
The longer your eye is off the ball. 
 
Set aside news, anger, vitriol, clickbait, and whatever else is keeping you from being truly focused. 
 
Get your body and mind in shape, help other people, love your family, and spend some time learning about the world. 
 
Do these things and the outcomes you're looking for ($$ + impact) become pretty likely.

outliers.hook: Every big change in my life started the same way —
outliers.text:
Every big change in my life started the same way —

I was afraid.

Quitting my job? Terrifying.
Moving to the mountains? Uncomfortable.
Starting my business? Felt impossible.

But fear shouldn't be a stop sign.

Think of it as an arrow.

The phone call you don’t want to make.
The idea that won’t leave you alone.
The move that feels risky.

They’re not obstacles.
They’re invitations.

So, I try and follow a simple rule:

If something makes me nervous, I try my best to lean in.

Not because I like fear. I don't.

But because I know what’s on the other side — something great.

What you resist gets bigger.
What you confront gets smaller.

What's scaring you right now?

Maybe that's exactly where you need to go next.

---

Enjoy this?

If so, learn how, through writing, I've built an audience of 700K+ followers and 375.6M+ impressions on LinkedIn: https://lnkd.in/eh9pVVuf

outliers.text:
You're not behind.

You're still becoming.

I see people in their 20s panicking because they haven't "made it" yet.

People in their 30s thinking they missed their chance.

People in their 40s believing it's too late.

But here's the truth:

Life doesn't follow a timeline.

- Lost at 22.
- Broke at 28.
- Unsure at 31.
- Rising at 35.
- Entrepreneur at 38.
- Building a dream life at 44.

There's no deadline for success.

I was 38 when I started my business.

Burned out. Starting from scratch. No clue what I was doing.

By 44, I built a $10M+ business.

Not because I was ahead.

Because I trusted the process and kept moving.

Most people quit because they compare their chapter 3 to someone else's chapter 20.

But your story is yours.

Stop rushing.
Stop comparing.
Just keep building.

Greatness isn't rushed.

It's earned one day at a time.

This is exactly why I built The Creator MBA.

111 lessons showing you how to turn "starting over" into "taking over."

Everything I know about becoming an entrepreneur:

- Building an audience
- Choosing a profitable niche
- Creating your first offer

outliers.hook: Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.
outliers.text:
Most people wait for the perfect moment to start their business.

They're waiting for:

The perfect idea.
The right economy.
More money in the bank.
Better market conditions.

But here's what I learned after building multiple companies:

There's never a perfect time to start anything.

Your idea will never feel ready.
The economy will always have problems.
You'll never have enough money saved.
The market will always be "uncertain."

The people who succeed aren't the ones who wait for perfect conditions.

They're the ones who start anyway.

They launch before they're ready.
They sell before the product is perfect.
They hire before they can afford it.

Because they know something most people don't:

You can't steer a parked car.

Movement creates momentum.
Action creates opportunity.
Starting creates solutions.

Every successful business owner I know has one thing in common:

They stopped planning and started building.

They figured it out along the way.
They adapted when things didn't work.
They kept moving when others stood still.

Your business will never be perfect.

But it can be profitable.

Stop waiting for everything to align.
Start building something that matters.

Want to see exactly how I built my first profitable business while everything was falling apart around me? 

I share the messy truth about starting when nothing feels right.

Read my complete story here: https://lnkd.in/eABypvdg

outliers.hook: Everyone on LinkedIn needs to read this post.
outliers.text:
Everyone on LinkedIn needs to read this post.

Did you know that 33% of people have trouble reading your LinkedIn posts due to sight or hearing disabilities?

These individuals depend heavily on tools and apps called screen readers. And we are not making it easy.

Here are 7 ways you and I can be more inclusive:

1. Stop using bold or italic formatting in your posts.
Formatted text sounds like complete jibberish on audio.

2. Tone down your emoji usage. If you can, avoid them.
Emojis are weird when actually pronounced on audio.
A good way to use them is at the end of sentences. 👌 

3. Write shorter, more concise sentences.
Listening is hard enough. Keeping up shouldn't be.

4. Always add ALT text to your image posts.
A simple description of what's in the image will do.
Example: Jasmin Alic smiling directly into the camera.

5. Don't use all caps unless you really have to.
An abbreviation like ROFL is okay. But THIS isn't.

6. Make your posts readable in 60 seconds or less.
A rule of thumb is to keep them under 1,100 characters.

7. Write hashtags in Pascal Case. Never as one word.
Capitalize every new word e.g. #BeMoreInclusive 

Remember:

Inclusivity benefits everyone. Not just disabled people.

Writing good content is almost like a superpower.

But making it accessible makes you a hero. ❤️ 

P.S. Repost this. Let's all do better to be more inclusive.

outliers.hook: Apparently, LinkedIn can't figure out I'm real. 💔
outliers.text:
Apparently, LinkedIn can't figure out I'm real. 💔

1. I was restricted 5 times this year. Logged out of my profile due to "suspicion of automated activity".

(There was NO automated activity)

2. A recent post got locked because I had a 52% engagement rate in the first 15 minutes. Bro, how can I even control people reacting to a post?!

3. I must be triggering all the "false positives" in the algorithm, as Jeremy from Favikon told me. It's just "unheard of" to get this level of engagement.

Sorry, but...

LinkedIn and Ryan Roslansky, what in the world do you want me to do?

Serious question.

I don't use automations.
I don't have virtual assistants.
I write all of my content manually.
I write every single comment myself.
I send all my DMs and connects manually.
I'm the only person with access to my profile.
I have precisely 0 tools connected to my profile.
I have precisely 0 extensions installed for LinkedIn.

So tell me...

How much more REAL do you need me to be?

I will NOT stay silent anymore!!!

My 2 cents:

(And my offer to fix this at the end of this post)

1. This issue is showing your incompetence. 1000s of users are going through this; not just me.

Please explain your restriction policies transparently.

2. You ask ME for information about my restriction. How would I know? Each of the 5 times, I wasn't even online!

YOU should be the one giving ME the information. 🤦‍♂️

3. You have access to everyone's data and activity. And you can easily see which tools are connected. So why pretend like you don't? Which brings me to:

If you see someone uses ZERO automation tools, how can you even restrict them for automation?

4. Your Support ticket system doesn't work. Period.

To top it all off, you told me I "reached the maximum number of verification attempts". What even is that?!

5. You are putting people's livelihoods at risk. Because you've pushed users for years to buy Premium, use LinkedIn for business, and then... what, shut 'em down?

Here's my offer:

Dear LinkedIn team,

As one of the more prominent creators on your platform, I feel obligated to address this. Book me a meeting with your team (in charge of this issue) and I will book a flight to either your New York or Sunnyvale headquarters in the next 48 hours. I will spend as much (unpaid) time as needed to help you figure this out. I will use my own money to fund this. I want to do more for your users than you are doing right now. Get me that meeting. I'm looking at flights right now. I MEAN THIS 1000%.

I've looked at legal counsel on this matter too.

I'm happy to involve lawyers in our conversations moving forward. By all means.

This... NEEDS to be fixed once and for all.

Not just for me. For everyone.

Enough is enough.

Thank you.

Signed:

Frustrated long-time user, LinkedIn Learning Instructor, LinkedIn-featured creator, top-ranked global creator, we-ve-also-met-in-person, you-literally-pay-me-a-salary-every-month, your Jasmin Alić

(Asking my network to repost this ♻️)

outliers.hook: This is my most requested post ever.
outliers.text:
This is my most requested post ever.

After 300K followers and 53K people taught, I can tell you with confidence ALL the advice in this post works.

Here's my complete LinkedIn guide for beginners:

(Save + Repost this before anything else ♻️)

---

1. First things first...

Make a decision. LinkedIn is not like all the other platforms. It takes mad time and effort to build here.

Make a decision = next 6 months, you're locked in!

---

2. How many times should you post?

In the beginning, frequency is good. But only for so long. Start off with 7x posts the first few weeks. You'll learn what works, what types of posts you enjoy more. But as soon as you're clear on that, try 3-4x posts. Maximum.

Quantity is never the goal.

Things to remember:

a. Pick 2-3 topics you want to be known for
b. Write 60% educational posts → authority
c. Write 30% personal posts → personability
d. Write 10% "broad" themed posts → virality
e. Publish ONLY if you have something to say

---

3. Instead of posting daily, comment daily (a lot)

Create 4 lists of profiles for daily commenting:

a. Your ICPs → Ideal Client Personas
b. Peers → leverage competitors' views
c. Bigger influencers → leverage their views
d. LinkedIn buddies → others in your follower range

The quality of your comments matters the most.

Bonus: Being early on each of the posts above WILL give you more profile views, and opportunities.

Aim for 50 comments per day = great habit.

---

4. Before you do 1-2 above, optimize your profile

More profile views won't mean anything if people are not clear on what to do on your profile.

(I recently dropped my complete profile optimization guide too - slide back on my profile, you'll find it)

---

5. Make an offer every single day

When I say "make an offer", I mean "send DMs" to warm prospects. Oh, the free acount here is limiting.

Get LinkedIn Premium → It's worth it.

Send DMs to your favorite commenters.
Send DMs to ICPs within your commenters.
Send DMs to ICPs from the "reactions" list too.
Send DMs to your Profile Viewers (primarily ICPs).

Things to remember:

a. Never pitch in your first DM (no pitchslapping)
b. Personalized voice / video notes work super well
c. Personalized guide / loom videos work even better
d. Use Linkbound to find leads inside your interactions

The goal of each DM? To book calls. Nothing more.

---

Bonus tips (post is getting long, sorry):

1. Stop tagging big creators to get more reach
2. Focus on profile views, not post impressions
3. Unfollow 5-10 people every day in your feed
4. Only comment on posts that are "on-brand"
5. Treat every single comment like a mini-post
6. Follow creators in regions you're selling to
7. Share freely + support everyone. Period.

---

Read this far? You the best. 🤝

This guide will help thousands of new LinkedIners today looking for guidance. I know this. So thanks for reading and pushing this post further!

Happy LinkedIn-ing!

- Coach J ❤️

P.S. Ask me anything about LinkedIn - I'm yours :)

outliers.text:
Most LinkedIn gurus charge $500/hr for this strategy.

If you're looking for a new job, new client, or new gig, these 7 steps will get you closer to your goals:

1. Pick 2-3 topics you're experienced in
Ex. copywriting, website conversion, email strategy

2. Publish 5 posts per week (Monday - Friday)
Ex. 4 informational, 1 announcing you're available

3. Focus every single post on just 1 main takeaway
My strategy: 1 post > 1 topic > 1 message

4. Find 20-30 LinkedIn creators in your niche(s)
Make a list of their posting times in a Google Sheet

5. Leave 1 meaningful comment on their every post
Try to comment early > for maximum profile visibility

6. Reply to 2-3 other comments on those same posts
This puts you in front of new people = new chances

7. DM potential new clients/connections after 2 weeks
Wait until you've spoken min. 10 times in the comments

Bonus: Post on Sundays too. Publish a personal post.
Ex. talk about a huge challenge - people relate to this

LinkedIn is no longer a job search platform. It's a platform where you create your own opportunities.

To all job searchers and client machines, good luck!

P.S. Repost this ♻️ Someone out there might use it!

outliers.hook: I've optimized more than 700+ LinkedIn profiles.
outliers.text:
I've optimized more than 700+ LinkedIn profiles.

Today, I'm sharing my 2025 guide to high-converting profiles on LinkedIn, based on real-world results.

(Save and repost this one for others ♻️)

First of all:

1. I don't mind sharing this for free
2. Sharing this doesn't damage my business
3. Knowledge like this helps everyone on LinkedIn

Happy implementation! 😉

---

1. Headline (The pre-profile experience)

The first 45 characters matter the most, because they are visible everywhere OFF of your profile. This is what gets prospects ON your profile in the first place.

1 rule only: Clarity.

Example:

a. I empower founders to find more freedom and value in their work - Financial Consultant

This is super unclear, as literally "anyone" could say they help clients find more freedom and value.

The "clear" version: I help founders manage their money and decrease yearly overhead by up to 200%

The rest (after 45 characters)?

Add keywords e.g. Speaker, Financial Consultant = useful for LinkedIn's Search feature + SEO.

---

2. Banner (The Crown Jewel)

The banner is SO underutilized! This is what prospects "see" first when they open your profile (data-backed).

The goal? Communicate your offer.

Make your client say "Aha" before ever swiping down your profile + add a CTA in your banner.

80% of profiles I've worked on didn't have a CTA inside the banner. Mind-boggling. You NEED one!

The new slide-able LinkedIn banners? Some tips:

1. Add 5 slides, with 1 word on each
2. Add 2-3 slides for your main offers
3. Ask 1 question on each slide for 1 offer

---

3. Profile link (The Blue Magic)

UNLESS you are running a newsletter campaign...

Choose the "old" blue URL, not the new pre-determined Premium ones that are "fixed" in the main feed. 

Why always blue?

No one makes purchase decisions in the main feed.

Every prospect will visit your profile first, then buy. 90%+ of my clients see more success with the blue link.

 Which link to use? Same as the CTA in your banner.

---

4. Same link as above should be FIRST in your Featured section. This means "Repeat your offer 3 times":

Banner → Bluey → Featured (The trifecta)
 
---

5. About (The "Not About You" section)

Follow the PAS framework to:

• Ask questions about your prospects' Painpoints
• Agitate the "ideal scenario" right after that
• Position yourself as the Solution

(Never talk about yourself sooner than this) 

I use PAS for 95% of the profiles I work on → Gold!

Bonus: About sections are indexed into search results, so make sure they are keyword-optimized for SEO.

---

6. Experience (Career Story section)

Don't treat this like a CV summary.

Instead, write stories for each job experience.

This is the coolest part of a LinkedIn profile for me → These make for SUCH great ice-breakers.

---

Read this far? Virtual handshake. 🤝

This guide is literally worth thousands of dollars. So I really hope you appreciate it. Cheers!

- Coach J ❤️

P.S. Ask me anything about LinkedIn - I'm yours :)

This puts you in front of new people = new chances

9. DM potential new connections after 2 weeks
Wait until you speak min. 10 times in the comments

10. Jump on networking calls in your spare time
Exchange advice and make some new friends, eh!

LinkedIn is no longer a job search platform. It's a platform where you create your own opportunities.

To all hiring managers and business owners:

Post a job ad under this post today. Thank you.

2024 is the year everything changes for you. ❤️

outliers.hook: LinkedIn's 7 Rules of Kindness 🫶
outliers.text:
LinkedIn's 7 Rules of Kindness 🫶 

(everyone should be doing this)

1. See a post you like?
↳ Don't just "like". Comment too.

2. See a post with zero likes?
↳ Be the 1st person that shows love

3. Someone tags you in a comment?
↳ Do your best to respond + thank them

4. Commenting on a post you (really) like?
Leave 4 comments in total (1 main + 3 replies)

5. Have zero clue what to comment on a post?
↳ Tag the author + leave a message of support

6. See a "small" profile publish "great" content?
↳ Show support regardless of "your" follower size

7. Support every person regardless - everywhere
↳ Let every person online be your "walking referral"

Remember: It costs $0 to support others online.

P.S. REPOST this ♻️ → be kind 😇

outliers.hook: "You can't share 100% of your knowledge."
outliers.text:
"You can't share 100% of your knowledge."

Oh but I can. For free.

Every time they complain, I prove them wrong. I've been doing this for years, and I don't plan to stop.

Just this year, I gave away:

1. My full LinkedIn guide for beginners
2. My full LinkedIn DM outreach guide
3. My full LinkedIn engagement guide
4. My 24-hour client closing strategy
5. My entire 2025 LinkedIn strategy

The irony? This isn't even 1% of what I coach. Not even "the tip of the iceberg", as the phrase goes.

I'm not earning LESS because of it. On the contrary...

I am still booked out.
I get invited to speak globally.
My Link Up community is thriving.

(best community out there, period)

Because your math? Give 100% = lose 100%.

My math? Give 1% = get 1000% back.

Keep gatekeeping. Please do.

The rest of us will keep building our businesses (yes, plural) because we're not afraid to share the pie.

Thank you. ✌️

P.S. 1 reposter today will get a Link Up membership. ♻️

outliers.hook: LinkedIn once promoted this to 1B users.
outliers.text:
LinkedIn once promoted this to 1B users.

Today, it's yours. My full engagement playbook!

Millions on LinkedIn are using my engagement strategy today. I taught this to the biggest creators. LinkedIn even called me about this. You've probably seen it:

1. The 1+3 comments on others' posts
2. The non-AI, mini-post type of comments
3. The pinned comments under your own posts

I'm proud it's all become a trend over the years. ❤️

Now, let's dive in.

(Save + Repost this before anything else ♻️)

---

1. Without commenting, you won't grow

Rule no. 1: Accept this. No excuses. You HAVE TO be present in the comments, not just in your posts.

---

2. Comments give you more visibility

Ideally, you'll post 1x per day. That's 7 posts weekly (at most). But realistically, you'll comment 20, 30 or more times per day. This is your unfair advantage.

---

3. Never "respond" to posts → "Add" to them

Think of comments as "mini thoughts" on others' posts. Someone mentioned 7 tips? You write number 8. :)

Always "add" to the conversation.

(Like a true expert)

---

4. Where to comment? Whose accounts? 

4a: When you don't know who's active in your niche

The manual way: Use LinkedIn search (upper left corner) and search for keywords in your niche e.g. "fitness".

3 filters: 

"Posts" + Sort by "Latest" + "Last 24 hours"

This will ONLY show you active and relevant folks. Hit "Follow". Tools like Extrovert and Aware can help find these in seconds, by the way.

--

4b: When you DO know who's active in your niche

Same search filters as above + 1 extra one: 

"From member"

This is where you add all the people you just followed.

4 groups, always:

1. Your prospects / ICPs (10 people, 1 list)
2. Your competitors / peers (10+ people, 1 list)
3. Your LinkedIn buddies (similar following size)
4. Bigger influencers (always leverage their reach)

Now, bookmark these in your browser for easier access. 4 bookmarks, 4 separate lists.

These are your "custom feeds". With 100% relevant posts, which makes commenting 10x easier and faster!

---

5. Commenting on your own posts

Aka. My "Pinned commenting" strategy

Always add a few comments under your own posts:

1. Bonus points - not covered in the main post
2. Fun facts or infographics or screenshots
3. Questions - organize AMA sessions
4. BTS - behind the scenes content
5. Announcements and promos
6. Links - much better place
7. Repost call-to-action

---

Ok, this is getting long now. 7 things to remember:

1. Never use AI or automation for your commenting
2. Never try to chase some magical "daily number"
3. Always focus on supporting 3-5 new folks daily
4. Always support accounts with "zero" reactions
5. Understand that your comments are "content"
6. Repurpose best comments as LinkedIn posts
7. Your network sees your every comment too!

And never (ever) join engagement pods. Thank you.

---

Read this far? Thank you. Leave a comment with your thoughts or repost if this was useful. ♻️

- Coach J ✌️

P.S. Ask me anything :)

I'm just a normal guy from Bosnia doing what I love. 😇

I've grown up in a city with a 100,000 population.
I never thought I'd see $100,000 in my (entire) life.

Today, over 100,000 people follow me on LinkedIn.
Today, I'm lucky to build multi-6-figure businesses.

They say numbers don't matter. It's vanity metrics...

outliers.hook: Most LinkedIn DMs are pushy. Or salesy.
outliers.text:
Most LinkedIn DMs are pushy. Or salesy.

Let's fix that. (Full outreach masterclass)

This read is 2 minutes long. So either keep reading, or save it for later. Either way, you're in for a treat!

I will explain my tried-and-tested "warm outreach" approach. Using Linkbound, Kondo and Sales Nav.

(neither is a must, but each one ties into the other)

1. Familiar faces first

Most people reach out to complete strangers. This is why "selling" feels odd. There's no trust. Instead, focus on those you've already interacted with on LinkedIn.

2. Linkbound for engagement filtering

Analyze your engagement (or from your competitors if you don't get much engagement) with Linkbound. It will give you a complete list of "warm leads".

3. Filter leads to match your ICP (ideal client profile)

Linkbound has a brand-new feature which tailor-matches your ICP criteria to your offer. It means every name is both "familiar" + "qualified".

4. Feed into your LinkedIn CRM (Sales Nav)

Follow their LinkedIn activity closely. In other words:

5. Become "top of mind"

The easiest way to send that first DM is to spark engagement elsewhere. In a comment. In a reply. On their post. On someone else's post. Doesn't matter.

6. Attract profile views

Every profile view you receive is a "warm" signal. You can now easily send the first DM saying "Hey, so happy you visited my profile! What brought you here?"

7. Send DMs daily

Make "reaching out to ICPs" a daily habit. No excuses. It will feel angsty at first. The more you do it, the easier and more enjoyable it gets. I promise you this!

8. Never pitch in the first message. Please.

9. Always follow the PBS psychology

Permission-Based Selling means you never outright send something (pitch deck, offer, link) "assuming" they need it. Always ask first. And get a Yes first.

10. Separate DMs into folders, with Kondo

Depending on who you message, you might have different conversations. Kondo (made by Mitchell Tan) lets you have multiple LinkedIn inboxes. Brilliant!

11. Set follow-ups in Kondo

I recommend setting alerts in Kondo for "no-replies". You're not necessarily being ghosted when someone doesn't reply. You just have to remind them.

12. Track progress in Linkbound

The more you talk to each person, readying the "sale", simply drag and drop your conversations further. You'll see your leads qualified in real time to a Yes or No.

13. Feed back into Sales Nav

A lead that says No now is not a "forever No". Keep up with their LinkedIn updates. Their network might be packed with new opportunities for you. Repeat 1-12.

TL;DR:

• Establish familiarity first by engaging
• Use Linkbound to qualify before outreach
• Use Kondo for streamlined DM management
• Use Sales Nav to source network opportunities
• Never pitchslap :)

Familiarity → Filtering → Permission → Trust → 💰

Really hope you find this useful. Cheers!

- Coach J

P.S. I'll reward 2 reposters. One with a year of Link Up membership. One with 6 months of Linkbound! ♻️

outliers.hook: Every person on LinkedIn needs to read this post.
outliers.text:
Every person on LinkedIn needs to read this post.

(I posted this 6 months ago, but it wasn't enough)

Did you know that 33% of people have trouble reading your LinkedIn posts due to sight or hearing disabilities?

These individuals depend heavily on tools and apps called screen readers. And we are not making it easy.

Here are 7 ways you and I can be more inclusive:

1. Stop using bold or italic formatting in your posts.
Formatted text sounds like complete jibberish on audio.

2. Tone down your emoji usage. If you can, avoid them.
Emojis are weird when actually pronounced on audio.
A good way to use them is at the end of sentences. 👌

3. Write shorter, more concise sentences.
Listening is hard enough. Keeping up shouldn't be.

4. Always add ALT text to your image posts.
A simple description of what's in the image will do.
Example: Jasmin Alic smiling directly into the camera.

5. Don't use all caps unless you really have to.
An abbreviation like LOL is okay. But THIS isn't.

6. Make your posts readable in 60 seconds or less.
A rule of thumb is to keep them under 1,100 characters.

7. Write hashtags in Pascal Case. Never as one word.
Capitalize every new word e.g. #BeMoreInclusive

Remember:

Inclusivity benefits everyone. Not just disabled people.

Writing good content is almost like a superpower.

But making it accessible makes you a hero. ❤️

P.S. Repost this. Let's all do better to be more inclusive.

outliers.hook: Good news! I made a 7-day LinkedIn plan for you.
outliers.text:
Good news! I made a 7-day LinkedIn plan for you.

Actually... 3 plans for 3 stages → find yours.

(Save this post + Repost before continuing)

Beginner: Needs consistency (first 1-2 months)

Day 1: Spend the day optimizing your profile (all of it)
Day 2: Unfollow everyone "irrelevant" in your feed 
Day 3: Comment on 20+ posts in your new feed
Day 4: Write your personal introduction post
Day 5: Write your first "advice" type post
Day 6: Send 5 DMs to 5 commenters
Day 7: Repeat 3, 5 and 6 weekly

Intermediate: Needs growth (2-12 months)

Day 1: Write your next 7 posts for the entire week
Day 2: Publish the 1st post → 3-4 per week is ok
Day 3: Start commenting on 20+ creators daily
Day 4: Create an in-depth post (3k characters)
Day 5: Create a shorter post > 300 characters
Day 6: Understand top formats → repurpose 
Day 7: Collaborate with an expert (1x week)

Pro: Needs to scale revenue (12+ months)

Day 1: Spend the entire day crafting an offer funnel
Day 2: Publish a post that shifts limiting beliefs 
Day 3: Publish a post describing your offer
Day 4: Publish a process breakdown post
Day 5: Publish a "hard sell" type of post
Day 6: Publish a "personal story"post
Day 7: Spend the entire day DM-ing

Whatever stage you're at, remember:

1. Content drives visibility and shifts mindsets
2. Comments can deepen any relationship
3. DMs turn relationships into deals
   
---

Who knows, maybe you can even mix-and-match these 3 approaches for your situation.

There is no one-size-fits-all LinkedIn strategy.

(This is just what I'd recommend at each stage)

But there are proven steps you can't miss! All here.

Hopefully, this helps. (Share it further ♻️)

P.S. Which category are you? 1, 2, 3?

outliers.hook: We did the same job. But I earned 5x more.
outliers.text:
We did the same job. But I earned 5x more.

How can you tell me this is fair?

10 years ago, I traveled 2,600 miles away from home to work as a teacher at an international private school. My first few months, before I received a promotion, I was sharing the office with other teachers. All foreigners.

It's where I met Waleed, a fellow English teacher.

I learned that his salary was 5x lower than mine.
Even though we did the saaame exact job.
1 reason: I was European. He wasn't.

His literal words:

"You're from Europe, bro. It's okay. That's why your paycheck is bigger than mine. No problem with that."

No. This IS a problem!

Because even 10 years later...

I see this happening all over LinkedIn right now. Folks are being "manipulated" into lower rates only because of their location. Or name. Or non-native accent.

But I also know the solution:

Personal branding. 

(Yes, I know we've overused this word into oblivion)

A "strong" personal brand allows you to:

1. Dictate your own price
2. Attract, not ask → all the time
3. Charge based on value, not location

In Link Up, my community members ask me for pricing guidance a whole lot - and I never allow them to underprice themselves. Ask any single one.

Pooja - received a 7-figure offer in her first month.
Dean - signed a deal with Formula 1 his first week.
Sally - 3 qualified leads after her very first post.

You're underpriced? Cool.

But don't "accept" it → Don't settle for "less".

To my brother Waleed, I really with I could've helped you back then. Instead, I'm guiding thousands today.

Repeat after me, please:

"My location does NOT impact my price.
My location does NOT determine my worth.
My location does NOT indicate I'm worth less."

Said it? Done? 

Thank you. Now go send some big fat invoices!

(Repost for others ❤️)

P.S. I'm doing something I've never done before. I want to mentor one of you for free. 1 repost today will get a free coaching hour with me ($1,500). Good luck!

outliers.hook: Want more customers? Here's a mega writing hack:
outliers.text:
Want more customers? Here's a mega writing hack:

Turn CTA (call to action) into CTV (call to value)

"Get started" → "Launch your new website"
"Contact" → "Get the answers you need"
"Download PDF" → "Learn 5 copy tips"

Don't tell your readers what to DO.
Instead, tell them what they GET.

CTA = Only the first step
CTV = The end result

Bonus (because why not):

CTT (call to think) → "Tired of belly flab?"
CTE (call to engage) → "I'm ready to lose 10 lbs!
CTS (call to specify) → "Your workout PDF is ready"

Is it finally time to change your CTAs? 😇

P.S. REPOST - teach your network something new ♻️

outliers.hook: Welcome back to LinkedIn in 2026.
outliers.text:
Welcome back to LinkedIn in 2026.

Here's your program for the next 300 days:

(this is not a motivational post, it's actual work)

 1. Publish 3-5 posts per week
 2. Write every day (for practice)
 3. Main goal: better profile views
 4. Daily commitment: 30-120mins 
 5. Publish 1 text-only post per week
 6. Publish 1 mid-length post per week
 7. Every day, check your profile visitors
 8. Every day, DM 1 visitor who's your ICP 
 9. Every day, comment on 10 new people
10. Every day, leave minimum 20 comments
11. Every day, try to support "small" creators
12. Every day, send 1-10 DMs looking for calls
13. Every quarter, host 1-3 LinkedIn Live events
14. Push limited-time offers only in those events
15. Try to re-optimize your profile every 3 months

All of the above with:

No automation. No AI. No shortcuts. No complaints. No "nobody has time for this". I've heard it all... ✌️

I've trained 57,000+ people how to use LinkedIn properly, whether 1:1 or in my Link Up community.

Do everything I told you = No way it doesn't work.

I'll see you on Thanksgiving when we celebrate your results together. Good luck!

- Coach J ❤️

(Repost to "push" others ♻️)

P.S. Which one (1-15) made you go "a-ha"? Let's chat.

outliers.hook: Here's my gift to anyone starting on LinkedIn in 2024.
outliers.text:
Here's my gift to anyone starting on LinkedIn in 2024.

A full content calendar for the next 365 days!

(including my secret framework for creating 100+ content ideas and hooks in 1 single day)

Save this. And repost this for others.

1. Weekly schedule 🗓️ 

3-5 posts per week.
Sunday through Thursday.

2. Posting schedule ⏰ 

Stick to a specific posting time.
This helps your initial engagement.
e.g. I post at 12:30pm CEST every time.

3. The golden rule for each post ✍️ 

1 post > 1 topic > 1 big message

If you have more than 1 message, leave it for another post. Don't overstuff your writing. Keep it clean.

Now...

At avg. 4 posts per week, this means you'll need 208 post ideas for the entire 2024? Ok, done.

This is the part where most gurus would include 208 "generic" and "general" post ideas for you to use as a template. If you want that, ask ChatGPT.

My way? It costs more. Today, you get it as a gift.

Here's my framework for creating (literally) 100+ LinkedIn post ideas in a single day. 

Ready?

4. Gift: My 1-to-9 framework 🎁  

(I charge good money to coach this to others) 

I believe every post can be written in 3 different ways, and from 3 different angles.

3 ways to write a post:

• positive statement → 'Commenting is easy to do'
• negative statement → 'Commenting well takes time'
• personal statement → 'Commenting changed my life'

So... Good + Bad + Personal Story.

Now, I also believe the easiest way to "start" writing posts is to write down your hook ideas first.

(And most people really struggle with writing hooks)

Here's your next task:

For each of the "3 ways" above, write your 1 post idea in these 3 very distinct ways.

a. As a "quick summary" → What's the post about?
b. As a "quote" → What's my customer thinking?
c. As a "platitude" → Think "T-shirt quotes"

This gives you 9 hooks (posts) from just 1 idea! 🔥 

Let's do it together? 

1. Positive → "Commenting is easy to do"
↳ Comments are LinkedIn's no. 1 growth hack
↳ "How do you comment so fast and so good?"
↳ 50+ comments a day keep the algorithm away

2. Negative → "Commenting well takes time"
↳ Commenting early is not a must (but a bonus)
↳ "I don't have all day to spend commenting on LI"
↳ You don't have to post daily. Just comment daily.

3. Personal → "Commenting changed my life"
↳ Every time you reply, someone's life changes
↳ She commented "I quit my job because of you".
↳ This comment hack gives me 100-1k followers daily 

There. 9 post ideas. In what? 30 seconds?

You can do this too → I promise it's (actually) this fast.

Write each LinkedIn post idea in 9 different ways.
↳ You'll have your 365-day calendar filled in no time!

5. Bonus 🙌  

Keep your ideas fresh throughout the year by "repurposing" your best-performing comments

(I do this min. once a week)

Write good comments → use them as posts 

Thank me later.

Good luck in 2024, LinkedIn friend!

Repost this ♻️ for others too, please. 

Appreciate you!

P.S. What's your favorite part above (1-5)?

outliers.hook: Officially reached 300,000 LinkedIn followers!
outliers.text:
Officially reached 300,000 LinkedIn followers!

Before I say "Thank you"...

Let me tell you a short story first:

A story of a man who came to LinkedIn post-divorce to share some positivity with the world. A Bosnian copywriter turned brand strategist turned coach who said "Imma share 100% of what I know here" whether I make a dollar or not. To help anyone anywhere learn the best strategies to improve their writing, build their personal brand, and grow their business. To be a voice among the voiceless. To teach softly. But lead loudly. 
 
If you "felt" this paragraph above, thank you. ❤️

You have quite literally changed my life. 
Thank you for supporting my journey here. 
Whether you've followed for months or years.

So what's next?

1. I'm going all in on community! Link Up has crossed 320 signups. And we're having an international meetup in my hometown Zenica in a few weeks. You in?

2. My tool Linkbound is already being circled by investors, after the amazing launch we've had. Wow.

3. Hired 10 people last year (literally based off their LinkedIn comments). New team members soon! The coaching business will be expanding - big time!

4. I'm officially the newest ins... [ehhh not yet]

Ask me anything you want in the comments.

Love,

Coach Jasmin 👋

P.S. 3 reposters today are getting my 1-year membership and 1 is getting free coaching. Good luck! ♻️
