outliers.hook: I just cancelled ALL our employee benefits. They were a total waste of money 🙄.
outliers.text:
Amelia Sordell reposted this

I just cancelled ALL our employee benefits. They were a total waste of money 🙄.

- Unlimited holiday. Gone.
- Unlimited learning (we pay for courses). Gone.
- Unlimited books from Amazon. Gone.

Because NO ONE was using them.

So instead, we’ve introduced: 

- 30 days paid holiday.
- £100 towards your commuting cost, monthly. 
- £45 towards your wellbeing, monthly. 
- £10 towards your home WiFi bill, monthly. 

These new benefits are actually costing me more than the ones we had before, but they’re so much better.

Because they’re what the team actually want. 

Besides, if you’re employee benefits don’t benefit your employees, what’s the point of having them?

💜

outliers.hook: I am a boss. Not a female boss.
outliers.text:
I am a boss. Not a female boss. 

I am an entrepreneur. Not a female entrepreneur. 

I am a business owner. Not a woman in business. 

The sooner we remove gender from these titles the better. 

Maybe we should start calling people 'Boy Bosses' and 'Male Entrepreneurs' to show how stupid it sounds.

outliers.hook: STOP calling your employees a “family”.
outliers.text:
STOP calling your employees a “family”.

Because you’re not a family.

- Families don’t have to let people go.
- Families don’t monitor each other’s performance.
- Families don’t get paid for their contribution. 
- Or leave because they’ve haven’t received remuneration or acknowledgment for that contribution.

You’re not a family. You’re a team.

A team that is conditional on EVERYONE pulling their weight.

And if your employer calling your team a “family” you’re probably unqualified to lead one. 

✌️💜

outliers.hook: Today, Marcus Rashford was honoured with an MBE.
outliers.text:
Today, Marcus Rashford was honoured with an MBE.

He lobbied tirelessly over lockdown to ensure that no child went hungry and you only have to look at the difference in food packages vulnerable children are receiving now vs. then to see how profound and long lasting his work has been. 

And in true Rashford style, he’s said he’ll be giving his MBE to his mum for the work she does. 

Who by the way, worked full time on minimum wage to raise he and his 4 siblings. 

What a guy. 

What a woman.

👏

Start NOW:

Your LinkedIn URL
Your Name

outliers.hook: I've stopped sending my team messages after hours.
outliers.text:
I've stopped sending my team messages after hours. 

Instead, I schedule emails and Slack messages to send at 9 am. It means I can brain dump whilst it's fresh in my head, and their evening remains undisrupted. 

Founders - Just because you're still working at 8pm, doesn't mean your team should be too. 

💜

outliers.text:
If you are expecting a LIVE from me, I am sorry. 

We f*cked up.

Actually, we didn’t - LinkedIn did. 

And I’m super disappointed. 

I LOVE doing these LIVES and when things don’t go to plan, it kind of sucks.

It’s something we’ve created for our clients but we want to share with you. 

If you would like a copy, please comment “Yes please!” In the comments and I will make sure you get one. 

Amelia 💜

outliers.hook: The “Huge Shortage” is because:
outliers.text:
The “Huge Shortage” is because:

1) Employers expect to hire ONE person with ALL the digital skills.... and pay them a competitive salary of £20k a year.

2) Employers are uninterested in training people in the skills the above might be missing.

There’s not a shortage of talent.

There’s a shortage of decent Employers.

outliers.hook: I’m getting divorced.
outliers.text:
I’m getting divorced. 

And whenever I tell people their immediate reaction is: “I’m sorry.” 

But I’m not sorry.

I’m not sorry I met an awesome guy and had 2 awesome kids.

I’m not sorry for memories, the crazy challenges and the incredible lessons I’ve learned along the way. 

I’m not sorry I got to spend 10 years living one life and now I get the opportunity to try living a new one. 

Society tells me my marriage was a failure.

But I don’t think it was. 

It didn’t last forever but that doesn’t mean it wasn’t awesome. 

And besides, I don’t know about you but I want to live my life for the plot, not for the ending. 

💜

outliers.hook: I dissolved my first business at 24.
outliers.text:
I dissolved my first business at 24.

I sent the wrong email to 15,000 people at 28.

I botched one of the biggest pitches of my career at 30. 

I forgot to tell finance to invoice 2 clients for 6 months, leaving a £50,000 hole in my revenue last year. 

I didn’t properly invest in operations until now, and it’s probably cost me £100,000 in business. 

I’ve missed deadlines. Forgot to call important people back. Send out work I wasn’t proud of. Picked up sales too late. Said the wrong thing. Befriended the wrong people. Spent £20,000 a month on an office we didn’t need. 

I have f*cked up time and time again. And I’m not talking small f*ck ups. I am talking huge, career altering, life changing, maybe this is the end f*ck ups.

But they’re not the end, are they? 

There is not a single thing I have said, done, been or become that I haven’t come back from. 

We all run and hide from our failures - there are plenty of people on this platform who would love to portray the picture of perfection, where every customer is happy and every team member thinks the sun shines out of their butt, but the reality is we all mess up. 

It’s what makes us human. 

And not every loss IS a failure. And not every success is a win. Really and truly you can’t win or lose. You win or you learn.

So, cheers to your f*ck ups. 

Besides, if you’re not messing up at least some of the time, you’re probably not trying hard enough… 😉


💜

outliers.hook: If I lost all of my followers, my business & my income tomorrow, here's what I'd do.
outliers.text:
If I lost all of my followers, my business & my income tomorrow, here's what I'd do. 

Day 0 - 30:

1. I would start a LinkedIn account, position my profile like a landing page and immediately turn on a Dripify sequence to start strategically adding my ideal customer to my network at scale. 

2. I would post 4x a week with a clear call to action to sign up to my newsletter.

3. I would start actively DM’ing ideal clients based on who engages with my posts. Example: “Hey [Name], saw you liked my post on [topic]. Curious—what’s your biggest challenge with [related issue]? No pitch, just want to help.”

3. I'd create a newsletter. I would use ActiveCampaign to automate nurturing and begin running opt-in campaigns to segment people into categories of "intent" to buy. 

4. I'd then open an Instagram account. I'd use my best performing "how to" LinkedIn posts and turn them into flipbooks, I'd commit to recording 2x reels per week (maybe video versions of my personal post and reactive post on LinkedIn). 

I'd embed value-driven lead magnets: free guides, audits, templates then set up Manychat to auto-reply to my DMs, story replies and comments I get on my posts - and drive them to sign up to my newsletter. 

5. I'd set up a TikTok because it's easier to grow there and vlog my life in 90 secs a day as an entrepreneur growing a business from scratch. I'd direct people to follow me on Instagram. 

Day 31 - 60: 

6. I'd launch a high-end, small-group cohort of business owners who want to turn their personality into their business's primary lead generator. 20x seats at £5000 for a 2-day program. 

7. I would deliver, refine, record and chop up the training. I would get testimonials from all 20 clients, and use these to build a landing page and ad creatives. 

Day 61-90. 

7. I'd turn the recorded coaching into a cohort-based program. I'd live launch the program once per month, then evergreen run the pre-recorded program. I'd charge £2000 per seat. 

8. I would then upsell attendees into a £97 accountability live group coaching group to continue the momentum, maintain accountability and network with others doing the same thing as you. 

9. Finally, I'd automate the lead flow with paid aids, cold outreach and partnership agreements with nano creators with engaged followings of business owners. 

£0 - £200,000 in revenue and a (conservative) MRR of £2425 in 90 days. 

I've lost everything before. 

My businesses, my home, my car. And I got it all back... and a lot more. 

If you're starting from 0 and you're feeling overwhelmed, know this;

Execution beats strategy.

Stop making plans (and excuses), and just f*cking do it 🚀

P.S - If you want to be one of only 20 business owners joining me to turn your personal brand into your primary lead generator, say ME PLEASE and I'll personally add you to the waitlist 🕺

2. I would post 4x a week

outliers.hook: Klarna tried to sue me — or at least they threatened to.
outliers.text:
Klarna tried to sue me — or at least they threatened to.

The billion-dollar Buy Now Pay Later unicorn threatened to sue me because my tiny little startup's brand was “infringing on their trademark”.

I started my business in August 2020. The height of the pandemic. People were losing their jobs left, right, and centre, and I, being the crazy person I am, thought it was a great time to start a business.

But my idea worked.

People loved the idea of Klowt, and I landed our first two clients in week one. Fast forward to September 5th 2022.

“Just pinged you a crazy email,” my EA said on WhatsApp.

Reading the strongly worded, lawyer-written letter demanding we change our entire brand "or else," - my immediate response was laughter. 

Surely this was a joke? 

“Well, that’s my LinkedIn post sorted for tomorrow,” I replied. 

But it wasn't a joke; it was real - in big letter-headed print: 'Cease and Desist: Klarna'.

The issues they had were;

1. Their brand name starts with a K. Our brand name starts with a K.
So naturally, they demanded we stop using our logo... (spoiler alert, you can't trademark a letter).

2. Our brand colours were purple. Theirs were pink. They claimed they were, 'subjectively', the same colour.

But none of this really matters, does it? 

Because Klarna didn’t really believe that Klowt infringed on their trademark or that purple and pink were the same colour. No, this entire exercise was about a billion-dollar company throwing its weight around. (And I know they're a billion-dollar company because they told me in their letter... 😂).

It was bulls*it. I knew it. They knew it.

And yet, here I was — the founder of a startup with a tiny team and tiny resources, staring down the barrel of a potentially lengthy and expensive defence case if I tried to fight it.

Could I even afford to fight it? 

Klarna is a multi-billion dollar corporation with unlimited resources. Most founders probably would have thought it was best to concede and change their brand. 

I am, however, pretty stubborn. 

And I hate bullies. 

So, I called my own legal 'team' (everyone needs a best friend who's a lawyer), who helped me draft a response, which told Klarna to go f*ck themselves — legally speaking.

I then recorded a video, which I posted to:

- LinkedIn
- TikTok
- YouTube
- Instagram

Reiterating how much I wanted them to go f*ck themselves. It even got picked up by the media. 

And do you know what? We never heard back from them.

So now, I write this post in the corner of my home office, sitting next to — you guessed it — a framed copy of the Klarna Cease and Desist letter.

It’s like a trophy.

A little momento to remind me - and now you - that people — and huge billion-dollar companies — will always try to bring you down when you start winning. 

Never f*cking let them.

💜

outliers.hook: I dissolved my first business at 24.
outliers.text:
I dissolved my first business at 24.

Sent the wrong email to 15,000 people at 28. 

And botched a HUGE pitch just last week. 

But the thing is, not every loss is a failure.

And not every success is a win.

Really and truly you can’t win or lose. 

You win or you learn.

Agree?

outliers.hook: This is me.
outliers.text:
This is me. 

At 8.49am this morning. 

Standing in the middle of our brand new, 30 person office in the heart of Mayfair. 

When I started Klowt in 2020, I had no intention of growing this big business - I just wanted to get paid to do something I love.

Now we’re;

- A team of 11.
- Hiring 10 new people.
- Are building personal brands for some of the most interesting and innovative entrepreneurs and leaders in the world.

So whoever needs to hear this today - whatever big scary dream you have, chase it. 

And chase it, insessently and consistently. 

Because a lot can happen in 2 years… and if you start now, who knows where you might be standing in 24 months time. 

💜

outliers.hook: Hands up to anyone who has seen a company post appear in their LinkedIn feed. Yeah, me neither.
outliers.text:
Hands up to anyone who has seen a company post appear in their LinkedIn feed. Yeah, me neither. 

Because LinkedIn ranks members' (your employees') content above company updates and sponsored ads. 

Which is why you almost never see a company page post on your feed. 

So; 

- If your team aren't posting content, no one will find your company page. 
- If no one finds your company page, no one will convert it into leads.

We just helped 2x FTSE businesses get 561% more reach online by using their team's profiles as a primary distribution channel. 

Your employees' personal brands are a huge opportunity - not a threat. 

💜

outliers.hook: How many company posts do you see in your feed right now? Answer is probably none.
outliers.text:
How many company posts do you see in your feed right now? Answer is probably none. 

That is because LinkedIn ranks members' content above company updates and sponsored ads. 

This is why your company page posts get 5 likes - all from it's employees - I am looking at YOU marketing department... 👀 

But it's also why you're frustrated as hell that your company page followers aren't growing and you aren't getting any ROI from the time and effort you and your team are spending on 

You have to see LinkedIn as a funnel.

Your team's profiles are at the top, your company page is at the middle, and your website is at the bottom. 

Employee personal profile > company page > website > conversion. 

Unless your team is proactively posting content to encourage viewers to look at your company page, you're not going to drive anyone anywhere. 

Stop wasting money paying an agency to manage only your Company page, because that is what you "should" be doing - and focus your efforts where you're actually going to make a difference. 

Your people. 

- Centralise a social media plan where people can grab marketing-approved content and post it from their own pages. 
- Create a vlog that everyone has to share. 
- Film short for micro video content that makes your team the stars, and, therefore, more likely to distribute it. 

There are a million ways you can get in front of your ideal audience on LinkedIn but relying exclusively on your company page is not one of them. 

Was this helpful? 

💜

outliers.hook: I got engaged on Friday night. To the love of my life.
outliers.text:
I got engaged on Friday night.
To the love of my life.
It was perfect.

For a long time, I was single. By choice.

I had a long and unpleasant divorce. Went on dates with really unpleasant people. 

It felt like this was my “lot”. 

But I didn’t want to settle.
I wanted someone who shared my values.
Who supported my dreams.
Who loved me not despite my flaws, but because of them—and never ever weaponised them against me.

So, I built a whole life on my own.
Solo holidays.
Solo wins.
Solo growth.
I was happy. Whole. Fulfilled.
And then—I met Nick Dixon.

We’re all sold this lie that your “better half” completes you. That in order to be whole you need to be fixed by someone else. 

But you’re not broken.
You’re not missing anything. 

So, stop waiting for “the one” and build your life anyway. 
Do the things you’ve always wanted to do. 
Go to the places you’ve always wanted to go. Become the person you’ve always wanted to be. 

Fall in love with yourself. 

Because fairy tale endings exist.
But only when you stop chasing them—and start choosing you.

And once you do, you’ll meet the right person - and they won’t complete your life.

They add to the one you already built for yourself. 

Now excuse me while I resume my non-stop happy tears crying 🥹💜

Do so wisely.

outliers.hook: I was recently contacted as an ex-employee’s reference.
outliers.text:
I was recently contacted as an ex-employee’s reference.

Their new employer asked me to state their salary. 

I declined. 

Because the salary you’ve earned before does not define the value you can add now. 

And the sooner employers realise that the better off we’ll all be.

Agree?

outliers.hook: STOP HIRING CANDIDATES TO FIT YOUR CULTURE!
outliers.text:
STOP HIRING CANDIDATES TO FIT YOUR CULTURE!

Hiring for culture fit is a fast-track pass to a place were innovation DIES.

If you only hire likeminded people, you’re only going to get similar results. Every. Single. Time.

Culture fit... 
 - REINFORCES A LACK OF DIVERSITY
 - ENSURES YOU MISS OUT ON GREAT TALENT
 - INCREASES RISK OF PASSING UNINTENDED BIAS INTO PRODUCTS

Culture add...
 - PAVES THE WAY FOR ENGAGING WITH DIVERSE TALENT POOLS
 - ENCOURAGES INNOVATION 
 - INSPIRES YOUR WORKFORCE TO LEARN, GROWN AND BE ABLE TO ADAPT 

Culture add is already a principle in place with industry leaders and innovators like Facebook, HubSpot, The Muse and Pandora... and you better believe these companies are leveraging this practice to improve their hiring efforts and increase their revenue. 

So, REDEFINE the way you hire candidates, overcome unconscious bias and start thinking about hiring people that CHALLENGE your culture, not conform to it - Check out my blog from 2019 (link in comments) and start hiring NOT for the culture you have but for the culture you ASPIRE to have.

 #recruitment #recruitmentadvice #talentacquisition #culturalfit #culturaldiversity #employerbrand

outliers.hook: I spend nearly £300 a month on Starbucks for my team - even though we have a coffee machine in the office.
outliers.text:
I spend nearly £300 a month on Starbucks for my team - even though we have a coffee machine in the office.

I traipse across Covent Garden in 6 inch heels every Monday, with my Head of Ops in tow to collect everyone’s mocha-choca-skinny-latte because;

1. We ALL work better with coffee.
2. It shows them - not just tells them - how appreciative I am of their work and contribution. 

79% of employees who quit their jobs cite a lack of appreciation as a major reason for leaving. So if investing in;

1. Confidence coaching.
2. Therapy.
3. High performance work.
4. Commuting expenses.
5. Home Wi-Fi costs. 
6. Monthly contributions to people’s well-being.
7. And £300 a month in coffee….

… makes them feel appreciated, you best bet I will continue to do it. 

💜

outliers.hook: Let me explain to you why Steven Bartlett has 2.5 million followers on LinkedIn and his company has less that 27,000👇
outliers.text:
Let me explain to you why Steven Bartlett has 2.5 million followers on LinkedIn and his company has less that 27,000👇

Brand messages are reshared 24x more frequently when shared via an employee or founders social media vs. the company’s social channels.

People buy from people - which is why these individuals have so many more followers than their company brands.

But it’s not just about likes and followers - it’s about serious ROI. 

Leads that come through an individual’s social media convert 7x more frequently than leads generated via any other channel. 

The entire reason why Steven Bartlett started building his personal brand was because he realised the majority of Social Chain’s business was coming off the back speaking gigs and interactions people had with him as an individual. 

Personal branding isn’t an ego driven activity - it’s about giving your company a competitive edge. 

Take it from someone who’s built a 100% inbound business off the back of hers ✌️💜

Do you post content on LinkedIn? 👇

outliers.hook: I turn 35 years old this week. Here are 35 things I wish I knew earlier.
outliers.text:
I turn 35 years old this week. Here are 35 things I wish I knew earlier. 

1. No one cares as much as you think they do.
2. Coffee is worth every penny. Good coffee even more.
3. Buying good coffee is not going to impact your ability to buy a house. 
4. Stop chasing people who don't choose you.
5. Networking isn't handing out business cards - it's making friends.
6. Ask stupid questions. Smart people do it all the time.
7. Almost everything good for you is embarrassing to start with.
8. Nobody remembers your embarrassing moments. 
9. Embarrassing moments are the price of entry of success.
10. Don't save your best outfits - wear the sequins. 
11. Taking a break is productive. 
12. The people you hang out with define your future.
13. Learn how to sell. Because sales is everything. 
14. Other people's opinions don't pay your bills.
15. Your gut knows the right decision before your brain does.
16. Speak less, but make it count.
17. Never ask permission to be yourself.
18. Writing down your thoughts is like therapy without the price tag.
19. Sarcasm doesn't always land. Who knew?
20. You don’t owe an explanation for changing your mind.
21. No one regrets being kind. 
22. You don't need to apologise for saying "No". 
23. The only shortcut to success is consistency. 
24. Feeling scared of trying something new isn't imposter syndrome, it's your body's way of telling you, you care. 
25. Read more biographies. It's cheaper to learn from other people's mistakes than your own!
26. The right partner makes you more of yourself, not less.
27. Every expert started with no f*cking idea what they were doing.
28. Money solves money problems, not emotional ones.
29. Learn to enjoy your own company.
30. Confidence is built by doing, not thinking.
31. Done beats perfect every damn time.
32. If it's uncomfortable, you're probably onto something good.
33. Don't postpone enjoying your life until retirement.
34. Not everyone has good opinions - don't trust them blindly. 
35. Celebrate yourself - no one else will EVER know what it took.

Happy birthday to me. 

And happy birthday to YOU - whenever your birthday is. 
You're amazing. 
You deserve to be celebrated. 
And you deserve EVERYTHING you want and more. 

Amelia x

outliers.hook: Until it’s my turn, I'll keep on clapping for others.
outliers.text:
Amelia Sordell reposted this

Until it’s my turn, I'll keep on clapping for others. 

Amelia and I have supported each other from day 1. 

If she's winning, it means that there's demand. 
If I'm winning, it means that there's opportunity.

If we're both winning it means there's community.

We need to stop seeing other's success as a threat.

And see it as a reason to keep pushing. 

Reminder to everyone on LinkedIn: applauding someone else's wins won't take away from yours, ever.

Repost ♻️ if you want more friends and less competition.

PS: tag your biggest cheerleader below... I'll tag mine!

outliers.hook: I was fired from my first job at 16 for clocking off early. That was so dumb.
outliers.text:
I was fired from my first job at 16 for clocking off early. That was so dumb. 

I once sent the wrong email out to 15,000 people as a marketing manager. 

I had 1000 leaflets printed with a mistake on them and we had to go through and manually write over that mistake on every single one (our department couldn’t afford a reprint). 

My first business went bust because of some bad business decisions I personally made. That was a rough one. 

Last year, I forgot - yes forgot - to send 2 clients invoices for 6 months totalling £50,000. I was basically the everything person and because we had no project management tool at the time, it got missed. 

I remember going for a huge retained job as a recruiter and when I went to give my presentation, my engagement video came up. I tried to laugh it off but the person I was presenting it to, didn’t think it was funny… It was absolutely mortifying.

In the same presentation mentioned above I wrote “relationsh*t” instead of “relationship” - the same above unimpressed presentee, pointed it out. 

I went for a huge pitch when I first started Klowt with a FTSE250 and went completely unprepared. I was told it was a coffee - it was a presentation to the board. I felt ambushed, but in hindsight I should have always had a presentation on hand for that type of situation. 

I didn’t put enough money aside to cover my tax bill last year because I didn’t realise I also had to make an upfront payment. This bit me in the arse, hard. Don’t make that mistake - always put aside more than you need! 

Why am I sharing this with you? 

Well, I am well aware that some of you might look at my perfectly polished profile, well-thought out copy (😉) and think I have my sh*t together.

I don’t. 

And that’s the polarity of social media.

You have people screaming “be authentic!” whilst simultaneously only sharing the top 10% of their life. 

I’ve done well over the last few years, and I’ve been unbelievably lucky to have you cheering me on. But if I’m lucky enough to have this platform, I have to also be honest on it.

The reason so many of us struggle with confidence and self esteem is we compare our day to day with someone else’s highlight reel. 

Stop doing that. 

Just because someone doesn’t share the f*ck ups, doesn’t mean there haven’t been any.

And hopefully by me sharing mine, it might give you the confidence to see that yours aren’t that big of a deal after all. 

💜

outliers.hook: I message 10 strangers on LinkedIn each month and ask them for a coffee.
outliers.text:
I message 10 strangers on LinkedIn each month and ask them for a coffee. 

- 2 completely ghost me. 
- 2 of them think I'm selling something and curtly reply "no". 
- 1 can't get their sh*t together, and we never meet. 
- 5 reply "would love to". 

Those random coffees have resulted in:

- Friendships.
- Partnerships.
- Client referrals.
- Paid speaking opportunities.
- Podcast invites and mentorships (on both sides). 
- Investment opportunities. 

Make a habit of reaching out to people when you don’t need something.

It used to be what you know.
Then it was who you knew.
Now it’s who knows YOU.

And it's very hard for people to get to know you if you're hiding behind your LinkedIn feed.... agree?

💜

PS - If this feels a bit out of your comfort zone, hundreds of founders and freelancers are learning how to build a personal brand that delivers them these kinds of opportunities without being scary! Join them klowt.com/membership/

outliers.hook: On the left was me in August 2020. Sitting in a tiny corner of my bedroom having just started my own business.
outliers.text:
On the left was me in August 2020. Sitting in a tiny corner of my bedroom having just started my own business. 

I had no capital to start. I couldn't afford a workspace - or even to buy a proper office chair so I used one from my kitchen. 

I was scared. Unconfident. Lacking almost all the knowledge that is required to start and run a business. But I did it anyway. 

On the right was the day we moved into our 1st proper office in Central London in May 2022. I have a team now. We're hiring 3 more as we speak. 

Have faith in yourself. Embrace the unknown and let life surprise you.

Because a lot can happen in 23 months. 

Keep chipping 💜

outliers.hook: Your average life expectancy is 80 years old - 29,000 days.
outliers.text:
Your average life expectancy is 80 years old - 29,000 days.

In adulthood, on average you'll spend:

- 248 days looking for a car parking space.
- 5 weeks arguing with people.
- 12 years at work - and 15 months of that will be above your contracted hours.
- 2 years of their life in meetings.
- 1.5 years commuting.
- 368 days in the pub.
- 2 whole years hungover.
- You'll cry 315 times.
- Spend 8 months laughing.
- And 8 years and 10 months of our lives watching TV.

Facebook users will spend more than 3 years of their life scrolling through people's life updates instead of living it.

Where you spend your time, and your energy is the SINGLE biggest contributor to the quality of your life.

Choose where you spend it wisely.

💜

outliers.hook: The concept of “hard work” upsets people - Maybe because it highlights their unwillingness to actually work hard.
outliers.text:
The concept of “hard work” upsets people - Maybe because it highlights their unwillingness to actually work hard. 

And that’s ok - you don’t NEED to work hard to be happy with your life. You need to do what’s right for you. 

But if you’re someone who is motivated to achieve something, stop buying into this “work smart” and “have balance” nonsense. 

Of course, apply your effort to the things that will actually get you somewhere - don’t be a busy fool. 

Of course rest is an important part of high performance. Enjoy your life - it’s why we do what we do right?

But effort IS required. 

And anyone that tells you otherwise is either lying or trying to sell you a course. 

💜✌️

outliers.hook: Shamelessly promote your business until you're rich.
outliers.text:
Shamelessly promote your business until you're rich.	
Tell your friends. 
Tell strangers. 
Talk about it online.
Talk about it off. 
Talk about it to anyone who’ll listen - and probably a few who won’t.

Wear t-shirts with your logo on. Create a background with your logo for Zoom meetings. Carry a bag with your business details on it everywhere. 

Shamelessly promote your business until you get every customer, client, supplier - hit every milestone, every target. 

And then when you do that - hire people, and pay them to shamelessly promote your business, too. 

No one is going to know your business exists unless you tell them about it.

And if you - the founder, are not willing to promote it, who the hell is? 

If you’re scared of being cringe or “too much”? That’s fine.
Be cringe. Be too much.

But also be paid.

Promote your f*cking business. 

And keep promoting it until you don’t have to explain what it is anymore - because all those customers you got do the promoting for you. 

💜

outliers.hook: I posted this video on TikTok. It got 1.5 million views.
outliers.text:
I posted this video on TikTok. It got 1.5 million views. 

It also got thousands of comments telling me I;

- Looked like a man (lol).
- Was a 3/10 ugly.
- Was stupid.
- Had too much plastic surgery.

It was also promptly dueted by dozens of car social media accounts who called me “a b*tch”. 

Charming.

Ironically, the post wasn’t even about a car... and here lies the problem with social media.

- Everyone is outraged.
- Everyone is angry.
- Everyone is toxic.
- Everyone is offended and upset by absolutely everything and anything that they don’t deem to be suitable... for THEM.

There is a trend of people outing these trolls, being nasty back to them, or trying to embarrass them.

Do you know what that is called? Bullying.

Now, I am not condoning bad behaviour, but let’s be objective here. Broken people want to break people.

And when you refuse to bite back, you take away their power.

SO, here is how I deal with negative comments; Engage in some light-hearted, empathetic counter-replies to diffuse the situation. I personally say things like;

- I hope you’re ok. 💜 
- Sending you the biggest hug, my friend. Sounds like you need it.
- Omg you think my natural face is full of filler?! THANKS. 🥰
- There are so many amazing hobbies that could be a better use of your time than commenting on my post. What about Pilates? Decoupage? Cross stitch? You look like a cross-stitch person. ☺️

You cannot control other people’s actions, but you can control your response to them. And the nicer your response, generally speaking, the happier YOUR life will be.

Reacting to negativity with negativity is the quickest way to lose.

We’re all capable of being the bigger person, so respond with kindness. Because in my experience, it always pays the highest ROI.

It’s also quite fun coming up with responses to “u r a man” that are both cheeky and empathetic... I can see the steam blowing out of the troll's ears from here. 👀😉

💜

outliers.hook: At 18, I wanted: - The millions
outliers.text:
At 18, I wanted:
- The millions
- The mansions
- The first-class flights

At 34, I have:
- A mortgage
- 2 feral kids
- And a "normal" life

I used to hustle my f*cking ass off because I wanted to be rich.

And at 34, I AM rich. Just not in the way I thought I would be. 

I’m rich because I can pick my kids up from school at 3:30pm on a Friday. And get to spend Sunday nights with them playing games. 

I'm rich because I get paid doing something I love and every single day I get to work on a business that I believe in, wholeheartedly. 

I'm rich because I travel when I want. It might not be first class, but the memories I've created over the last few years will carry me through until I'm old. 

It's taken me 16 years to realise, there's a HUGE difference between being rich and having a rich life.

And the quality of your life usually depends on what one of those two you're chasing. 

Yours Sincerely, 

A very happy, and fulfilled non-millionaire. 

 💜

P.S. - A huge part of living a rich life is dependent on financial freedom. I'm not a millionaire, but I do have that -  I talk a lot about how in my free newsletter https://lnkd.in/ej9Np4Zt.

outliers.hook: It's f*cking lazy of Hiring Managers to dismiss job-hoppers.
outliers.text:
It's f*cking lazy of Hiring Managers to dismiss job-hoppers. 

Job-hoppers are quitters. Right? 

Or:

 - Do they move on quickly when something is not right?
- Are they risk takers? 
- Are they considered enough to shop around before making a commitment?
- Are they innovators, and therefore get bored easily?

I'm very pro-squiggly career. 

Check out my own LinkedIn profile career timeline for evidence of that. 

Why? 

In my experience, with each new role a job-hopper has, they obtain the skills they need to succeed in their next one. 

Now, you can, of course, be an unqualified job-hopper. But not all job-hoppers are unqualified.

Not all Hiring Managers are qualified... 

But job-hopping doesn't mean a candidate is unqualified. It just means they've had a more diverse experience in getting that qualification under their belt. 

So, don’t dismiss people because they’ve moved jobs. 

You might miss out on some of the best talent in your business. 

What do you think? 

💜

outliers.hook: I don't know why we hate to see people win.
outliers.text:
I don't know why we hate to see people win.

I got a new car last year. My dream car, actually. 

It was second-hand, low mileage. It took quite some time to find... But it was my DREAM car. 

And I'd worked my butt off for 3 YEARS to be able to buy it. 

And the day that I bought it, all I wanted to do was scream from the rooftops how proud I was of myself. 

I'd just come through a messy (and expensive) divorce, bootstrapped my business. Managed to somehow successfully play my most important role of 'mum' and thrive throughout the chaos - I was chuffed. 

But I didn't tell anyone. 

Because I was so worried about people thinking I was fickle. Or being told I was "bragging" or not humble enough. 

Well, to quote my good friend Stef Sword Williams, f*ck being humble. 

Humility has its place - but not when it's dimming the light of the things you're proud of. 

Can we make it cool to celebrate our success again?

Cool to speak candidly about the struggle we had to get to where we are, and how sure, we have so much more to learn but my gosh we're proud of how far we've come. 

Cool to aim for the stars and tell the world when we reach them. 

The world feels like such a dark place right now. It can feel like everything is going to sh*t. 

Celebrating each other drowns that out. 

So let's win LOUDLY. 

Because when we are loud, we show other people what’s possible.

And possibility is what gives people hope. Hope drives change.

And in my opinion, that’s exactly what the world needs more of right now. 

What do you think?

💜

P.S - If you can relate, join me in today's newsletter: https://lnkd.in/ejDbD94R 

And shout out to Forward Group for finding me the car. I still look at it and smile every single time I get in it 🥹

outliers.hook: 17 rules for a life well lived:
outliers.text:
17 rules for a life well lived:

1. Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2. Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3. Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4. Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5. Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6. Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7. Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8. Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9. Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10. Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11. Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12. Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13. Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14. Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15. Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16. Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17. Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

outliers.hook: Last week, I spent 7 hours creating 5 AI prompts to help ghostwriters edit their LinkedIn content:
outliers.text:
Last week, I spent 7 hours creating 5 AI prompts to help ghostwriters edit their LinkedIn content:

• Capture a client's voice
• Simplify wordy writing
• Turn long articles into punchy LinkedIn posts

All in a matter of seconds.

The best part?

You don't need to be an AI or writing expert to use these prompts.

I originally built these for my Premium Ghostwriting Academy students to help them edit their clients' content.

But the output was so good, I had to get them into the hands of more writers.

So, I'm giving away my entire Ghostwriter Editing AI Library for free.

(These are the exact frameworks I used to write for 300+ industry leaders and generate over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter—now downloaded into AI prompts.)

These 5 prompts will help you:

• Add flow between disconnected ideas
• Instantly clean up wordy, unclear writing
• Analyze and match your client's unique voice "mix"
• Convert long-form content into engaging LinkedIn posts

And more.

Want access?

✅ Like this post
✅ Comment "edit"
✅ Send me a connection request (if we're not already connected)

outliers.hook: 7 signs your job is about to be replaced by ChatGPT-4:
outliers.text:
7 signs your job is about to be replaced by ChatGPT-4:

~

1) You get paid per hour (for digital work)

"Hourly" is a signal you are getting paid for your time & effort.

Not your value.

The age of AI is going to (painfully) reveal how many people have been getting paid to "sit there" vs getting paid to generate an outcome.

~

2) You are a "digital laborer."

These are the new blue-collar jobs:

• SEO writers
• Website optimizers
• Back-end developers
• Landing page builders
• Google AdWords optimizers
• Etc.

If you currently get paid to build "piping" for the Internet, bye bye.

~

3) Your task list = "doing," not "thinking."

All the highest-paid people, in any industry, don't get paid to "do."

They get paid to "think"—and then other human beings "do the doing."

So if AI replaces a lot of the "doing..."

Then you need to stop "doing" & start "thinking."

~

4) Your work requires zero creative decision-making.

• Taking notes on calls
• Writing company press releases
• Reporting "the facts" for a news publication
• Etc.

All these jobs are just different versions of manual data retrieval & synopsis.

Going to be replaced by AI.

~

5) Your creative decision-making is arbitrary.

Ever sit in a meeting where 8 people debate whether the logo should be dark blue or light blue?

0 value. (No unique frameworks here.)

AI replaces baseline subjectivity because it can generate infinite options to choose from.

~

6) You get paid to create commodity projects.

• Decks
• Proposals
• Holiday emails
• Website mockups
• Etc.

Even before AI, all these "digital assets" were being turned into beautiful, no-design-required templates.

With AI? Game over.

1-Click. Generate. Download.

~

7) Your content/POV is easily substitutable. 

It doesn't matter if you think you're "talented."

If you write, design, build, or create things people can easily find elsewhere... your economic value is lower than you think.

Being King of your Category should be the goal.

~

10 years ago, everyone thought the physical laborers (plumbers, electricians, etc.) would be first to get "automated away."

Turns out, that's wrong.

It's the digital "do-ers" that will be first to go.

~

Thriving in The Age of AI is going to be all about leverage.

Either compete with humans & technology over the "doing."

Or ascend, create new & unique frameworks, and get paid to "think" (and let AI "do the doing").

outliers.hook: When I graduated from college, I moved into a studio apartment.
outliers.text:
When I graduated from college, I moved into a studio apartment.

No AC. Heater from 1970s. 

Making minimum wage.

8 years later, I just moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here's what I've learned about the journey of "success."

-

Lesson 1: Your habits in your tiny apartment define your habits in your dream house.

I wrote every day in that studio apt.

Even in the summer. 100 degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers every 40 min to cool off.

Only difference today = AC.

-

Lesson 2: All you really need is a bed and a desk.

My first 3 years out of college, I slept on a $79 air mattress from Target.

My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

When I look at my belongings today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed and my desk.

All you need.

-

Lesson 3: Discipline is the leading indicator of success

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of Internet for 4 years to write my first book.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

-

Lesson 4: Money doesn't fix everything

I was very insecure about my apt.

I was ashamed at how poor I was.

I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities weren't "my apartment's." They were my own.

And money wouldn't fix them.

-

Lesson 5: If you can't do it here, you won't be able to do it there.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll start working on Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't yet convenient.

-

Lesson 6: The more you achieve, the harder it is to stay disciplined

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 8 years ago.

I own a TV now. I have disposable income. I can travel.

In my 20s, I didn't even have those options.

Not-having can be a gift too.

-

Lesson 7: "Wanting" is more fun than "Having"

2 days after I moved into my dream condo, my weekly therapy session rolled around.

"Now what?" I asked him.

I'd spent almost a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

Only to get "here" and not know how to enjoy it.

-

Final story:

Two years ago, I had the opportunity to ghostwrite for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home for a work weekend—a massive ranch with a multimillion dollar home music studio. He owned every keyboard money could buy.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different today!"

He shook his head and said, "Actually, it's killing me."

We all think the journey somehow gets easier once we've achieved our dreams, or made a certain amount of $$$.

But I've learned it's the opposite.

The journey & creative process gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Focus on mastering yourself, your habits, state of mind, ability to create no matter how hot the room is, whether you have Internet or not.

Your bed, your desk, and your apartment might change.

But the work won't.

outliers.hook: 5 dead-simple steps to write a 60,000-word book in 30 days—even if you've never written a book before:
outliers.text:
5 dead-simple steps to write a 60,000-word book in 30 days—even if you've never written a book before:

For context:

It took me 4 years to write my 1st book.

And it took me 4 months to write my 2nd book.

Now, I can write a 60,000-word book in 30 days.

Here's the framework:

1. Your book's Main Title/Subtitle is 80% of the work.

• What question (of the reader's) are you answering?
• What problem are you solving?
• What solution are you unlocking?
• How are you going to get there?

Don't start writing till you can answer these 4 questions.

---
2. Make a list of all the Main Points you want to make in your book.

Again, don't start writing yet.

List out:

• The big ideas that matter most
• And decide in what order

If you can do this, with clarity, your book is 90% done.

---
3. Under each Main Point, list out all the little sub-points.

Again, don't start writing yet.

Keep listing:

• What smaller ideas do you want to include?
• What examples?
• What insights?
• What stories?

Do this, and your book is 95% done.

---
4. Now just go down your list of Main Points, section by section.

All your sub-points are your "notes" of what to write about.

This makes the writing 100x easier.

Because you literally have the words right in front of you.

"Say this here."

Now you're 99% done.

---
5. Do a final spelling/grammar read through.

Since you took the time to organize all your thoughts FIRST, by the time you "finish writing," you're done.

Give your book a once-over for spelling/grammar.

And you're 100% done.

---
Boom, that's how to write a 60,000-word book in 30 days.

Here's the 5-step framework I use:

1. Choose the Main Title & Subtitle
2. Make a list of all the Main Points
3. List out all the little Sub-Points
4. Fill in your Sub-Points
5. Do a final spelling/grammar check

---
📌 If you want an insanely lucrative side hustle where you can practice writing WHILE working on your book...

I highly encourage ghostwriting (this is what I did).

Land your first $5,000 client in 5 steps here:

https://lnkd.in/eGAyHceM

outliers.hook: This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon.
outliers.text:
This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon.

He turned 'Think Different' into $3 trillion, crafted the iMac story, and helped save Apple from bankruptcy.

When Ken Segall joined Apple's ad agency in 1997, Apple was:

• 90 days from bankruptcy
• Its brand message was a mess
• And sales were plummeting 

Jobs needed someone who could turn complex tech into simple stories.

Enter Ken Segall, the creative director who'd write the words to define Apple.

His first task? 

Create a campaign to tell the world Apple was back.

Segall's team presented several campaigns to Jobs—but one stood above the rest: 

"Think Different"

Two simple words that would become the foundation of Apple's revival. 

Segall used 5 writing rules to bring Apple back from the brink:

1. Simplicity is power. 

• Remove every unnecessary word
• Cut complex explanations
• Focus on emotional impact

The "Think Different" tagline is just 2 words, and the campaign was full of black & white photos.

2. The name tells the story.

Before Segall, Apple's computers had names like Performa 6400, PowerBook 5300, & Macintosh Quadra 800.

Segall convinced Jobs to simplify everything to "i" products:

• iMac
• iPad
• iPhone

Steve Jobs wanted to call Apple’s new computer "MacMan," even though it sounded like a toy. 

Segall pitched "iMac," with a focus on simplicity and the internet—but Jobs hated it.

Yet, after rejecting other names, Jobs quietly approved it.

Segall later joked, "Without it, we’d have PhoneMans and PadMans today."

3. Write like you talk.

Before Segall, Apple's marketing was filled with technical jargon and specs.

He transformed it into copy that was:

• Simple
• Conversational
• Easy to understand

4. Find the drama.

Every product needed a story:

• iPod: 1,000 songs in your pocket
• iPhone: A revolutionary mobile phone
• iMac: The internet computer for everyone

This would capture the customer's imagination.

5. Follow proven frameworks.

His writing framework was simple:

• Start with the problem (make it personal)
• Build emotional tension (highlight the stakes)
• Introduce the solution (make it irresistible)
• Show the transformation (paint the "after")
• End with a clear CTA (drive action)

The power of great writing transformed Apple from near bankruptcy to the world's most valuable company.

But the modern-day Segalls aren't writing for Apple. 

They're ghostwriters scaling the message of founders, CEOs, and businesses.

Because every founder & business leader needs a Ken Segall to scale their message online.

But founders are too busy building to write—creating massive demand for skilled ghostwriters.

I know because I've made $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter.

It's how I learned from industry leaders & got paid to practice my writing.

I created this free Premium Ghostwriting Blueprint to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

outliers.hook: 9 entrepreneurship clichés everyone parrots, but are actually wrong:
outliers.text:
9 entrepreneurship clichés everyone parrots, but are actually wrong:

1. "The customer is always right."

No, they're not.

You do not want to optimize your business for some rando who doesn't value your product/service/category.

The customers who ARE always right are your Superconsumers.

They spend 30-70% more.

Optimize for them.

--

2. "It's all about Product-Market Fit."

No, it's not.

The moment you decide to try to "fit" into an existing category, you are unconsciously competing for 24% of the economics.

You'll never create your own category.

And will forever be in the existing Category King's shadow.

--

3. "The best brand always wins."

No, it doesn't.

Google is one of the "best" brands in the whole world.

How come Google+ failed?

Because you can't take your BRAND and walk into someone else's category and expect it to matter.

Categories make brands.

Not the other way around.

--

4. "If you're too expensive, customers won't buy from you.

Yes, they will.

Price isn't what causes someone to buy or not buy.

Education is. 

The customer has to understand the CONTEXT of the price. 

• A photo on the Internet? Free.
• An NFT of the same photo? $758,253

--

5. "Marketing is what you do when your product sucks."

What?

It took humans 300 years to realize a better use case for the wheel was transportation, not pottery.

If the greatest product ever invented couldn't speak for itself, what makes you think your dashboard can?

--

6. "Customers care about your Founder Story."

No, they don't.

They only care if your Founder Story educates them on the transformation THEY CAN EXPECT to experience as well.

You, as the founder, are not the main character.

The customer is.

--

7. "The key to success is work ethic."

No, it's not.

Work ethic matters, but if you are banging away on a product/service/company in someone else's category (or worse, a declining category), how hard you work becomes irrelevant.

You're fighting a losing battle.

Change categories!

--

8. "The best companies are like families."

No, they're not.

The best companies are like championship-winning teams. 

• If you can't keep up, you're out.
• If you can't handle your responsibilities, you're out.
• If you can't make the whole team better, you're out.

--

9. "Just focus on shipping a Minimum Viable Product"

Please don't.

Listen to the words in any other context:

• Minimum Viable Wedding Proposal
• Minimum Viable Marriage
• Minimum Viable Children

The last thing you want is to ship customers an MVP.

Make it LEGENDARY.

--

90% of what we are taught about entrepreneurship, or what we hear surrounded by entrepreneur-speak on social media, is wrong.

It's people parroting thinking they heard from someone else.

But thinking about thinking is the most important kind of thinking.

outliers.hook: I can write a 60,000-word book in 30 days.
outliers.text:
I can write a 60,000-word book in 30 days. 

The secret?

Math (and a simple 3-step process).

First, the numbers.

Here's how to break down your 60,000-word book:

• 10 chapters at 5,000 words each
• Each chapter has 5 to 7 sub-questions
• These sections are then only 700 to 1,000 words long

Each section is the length of a blog post or a newsletter.

So your book is a series of shorter posts bolted together.

This should help take some pressure off writing a "60,000-word book." 

Instead, take it in chunks.

Then add in an introduction and conclusion, and you can easily hit 60,000 words.

Second, the process.

(This works even if you've never written a book before.)

Here are 3 steps to write a 5,000-word chapter in 90 minutes:

Before you start writing, you need to have two items in place:

1. Your title
2. Your outline

If you don't have these, you don't know what you're writing about.

Step 1: Prep the Chapter on the page

1. Write out the overarching question for the chapter
2. List out the sub-questions/topics for the question

With the chapter prepped, writing becomes a fill-in-the-blanks exercise (and far less intimidating).

Step 2: Expand each sub-question/topic

For each of these, ask yourself:

"What does the reader need to understand about the sub-question and overarching chapter question?"

The best way to think about this?

Use the 10 magical ways of expansion.

Any piece of writing can be expanded in 10 ways:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

Mix and match these to answer the sub-questions and overarching question of the chapter for the reader.

Step 3: Make a round of edits

Don't get bogged down in this.

Some editing pointers:

• Look out for repetition
• Edit for flow and readability
• Do a final read-through out loud to pick up any clunky parts

That's it—you just wrote a 5,000-word chapter in 90 minutes.

That's it!

📌 My first took me 4 years to write (before I learned this framework).

Since then, I've written 10+ books.

And I earned a lucrative living while doing it.

How?

By ghostwriting.

It's how I got paid to write, learn, and network with industry leaders.

Want to land high-paying writing clients?

Get started here: https://lnkd.in/et-ugWr4

outliers.hook: 9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:
outliers.text:
9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:

1/ No one owes you a job.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. 

That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

2/ If you don't grow together, you grow apart.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

You change.

They change.

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

3/ Safety is an illusion.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves.

Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

4/ Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

5/ Status symbols only work on people "below you."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

6/ It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

7/ The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are.

If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama.

If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people.

If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs.

8/ Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more and more developed.

Actions tell your story.

9/ The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as a game.

Others, an adventure. An experience. A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

And live it fully.

outliers.hook: The most underrated, oddly-ignored, yet highest-leverage skill in 2025:
outliers.text:
The most underrated, oddly-ignored, yet highest-leverage skill in 2025:

Lean Writing.

It's how:

• Malcolm Gladwell
• Mark Manson
• Ryan Holiday

Make millions every year.

How?

Well, do you see a pattern here?

• Mark Manson's "Subtle Art" started as a blog
• Malcolm Gladwell's "Outliers" was a New Yorker article
• Ryan Holiday's "The Obstacle Is The Way" began as a blog

They all use a framework I call Lean Writing.

Most legacy writers spend 10-20 hours on blog posts nobody reads

But Digital Writers can publish a post that gets thousands of views.

Unfortunately, most writers hate social media.

But it's the secret to Lean Writing.

The Lean Writing method has 3 simple steps:

1. Test ideas online with short-form content
2. Listen for feedback & double down on what works
3. Expand those ideas into longer assets & even businesses

The Lean Writing content ladder looks like this:

• LinkedIn Post → Atomic Essay 
• Essay → Free email course 
• Course → Paid digital product
• Product → Business

The next expansion only happens if the previous one resonates with readers.

But how do you expand your writing at each stage?

Use the 10 Magical ways to expand any piece of writing:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

So, how do you test your ideas?

Use social platforms:

• Platforms send your content to readers
• If they engage, it goes to more people
• And so on and so on

Digital writers know that algorithm hacking isn't how you test your ideas.

Instead, let the algorithm do its thing—it will always surface the best content.

Eric Jorgenson knew this when he was writing the Naval Ravikant's "Almanack".

For 3 years, he collected Naval's:

• Tweets
• Podcast clips
• Interview fragments

Naval's ideas were already validated on social media. 

All Jorgenson had to do was curate/expand them into a book.

When James Clear, Mark Manson, or Ryan Holiday share their social posts or newsletters, they aren't trying to write a masterpiece.

They are testing ideas.

When people comment on specific points, they know exactly which ones deserve more attention.

This is the exact framework I used to write social content for 300+ industry leaders (and generate millions as a ghostwriter).

A data-driven approach meant I could rack up millions of views for my clients without burning out.

I'm convinced ghostwriting is the best writing opportunity of 2025:

• Work directly with industry experts
• Master writing frameworks
• Practice your craft

All while getting paid.

It's how I went from a broke freelancer to $20K/month in 60 days.

Want to know how to land high-paying clients in 2025?

Start with my free, 5-step ghostwriting roadmap: https://lnkd.in/eBkcydRr

outliers.hook: Everyone talks about legendary writers like Hemingway, King, or Thoreau.
outliers.text:
Everyone talks about legendary writers like Hemingway, King, or Thoreau.

But there is a British author who redefined the writing game forever.

Here’s the secret technique J. K. Rowling used to write one of the best-selling book series of all time:

At 32, J.K. Rowling was a single mom on welfare, struggling to make ends meet. 

One day, while stuck on a delayed train, the character Harry Potter came to her fully formed.

And she spent the next 5 years meticulously planning the 7-book series before writing a single word.

Today, Harry Potter is the best-selling book series of all time (closely followed by Goosebumps).

Here's a breakdown of the 10-column outline method she used to create Harry Potter:

Rowling's 10-column outline was divided into 2 key areas:

1. Progress & Progression (Time & Chapters) 
2. Plot & Characters (Main Plot, Subplots, Characters)

By focusing tightly on all these interconnected elements, she kept readers engaged and the story moving forward.

Each chapter in the series features 3+ interwoven subplots:

• The central mystery (e.g. The Chamber of Secrets)
• Character development (e.g. Ron's insecurities)
• Hogwarts life (e.g. Quidditch matches)

These subplots add depth to each chapter.

In Order of the Phoenix, Rowling streamlined her story:

• 1 main plot (The Prophecy & Voldemort's return)
• 5 subplots (Umbridge, Dumbledore's Army, etc.)
• Under 10 key characters

To make sure she never missed a key plot point, Rowling did a detailed outline.

And to add more complexity, Rowling's narrative over the 7 books spans 3 generations:

1. Harry, Ron, & Hermione
2. James, Lily, & the Marauders 
3. Dumbledore & Voldemort's origins

Each layer builds on the others, creating a dense wizarding world that feels real to the reader.

Rowling's consistent structure provided a roadmap and built anticipation for the series' climactic finale.

• 1 school year at Hogwarts
• 1 central mystery to solve
• 5 subplots intertwine with the main story

That's it!

Rowling's 10-column outline can be any writer's Marauder's Map:

1. Time (When does the story take place?)
2. Chapter (How will you structure your tale?)
3. Plot (What's the main narrative throughline?)
4. Subplots (How will you add complexity?)
5. Characters (Who will drive your story?)

Every storyteller should learn from Rowling's approach:

• Craft a compelling premise
• Develop a detailed outline
• Interweave subplots to add depth
• Create layered generational stories
• Narrow the focus to intensify drama

I love studying the art of writing.

But the fastest way to monetize as a writer is to become a Premium Ghostwriter.

It's how I went from a broke freelancer to $20k/month in 60 days.

Here are the 5 steps to become a Premium Ghostwriter: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

outliers.hook: This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon.
outliers.text:
This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon.

At Apple, he:

• Crafted the iMac story
• Turned 'Think Different' into $3 trillion
• And ultimately helped save Apple from ruin

In 1997, Apple was 90 days from bankruptcy.

At the time:

• Apple's brand message was a mess
• Their sales were plummeting
• And cash was running low

Enter Ken Segall, the creative director who'd write the words to define Apple.

Segall's team presented several campaigns to Jobs.

But one stood above the rest: 

"Think Different"

These two simple words became the foundation of Apple's revival. 

Segall used 5 writing rules to bring Apple back from the brink:

1. Simplicity is velocity

• Remove every unnecessary word
• Cut complex explanations
• Focus on emotional impact

The "Think Different" tagline is just 2 words, and the campaign was full of black & white photos.

2. The name tells the story

Before Segall, Apple's computers had names like Performa 6400, PowerBook 5300, & Macintosh Quadra 800.

Segall convinced Jobs to simplify everything to "i" products:

• iMac
• iPad
• iPhone

Steve Jobs wanted to call Apple’s new computer "MacMan," even though it sounded like a toy. 

Segall pitched "iMac," with a focus on simplicity and the internet.

Jobs hated it.

Eventually, he quietly approved it (after rejecting other names).

Segall later joked, "Without it, we’d have PhoneMans and PadMans today."

3. Write like you talk

Before Segall, Apple's marketing was filled with technical jargon and specs.

He transformed it into copy that was:

• Simple
• Conversational
• Easy to understand

4. Find the drama

Every product needed a story:

• iPod: 1,000 songs in your pocket
• iPhone: A revolutionary mobile phone
• iMac: The internet computer for everyone

This would capture the customer's imagination.

5. Follow proven frameworks

His writing framework was simple:

• Start with the problem (make it personal)
• Build emotional tension (highlight the stakes)
• Introduce the solution (make it irresistible)
• Show the transformation (paint the "after")
• End with a clear CTA (drive action)

The power of great writing took Apple from near bankruptcy to the world's most valuable company.

But the modern-day Segalls aren't writing for Apple. 

They're ghostwriters scaling the message of founders, CEOs, and businesses.

Because every founder & industry leader needs a Ken Segall to scale their message online.

But founders are too busy building to write—creating massive demand for skilled ghostwriters.

I know because I've made $3,000,000+ ghostwriting for over 300 industry leaders:

• Founders
• Executives
• NYT best-selling authors

And right now, ghostwriting on LinkedIn is the easiest way to get started.

I put together a free, 5-day email course to help you land high-paying writing clients.

Get started here: https://lnkd.in/gHhF5szB

outliers.hook: 9 entrepreneurship cliches everyone parrots, but are actually wrong:
outliers.text:
9 entrepreneurship cliches everyone parrots, but are actually wrong:

1/ "The customer is always right."

No, they're not.

You do not want to optimize your biz for some rando who doesn't value your product/service/category.

The customers who ARE always right are your Superconsumers.

They buy more often, and spend 30-70% more $$$.

Optimize for them.

2/ "It's all about Product-Market Fit."

No, it's not.

The moment you decide to try to "fit" into an existing category, you are unconsciously competing for 24% of the economics.

You'll never create your own category.

And will forever be in the existing Category King's shadow.

3/ "The best brand always wins."

No, it doesn't.

Google is one of the "best" brands in the whole world.

How come Google+ failed?

Because you can't take your BRAND and walk into someone else's category and expect to matter.

Categories make brands.

Not the other way around.

4/ "If you're too expensive, customers won't buy from you."

Yes, they will.

Price isn't what causes someone to buy or not buy.

Education is. 

The customer has to understand the CONTEXT of the price. 

• A photo on the Internet? Free.
• An NFT of same photo? $758,253

5/ "Marketing is what you do when your product sucks."

What?

It took humans 300 years to realize a better use case for the wheel was transportation, not pottery.

If the greatest product ever invented couldn't speak for itself, what makes you think your dashboard can?

6/ "Customers care about your Founder Story."

No, they don't.

They only care if your Founder Story educates them on the transformation THEY CAN EXPECT to experience as well.

You, as the founder, are not the main character.

The customer is.

7/ "The key to success is work ethic."

No, it's not.

Work ethic matters, but if you are banging away on a product/service/company in someone else's category (or worse, a declining category), how hard you work is irrelevant.

You're fighting a losing battle.

Change categories!

8/ "The best companies are like families."

No, they're not.

The best companies are like championship-winning teams. 

• If you can't keep up, you're out.
• If you can't handle your responsibilities, you're out.
• If you can't make the whole team better, you're out.

9/ "Just focus on shipping a Minimum Viable Product"

Please don't.

Listen to the words in any other context:

• Minimum Viable Wedding Proposal
• Minimum Viable Marriage
• Minimum Viable Children

The last thing you want is to ship customers an MVP.

Make it LEGENDARY.

outliers.hook: How to write a book in 30 days:
outliers.text:
How to write a book in 30 days:

Disclaimer: This only works for content where you are already the expert/speaking from personal experience.

If you are researching a NEW topic/something you know nothing about, then it will take you longer.

But once you HAVE the information, you can write fast.

Here's how:

Step 1: Name The House

A great book is not a bunch of words.

A great book is an idea, presented coherently, in a way that walks the reader's thinking FROM where they currently are TO somewhere new & different.

Before you write a single word, where are you taking the reader?

Step 2: Draw The Floor Plan

A "book" is typically 30k-60k words.

60k / 12 chapters = 5,000 words per chapter.

That's a long-form blog post.

So, start by creating 12 word documents with a 5k word limit/goal.

Step 3: Title The Rooms

Inside your Floor Plan, it's helpful to know which room is which, right?

So, name your chapters:

• Big Idea 1
• Big Idea 2
• Big Idea 3
• Etc.

Think of each chapter as a different "bucket" of information.

Step 4: Fill The Rooms With Furniture

Don't worry about writing (or organizing) yet.

Let's just get the boxes inside the rooms.

This is everything you want to "say"

• Main Point 1
• Main Point 2
• Main Point 3

5,000 word chapter / 5 Main Points = 1,000 words per "Main Point."

Step 5: Organize The Rooms

Once you have all your Main Points inside each room, you now know what you want to "say," now you need to figure out how to say it.

• Which Main Point comes first?
• Which Main Point comes second?
• Which Main Point comes third?
• Etc.

Here I use a technique called Prepping The Page.

Let's assume 4 sub-points per Main Point.

1,000 words per "Main Point" = 250 words per sub-point.

That's 4 Atomic Essays per section.

Or 20 Atomic Essays per chapter.

Easy.

Step 6: Decorate The Rooms

Ah, *now* it's time to get into the writing!

• What stories can you tell to add context to each Main Point?

• What interesting stats can you include to beef up your argument of a Main Point?

• What takeaways can you include?

Step 7: Clean The Rooms

The last mile here is cleaning up:

• Sentences & Grammar
• Compressing subpoints/stories
• Removing rambling
• Deleting similar points made multiple times
• Etc.

Notice:

85% of the work in writing a book is not really the writing itself.

It's thinking.

And then organizing the thinking.

"The writing" is the last mile of stylistic edits. 

But no amount of stylistic edits can take the place of writing with poor thinking.

I have been writing books for years, and I am convinced great books can be produced in volume with the right formulas.

This is something I plan on writing about extensively over the next few years.

So please comment any questions you have!

outliers.hook: 30 things successful people tell themselves:
outliers.text:
30 things successful people tell themselves:

1. Today is a great day to build.

2. No one is responsible for my success besides me.

3. I welcome the learning process with open arms.

4. Everyone was once a beginner.

5. Mastery over my craft is the highest accolade.

6. I will not let someone else define my self worth.

7. To unlock the upside, I must face the risk of the downside.

8. I need to be patiently impatient and have a bias toward action.

9. The enemy of long-term compounding is short-term results.

10. Wealth is built over decades, not days.

11. I have no competition, because I am only competing against myself.

12. The world rewards those who do what others do not.

13. I trust myself and my convictions.

14. Every failure is an opportunity to learn.

15. Every success is an opportunity to start again.

16. Forward is the only option. There is no going back.

17. I own my shortcomings—and leverage them to my advantage.

18. Dwelling on a mistake where the lesson has already been extracted is a waste of precious time.

19. My potential knows no limits.

20. Simplicity is velocity, because the simple thing is the hard thing.

21. It's better to move slowly in the right direction than quickly in the wrong direction.

22. I do not accept any obstacle in my way.

23. "Tomorrow" is an excuse. Do it now or don't do it at all.

24. I don't know how yet—but I will figure it out.

25. What I once wanted, I currently have. (The destination isn't what matters.)

26. Hours are a poor measure of effort. Intentional practice is the only thing that matters.

27. I will not judge my entire life based on any single day.

28. Better to be singular and deep than multifaceted and shallow.

29. Real progress is made in silence.

30. Life is not a zero-sum game. We can all win.

outliers.hook: 9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:
outliers.text:
9 uncomfortable truths most people spend their entire lives avoiding:

1/ No one owes you a job.

When you graduate from high school or college, nobody owes you a paycheck. That's a lie we've been told.

Your value to a company only exists so long as that company needs you.

You are human labor leverage.

Whether you like it or not.

-

2/ If you don't grow together, you grow apart.

Friendships, relationships with your siblings & parents, life partners.

All of these require nurturing on a consistent basis.

Otherwise, life pulls you apart.

You change.

They change.

Eventually, you don't recognize each other.

-

3/ Safety is an illusion.

People spend their entire lives working jobs they hate chasing "safety."

So do entrepreneurs: "Once I make enough $$$, then I'll be safe."

These are lies we tell ourselves.

Life is full of Black Swan events.

You never know what could happen.

-

4/ Your parents are flawed individuals, just like you.

As children, we put our parents on a pedestal—and project our wants and needs onto them.

Many people spend their entire adult lives continuing to do this.

Failing to accept their parents for who they are.

-

5/ Status symbols only work on people "below you."

Nobody richer than you cares about your Rolex. 

Nobody with more championship rings than you cares about your championship ring.

Face it: status symbols only impress the people who are easy to impress.

-

6/ It's easier to be broke and guaranteed to fail than rich and at risk of losing it all.

Responsibility scares people.

So instead of accepting responsibility, most people defer it.

Better to know they are unhappy and complain vs try to fix it and fail.

-

7/ The people in your life are a "hall of mirrors" for who you are.

If you are a dramatic person, you are going to attract drama.

If you are an insecure person, you are going to attract insecure people.

If you are an egomaniac, you are going to attract egomaniacs.

-

8/ Your life story is the sum total of all your decisions.

Most people think they ARE someone in life—and they make decisions.

In reality, it's flipped:

You are no one. But with each decision you make, who you ARE becomes more and more developed.

Actions tell your story.

-

9/ The purpose of life is to give your life purpose.

Some people view life as a game.

Others, an adventure. An experience. A "waiting period between lives."

All of these are right. Or, none of them are.

Your life's purpose is yours to choose.

And live it fully.
