outliers.hook: When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.
outliers.text:
When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.

8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here are 7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":

Lesson 1: Your habits in your apartment define your habits in your dream house.

I wrote every day in that studio apartment.

Even in the summer, in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers every 40 min to cool off.

Only difference today = AC.

Lesson 2: All you really need is a bed and a desk.

For the first 3 years out of college, I slept on a $79 air mattress from Target.

My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

When I look at my belongings today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed and my desk.

All you need.

Lesson 3: Discipline is the leading indicator of success.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of Internet for 4 years to write my first book.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

Lesson 4: Money doesn't fix everything.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed of how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities weren't "my apartment's." They were my own.

And money wouldn't fix them.

Lesson 5: If you can't do it here, you won't be able to do it there.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll start working on Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't yet convenient.

Lesson 6: The more you achieve, the harder it is to stay disciplined.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago.

I own a TV now. I have disposable income. I can travel.

In my 20s, I didn't even have those options.

Not-having can be a gift too.

Lesson 7: "Wanting" is more fun than "Having".

Two days after I moved into my dream condo, my weekly therapy session rolled around.

"Now what?" I asked him.

I'd spent almost a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

Only to get "here" and not know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I had the opportunity to ghostwrite for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home for a work weekend: a massive ranch with a multimillion-dollar home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different today. You have so many options!"

He shook his head and said, "Actually, it's killing me."

He would rent a studio apartment in the city to make his next album.

We all think the journey somehow gets easier once we've achieved our dreams, or made a certain amount of money.

From my own experience, and what I've learned ghostwriting for so many successful people, it's actually the opposite.

The journey & creative process gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Your bed, your desk, and your apartment might change.

But the work won't.

outliers.text:
Over the past 5 years, I have ghostwritten more than 4,000 articles for startup founders, C-level executives, Silicon Valley investors, and more.

Want to know a secret?

I use the same 5 templates every time.

Template 1: The mistakes & the lessons

• Decision
• Outcome
• Mistake
• Lesson

Template 2: The frameworks

• Name the problem
• Pinpoint actionable steps
• Celebrate outcome

Template 3: The future—here's why

• Claim where your industry is headed
• Explain why
• Cite 2-3 studies/stats for credibility

Template 4: Category POV

• Share what's wrong with the old category
• Name & Claim new category
• Describe the outcome after moving from old to new

Template 5: Myths

• Say what most people think, but get wrong
• Say what does the world NEEDS to know (the truth)
• Explain what this knowledge unlocks

I have been ghostwriting for business founders, executives, and investors for years.

And I've accumulated tens of millions of views doing so (under other people's names).

Chances are, you've read something I've written online—you just didn't know it.

These are my secret frameworks.

outliers.hook: I journal every single morning for at least 30 minutes.
outliers.text:
I journal every single morning for at least 30 minutes.

I've been doing this since 2010.                                                          

But over the years, I've learned you can (very easily) overcomplicate the act of journaling.

Which is why now I only use 4 prompts:

--

Question 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

--

Question 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

--

Question 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

What's around the bend?

What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

--

Question 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

--

I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused.

I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them.

Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 5 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

outliers.hook: Most life advice sucks.
outliers.text:
Most life advice sucks.

Platitudes don’t change your life.

Here’s the stuff that will.

17 rules for a life well lived:

1/ Make decisions from a place of abundance, not scarcity.

Your best ideas are ahead of you, not behind you.

2/ Avoid the quick and easy paths.

Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

3/ Don't be fooled by your own achievements.

The moment you begin to lean on what you historically achieved, the potential for future achievements goes down.

4/ Aim to master the process, not celebrate the rewards or outcomes.

The only timeless reward is loving what you do, and improving at what you do, every single day.

"By the time you can afford it, the car ain't important." —Nas

5/ Honor the beginning, middle, and end of all relationships.

Your best friend today might not be your best friend tomorrow.

Your mentor, teacher, guru, might not be who you need to learn from in the next chapter.

6/ Build the life that's right for you.

Different people value different things in life.

Define your own purpose.

7/ Don't stress the final result. Just embrace the adventure.

There are no positive or negative outcomes.

Only lessons—some exciting, some painful, but lessons all the same.

8/ Live within your financial means.

The short-term enjoyment of "more things" doesn't outweigh the stress you inherit when you try to live a life you can't afford (yet).

9/ Never take for granted a genuine connection.

True friends are hard to find.

Honest conversations are rare.

Moments of vulnerability are special.

Soak these up.

10/ Be selfish and selfless.

Give to yourself, first.

So that you can give more to others.

And when you give, don't expect to "get" anything in return.

Enjoy the joy of being in a position to give selflessly in the first place.

11/ Who you are is more important than what you do.

Your career doesn't define you.

Your job title is irrelevant.

The measure of a person is who they are holistically, in all areas of their life.

12/ Do not hinder your own growth to preserve someone else's faulty foundation.

You are not responsible for their development (or lack thereof).

13/ Don't judge success by the day or week. Examine it over the course of a year or decade.

Growth is rarely spotted on a day-to-day basis.

It's like watching your hair grow.

Instead, monitor your trajectory over longer time horizons.

14/ Spend more time working on the "inner you" than the "outer you."

Confidence isn't your posture, clothes, watch, or car.

Beauty isn't the way you do your hair.

Everyone can see who you truly are, deep down.

So spend more time working on that version of yourself.

15/ Deprive yourself of what you want, to give yourself what you need.

16/ Always speak the truth.

As my grandma used to say, "Oh what a tangled web we weave when first we practice to deceive."

17/ Play the long game.

Slow and steady consistency is the most powerful driver of growth.

outliers.hook: Freelance Writing 101
outliers.text:
Freelance Writing 101

In 2016, I was making $50,000 per year as a copywriter at an advertising agency downtown Chicago.

Then, I quit my job.

30 days later, I was making $20,000 per month as a freelance writer.

Here's my Ultimate Guide to writing & making money online:

Step 1: Master ONE type of writing

The biggest mistake freelance writers make is spreading themselves thin.

- Writing blogs
- Writing newsletters
- Writing website copy
- Writing marketing materials

This is inefficient.

Master one thing. Do nothing else.

Step 2: Charge per ASSET, not per hour.

Writers who charge per hour (or worse, per WORD) are killing themselves.

Your value is not the # of hours you spend. 

It's the result you deliver.

Master 1 type of writing. And charge for the outcome.

Ex: 1 newsletter = $X.

Step 3: Work for free

The ROI on free work is limitless.

I went from being a nobody to ghostwriting for mega millionaires and billionaires by working for free for them, first.

Prove yourself. Show you can deliver the result. Keep sharpening your skills.

The money follows.

Step 4: Don't wait for clients. Sell!

After you've done some free work and proven you can *do it,* don't sit around.

Take it upon yourself to sell your services.

DM people you want to work for. Cold email. Tap your network. Get busy.

Even offer to do the 1st one free.

Step 5: Upsell current clients

There are 2 ways to grow your freelancing business.

- More, new clients
- More revenue from current clients.

The latter is 10x easier.

Ex: if your clients pay you to write newsletters, you can ALSO offer them long-form newsletters (for $3x).

Step 6: Raise your prices

When I first quit my job, I was charging $100 per ghostwritten article.

Then $200

Then $500

Then $800

Today, I charge almost $3,000 per ghostwritten article.

You have no idea where your *ceiling* is. 

The better you get, keep raising your prices.

Step 7: Optimize for GREAT clients (not mediocre ones)

Ideal clients save you time.

Mediocre ones cost you time.

$1,000 from an ideal client is better than $3,000 from a mediocre one. 

Ignore the dollar amount and focus on EFFICIENCY.

You'll earn more & work less.

Step 8: Celebrate each and every win

Every time you work with a bigger client, let all new prospective clients know.

Every time you reach some new milestone, use that in your sales conversations.

Make it very clear this is what you do, day in and day out.

Step 9: Put all your learnings into a product

The long-term gift of performing a service is racking up YEARS of expertise you can then turn into a product.

I did this by collecting all my strategies for digital writing and putting them into a book titled The Art & Business of Online Writing.

And then expanded that book into a cohort-based program called Ship 30 for 30, teaching people how to start writing online.

This is how you productize yourself and your learnings.

outliers.hook: Over the past 8 years, I have written more than 5,000 articles online.
outliers.text:
Over the past 8 years, I have written more than 5,000 articles online.

Accumulating 100 million+ views.

But guess what?

I use the same 5 headline styles every single time:

-

1/ The List

Everyone says they hate listicles.

...and then they click on them.

Lists are an amazing way to organize information in a way that is skimmable for the reader.

The key is:

If you're going to write a "7 Ways To..." article, you better deliver on your promise.

Here are some of my most-viral list article titles:

• 20 Things Most People Learn Too Late In Life (5M+ views)

• 19 Tiny Habits That Lead to Huge Results (2M+ views)

• 20 Things Nobody Tells You About Growing Up (1M+ views)

Lists work when the content DELIVERS.

-

2/ The Story

The best stories compress tons of information.

As Winston Churchill said: "If I had more time, I would have written you a shorter letter."

The key to writing a Story headline is this:

Be CLEAR, not Clever.

Writers think in order for it to be a "story," the title needs to be something mysterious.

This is a myth.

Ex: one of my most viral stories ever was:

What I Learned Being Betrayed By My 6 Closest Friends Growing Up

You 100% know what you're getting.

-

3/ The Opinion

If you're going to have an opinion, don't say it whilst sitting on the fence.

Pick a side.

Plant your flag in the ground.

Stand for something.

If you want to be heard on the Internet (whether you like it or not), you need to be polarizing.

Writers tend to misunderstand this advice and think they need to get political.

Polarizing just means knowing who your content ISN'T for.

And doubling-down on who it IS for.

Avoid words like: 

• Maybe
• Might
• Sometimes

And use words like:

• Absolutely
• Always

-

4/ The Question

If you are writing to answer a specific question your reader has, consider stating their question in the headline.

Ex: one of my most viral Q&A titles ever was:

How Do You Reinvent Yourself? Use These 7 Tiny But Powerful Mental Shifts

The secret here is DO NOT ANSWER THE QUESTION IN THE HEADLINE.

Don't say:

Want To Sleep Better? Give Up Coffee, Netflix, And Twitter

Instead say:

Want To Sleep Better? Give Up These 3 Everyday Guilty Pleasures

You have to create a curiosity gap for the reader.

-

5/ The Framework

My favorite headline style of all.

Readers LOVE frameworks. It feels like you're handing them a package that says: "Do this, it's a shortcut."

Frameworks can be:

• How To guides
• Templates
• Exercises
• Etc.

But in a Framework headline, there are 3 big things you need to communicate:

• What's the name of the framework?
• Who is it for?
• And why is it so important?

Ex:

The Endless Idea Generator: A Simple 5-Step Process For Generating 100 Article Ideas In 30 Minutes

99% of viral content on the Internet fits within these 5 headline styles.

They are everywhere.

Look at the next news notification on your phone—I bet it fits one of these styles.

So, use what works.

And make it your own.

outliers.hook: Want to write a 60,000-word book this October?
outliers.text:
Want to write a 60,000-word book this October?

Here's my (dead-simple) 3-step process:

The secret to writing a book?

Math.

• 10 chapters at 5,000 words each
• Each chapter has 5 to 7 sub-questions
• These sections are then only 700 to 1,000 words long

Each section is the length of a blog post or a newsletter.

So your book is a series of shorter posts bolted together.

Add in an introduction and conclusion, and you can easily hit 60,000 words.

Now, for the process.

Before you start writing, you need to have two items in place:

1. Your title
2. Your outline

If you don't have these, you don't know what you're writing about.

Now, here are the 3 steps to write each chapter:

Step 1: Prep the Chapter on the page

1. Write out the overarching question for the chapter
2. List out the sub-questions/topics for the question

With the chapter prepped, writing becomes a fill-in-the-blanks exercise (and far less intimidating).

Step 2: Expand each sub-question/topic

For each of these, ask yourself:

"What does the reader need to understand about the sub-question and overarching chapter question?"

The best way to think about this?

Use the 10 magical ways of expansion.

Any piece of writing can be expanded in 10 ways:

• Tips
• Stats
• Steps
• Lessons
• Benefits
• Reasons
• Mistakes
• Examples
• Questions
• Personal Stories

Mix and match these to answer the sub-questions and overarching question of the chapter for the reader.

Step 3: Make a round of edits

Don't get bogged down in this.

Some editing pointers:

• Look out for repetition
• Edit for flow and readability
• Do a final read-through out loud to pick up any clunky parts

And just like that, you wrote a 5,000-word chapter.

Repeat 11 more times & your book's finished.

Since then, I've published 10+ books.

And I made a 6-figure living while doing it.

outliers.hook: The easiest way to 10x your writing:
outliers.text:
The easiest way to 10x your writing:

Formatting — here's why:             

Formatting makes your work:

• Easier to skim
• Easier to quote & share
• And easier for readers to “remember” and repeat to their friends

So here's my 4-step formatting framework (this will change your life as a writer): 

Step 1: Start every section with a single-sentence opener.

When you open each area of your work with a single sentence, you:

• Keep readers interested
• Make things easier to read
• Subconsciously encourage them to continue

Here are the 6 most effective openers:

• Open with a moment in time
• Open with a vulnerable statement
• Open with a controversial opinion
• Open with a weird, unique insight
• Open with a thought-provoking question
• Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence

These are my go-to proven ways of hooking the reader.

--
Step 2: Always look for opportunities to turn long paragraphs into bulleted lists.

If you are:

• Listing anything, ever, or,
• Rambling off a dozen quick examples, or,
• Making a series of distinct points to drive home a point

...make it into a bulleted list.

(Like this!)

--
Step 3: Where are your subheads?

Subheads are bolded sentences that help the reader more easily follow your line of thinking.

• They split the page up evenly
• They offer big, bolded “milestones”
• They help the reader identify where they’re going

Subheads give clarity.

--
Step 4: Use the 1/3/1 writing rhythm.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

You can also use:

• 1/4/1
• 1/5/1
• 1/2/5/2/1
• Etc.

The key to injecting skimmability into your writing is alternating the length of sentences and sections.

--
In all your formatting choices, your primary concern should always be:

"How can I make this easier for the reader?"

• Is a bulleted list easiest? Do that.
• Is a step-by-step guide easiest? Do that.
• Is a series of small, rapid-fire sections easiest? Do that.

Your North Star is the reader's enjoyment.

---

outliers.hook: 19 brutal truths about going your own way in life:
outliers.text:
19 brutal truths about going your own way in life:

1. Nobody will believe in you until you believe in yourself.

2. The right way for someone else is the wrong way for you. (And the right way for you is the wrong way for someone else.)

3. It's only a mistake if you don't extract a lesson.

4. You can't change what you aren't aware of.

5. It's not about what rewards you receive, it's about who you become.

6. Greatness is a tiny decision you make each day.

7. Focus is the path of least resistance. (Simplicity is velocity.)

8. The world says you can't until you can.

9. What you want is who you are.

10. Actions speak louder than words, and results speak louder than aspirations.

11. Those who don't understand you don't understand themselves.

12. Other people's expectations are the heaviest burdens of all. (Put them down.)

13. Differentiation costs acceptance.

14. Errors are just misdirected efforts.

15. Wondering is a window, not a door.

16. Shortcuts are always longer roads in disguise.

17. You can only truly know what you have experienced.

18. Fulfillment is a byproduct of self-acceptance.

19. The journey is the joy.

1. Today is a great day to build.

2. No one is responsible for my success besides me.

3. I welcome the learning process with open arms.

4. Everyone was once a beginner.

5. Mastery over my craft is the highest accolade.

9. The enemy of long-term compounding is short-term results.

12. The world rewards those who do what others do not.

13. I trust myself and my convictions.

14. Every failure is an opportunity to learn.

15. Every success is an opportunity to start again.

16. Forward is the only option. There is no going back.

17. I own my shortcomings—and leverage them to my advantage.

outliers.hook: The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.
outliers.text:
The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.

Or, you could spend less than $250 and read these 8 legendary books on marketing, positioning, and copywriting:

1. Play Bigger by Christopher Lochhead, Al Ramadan, and Kevin Maney,

Category Kings win 76% of the category economics.

Everyone else fights over the remaining 24%.

You cannot be a legendary marketer in today's day and age without understanding Category Design.

--

2. Superconsumers by Eddie Yoon

The best way to grow your business isn't "more customers."

It's more Superconsumers.

Supers spend 30-70%+ more than the average consumer, and buy more products/services more often.

Win them, and you win the game.

--

3. Positioning by Al Ries & Jack Trout

One of the most timeless marketing books ever written, Positioning is the art of creating new space in the customer's mind for your product/service/offering.

No differentiated position, no meaningful company.

--

4. What They Don't Teach You at Harvard Business School by Mark H. McCormack

There's the way the world works "in theory." (School)

And then there's the way the world actually works.

This book is a wake-up call: relationships drive everything.

5. How To Win Friends & Influence People by Dale Carnegie

One of the first books to straddle the line between business and self-help.

This is a guide on communication, relationship-building, and creating your own definition for success.

--

6. Think & Grow Rich by Napoleon Hill

The fact this book isn't required reading in MBA programs blows my mind.

In order to become successful, Hill says you must position your Chief Aim in your mind's eye.

And obsessively pursue it.

--

7. The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson

Curated wisdom from Silicon Valley investor & founder of AngelList, Naval Ravikant, this is one of my favorite "business" books ever.

It is a guide for success while also acknowledging the flaws of the game.

--

8. The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel

Finally, before you graduate and enter "the real world," you should probably know...

Getting rich is easy.

Staying rich, and not behaving poorly with money, is the hard part.

--

9. BONUS: The Art & Business of Online Writing by Me 

The single most valuable skill you can have in the world today is the ability to communicate clearly, at scale.

Aka: writing online.

outliers.hook: A lot of writers misunderstand what "copywriting" actually is.
outliers.text:
A lot of writers misunderstand what "copywriting" actually is.

Let me start by telling you what it's not:

• It's not using *shocking* words to get people's attention
• It's not making outlandish promises you can't keep
• It's not using psychology to trick customers
• It's not hard-selling customers

The whole art of copywriting is really a combination of:

1. Category Design - Moving the customer's thinking FROM somewhere... to somewhere else.

2. Languaging - Strategically use language to coin terms for the Problem you're solving, the unique Solution you're deploying, and the categorical differences between the "old" and the "new."

3. Education - Demonstrating to the potential customer that you understand their problem SO intimately, and you know how to fix it SO actionably, that they walk away from your "pitch" more educated and knowledgable regardless of whether or not they buy.

4. Packaging - Making it obvious exactly what you're offering, and how it gets done, that all the customer/client has to do is say, "Ok let's do it."

5. Pricing - Price anchoring the value of what you're offering as either a path to earning 10x more than what they pay, or save 10x more than what they pay.

If you understand how to do the above 5 things, you can sell *anything* on planet earth—without it ever feeling like a "hard sell" to the customer.

outliers.hook: Over the past year, I have shared hundreds of small (but powerful) Writing Tips online.
outliers.text:
Over the past year, I have shared hundreds of small (but powerful) Writing Tips online.

But these 10 have accumulated more than 1,000,000 views and thousands of comments & shares. 

Here they are all in one place:

1/ Create skimmable lists

• Compress what you're saying into bullets
• Make sure you're emphasizing the right points
• Then expand each point in more detail

Said differently: If you don't know the bullet points of what you're writing, you don't have clarity in what you're saying.

2/ Delete the word “that.” 

99% of the time you don’t need it.

3/ Make the last word the most important word in the sentence.

"You knew who the first President was."

This is weak because "was" is not the most important word in the sentence.

Rewrite: 

"You knew the name of the first President."

4/ Increase engagement by increasing your RoR. 

Rate of Revelation = how quickly you introduce new information to the reader. 

Low score: 1 idea stretched across hundreds of paragraphs / pages. 

High score: 10+ ideas compressed into a handful of paragraphs / pages.

5/ Prep the page 

Staring a blank page is overwhelming.

To immediately make the writing less intimidating, "Prep the page."

• Name the document
• Start w/ a working title
• Write a first sentence
• Outline your subheads

Filling in the skeleton = 10x easier.

6/ Don't use semicolons.

Most readers don't know what they mean.

(And most writers use them incorrectly.)

Commas and periods are much cleaner.

7/ Make your first sentence a short, strong declarative statement.

Ex: "There is only 1 way to get rich."

BOOM.

That short, strong declarative statement hooks the reader, and tells them what they're about to dive into.

8/ Write in different interfaces (analog & digital).

• Physical journal
• Napkins
• Looseleaf paper
• Medium UI
• Quora answer
• Reedsy book editor
• Substack newsletter
• Microsoft Word doc
• Etc.

You will see your writing differently in each UI.

9/ Cut all tiny-word-chunks

• "And so as"
• "It is for"
• "If it is"
• "Is it that"

Anytime you notice 3+ tiny words stacked next to each other, delete them.

The sentence becomes cleaner.

I call this: "Pulling the weeds between your flowers."

10/ Delete opening "i" phrases

• "I think"
• "I believe"
• "I have learned"
• "I conclude"

You're the author. 

Which means all the things you're saying are your own thoughts, beliefs, lessons learned, and conclusions reached.

Repeating so is unnecessary.

outliers.hook: This week, I bought out the lease on my BMW. And as I was signing the papers, the sales guy asked me what I do for work:
outliers.text:
This week, I bought out the lease on my BMW. And as I was signing the papers, the sales guy asked me what I do for work:

“I’m a writer,” I said. 

“Have a couple companies. And I teach people how to write online.” 

He paused, looked at me and said, “Never heard that one before. And you’re not scared about AI taking your job?”

As he said this, he handed me a STACK of papers, that I had to sign with a pen, and initial 50 times, in order to buy my car. 

“Nope,” I said. 

Everybody thinks the future is here. 

Everyone thinks AI is going to replace everything, tomorrow. 

Meanwhile… most industries still require PEN AND PAPER. 

Yea. I’ll be fine. 

Thanks for the car.

outliers.hook: I can write the same idea in 4 different ways.
outliers.text:
I can write the same idea in 4 different ways.

It's called the 4A Framework—here's how it works:

Most writers don't realize you can write every idea in 4 different ways:

• Actionable (Here's how)
• Analytical (Here's a breakdown)
• Aspirational (Yes, you can)
• Anthropological (Here's why)

Let's take an example using the topic of personal finance.

• "I Will Teach You To Be Rich" by Ramit Sethi is Actionable
• "The Changing World Order" by Ray Dalio is Analytical 
• "Rich Dad Poor Dad" by Robert Kiyosaki is Aspirational 
• "The Psychology of Money" by Morgan Housel is Anthropological 

Now, for each "A" you have different ways of writing about it:

1/ Actionable

These are actionable, implemental pieces of content.

The reader should gain some new insight or instruction they didn't have beforehand.

• Tips
• Hacks
• Resources
• Ultimate guides

Take your core idea and help the reader put it into practice.

2/ Analytical

These are breakdowns involving numbers, frameworks, and processes.

Take your core idea and support it with numbers and analysis.

• Trends
• Reasons
• Numbers
• Examples
• Swipe files
• Teardowns

Help the reader unlock a new way of thinking.

3/ Aspirational

These are stories of how you or others put your core idea into practice.

• Traits
• Stories
• Quotes
• Lessons
• Mistakes
• Reflections

Help the reader understand the benefits they unlock when they see the world through this new lens.

4/ Anthropological

These are things that speak to universal human nature.

• Fears
• Failures
• Struggles
• Limiting beliefs
• Misconceptions

Create a sense of urgency for the reader to fully embrace your core idea or be forever left behind.

That's it!

If you use this framework, you'll never be short of writing ideas again.

---

• Generate hundreds of ideas
• Start going viral with ease

outliers.hook: I wasted years as a horrible writer.
outliers.text:
I wasted years as a horrible writer.

But then I found Tim Ferriss's writing routine and the mental models behind his 5 New York Times best-selling books.

4 mental models you can start using today:

1. Two "crappy pages" per day

Tim sets a low bar to build a consistent daily writing habit.

While writing, Tim thinks, writes, and edits as if he is catching up with an old friend over a few drinks.

This immediately takes the pressure off producing "quality."

2. Voice notes and interviews

When Tim is stuck with a chapter, he hires someone to interview him about the topic.

This thinking out loud forces him to test and refine his core ideas.

3. Fear Setting Exercise

Self-doubt haunted Tim, so he turned to the Stoic philosophy of Seneca, who taught:

“We suffer more in imagination than in reality.”

Tim created a practice to see that he could handle whatever "bad" situation might arise. This lowered the stakes and helped him to start writing.

4. Copywork

To find his voice, Tim copied the styles of journalists he admired, like Chip Heath and Malcolm Gladwell.

• He'd find a piece he admired
• Then rewrite word-by-word digitally or by hand

Stephen King is also a big believer in this practice, too.

One final note:

Tim pitched "The 4-Hour Workweek" to 27 publishers. 

26 rejected him.

But his lucky break came when he met Jack Canfield, author of "Chicken Soup for the Soul."

Jack saw the book's potential and pulled strings to get it sold.

To date, Tim has sold over 2.1 million copies of the book.

The lesson?

Keep trying and putting in the work—you create your own luck.

That's it!

It's how I built a $180k/month agency in less than 18 months.

outliers.hook: I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight.
outliers.text:
I've journaled for 30 minutes every morning for 14 years straight. 

No fancy journal. No complex rules. 

Just a pen, paper, and 4 simple prompts to help focus my mind and find clarity.

Here's the dead-simple system I use every day:

Prompt 1: "What did I do yesterday?"

The very first thing I do is reflect on the past 24 hours:

• What did I do yesterday?
• How did it feel?
• What did I learn?

I like this prompt because only the most important things come to mind.

Pay attention.

---
Prompt 2: "What am I working on?"

A big part of journaling for me is repeating things I want to internalize:

• Repeating goals
• Repeating mindset patterns
• Repeating feelings & lessons

The more I repeat it in my journal, the more I think about it during the day.

---
Prompt 3: "What's coming up?"

Next, I look toward the future.

1. What's around the bend?
2. What can I start proactively processing through now?

This helps prepare the mind in advance for feelings, challenges, or obstacles—and helps you not feel so "caught off guard."

---
Prompt 4: "What am I grateful for?"

I end every journaling session with a Thank You.

I thank the path in front of me.

I remind myself I am grateful for every opportunity to learn, whether it's difficult or not.

And I welcome the day to come with open arms.

---
I started journaling deliberately like this when I was 19 years old.

I'd just gotten back from a rehab trip, and felt extremely lost & confused. I didn't know what I wanted out of life, or whether I would amount to much.

So I wrote to myself about it.

This has become my favorite part about journaling over long periods of time:

You can literally see the progress in front of you.

Things I journaled about when I was 19, I don't journal about anymore. I've grown out of them. Keeping a journal documents your growth as a human.

I will keep this habit until the day I die.

I attribute a significant portion of my success over the past 10 years to my daily journaling practice.

There is no better way to hear yourself, become conscious of your thoughts, and take actionable steps forward.

---

outliers.hook: In 2015, Elon Musk asked the writer Tim Urban to decode his $258 billion net worth.
outliers.text:
In 2015, Elon Musk asked the writer Tim Urban to decode his $258 billion net worth.

As the "Wait But Why" writer, Urban was known for dissecting complex topics.

He'd written about everything from EVs to space exploration. 

Six months and 1,000s of hours of research later, Urban found 5 cornerstones to the Elon Universe:

1. First Principles Thinking.

Elon has the superpower to ignore conventional wisdom. He reasons from the ground up based on facts.

When everyone said battery packs were expensive and impossible to scale, Elon used this framework to ask why and found ways to manufacture these at scale.

2. Dream big. Insanely big.

In the past 5 years alone, Elon has: 

• Sent +400 rockets to space
• Accelerated EV by +5 years
• Increased AI Compute Power by 500% 

Elon sees his projects as civilization-level endeavors—which is the burning fire behind his 120+ hour workweek.

3. Create a mission-driven culture.

Urban found Elon’s radical vision and obsession for the greater good of humanity ignited a culture of passionate innovation at his companies that you can’t find anywhere else.

4. Become obsessed with systems.

Elon believes life is intertwined with technology. 

He even described his children as "computers," indicating how he perceives most relationships through logic and utility rather than emotions.

This "systems-based thinking" helps him identify bottlenecks and create efficiencies.

5. Live life like it's a video game.

In a video game, there is:

• No need to protect your identity
• No fear of the opinion of others

Urban found that all great people (like Elon) live life like a video game and "stay hungry, and foolish" (to quote Steve Jobs) at all times.

By writing a deep dive on Musk, Urban gained exclusive access to learn from him directly:

• First Principles Thinking. 
• Dream big. Insanely big.
• Create a mission-driven culture.
• Become obsessed with systems.
• Live life like it's a video game.

These are the cornerstones of Elon's success.

Digital Writers like Tim Urban can get access to the top 1%. 

But Premium Ghostwriters get paid to learn and network with industry experts.

I've ghostwritten for 300+ industry leaders:

• CEOs 
• Crypto billionaires
• Silicon Valley founders

And more! 

Want to become a Premium Ghostwriter? 

Here's a free, 5-day crash course to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ey5KgfRj

outliers.hook: The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.
outliers.text:
The average tuition for an MBA comes to $60,000 per year.

That means you're $120,000 in debt...

...before you begin your business journey!

Or, you could spend less than $250 and read these 8 legendary books on marketing, positioning, and copywriting:

1/ Play Bigger
 
Category Kings win 76% of the category economics.

Everyone else fights over the remaining 24%.

You cannot be a legendary marketer in today's day and age without understanding Category Design.

2/ Superconsumers
 
The best way to grow your business isn't "more customers."

It's more Superconsumers.

Supers spend 30-70%+ more than the average consumer, and buy more products/services more often.

Win them, and you win the game.

3/ Positioning

One of the most timeless marketing books ever written, Positioning is the art of creating new space in the customer's mind for your product/service/offering.

No differentiated position, no meaningful company.

4/ What They Don't Teach You at Harvard Business School

There's the way the world works "in theory." (School)

And then there's the way the world actually works.

This book is a wake-up call: relationships drive everything.

5/ How To Win Friends & Influence People

One of the first books to straddle the line between business and self-help.

This is a guide to communication, relationship-building, and creating your own definition for success.

6/ Think & Grow Rich

The fact this book isn't required reading in MBA programs blows my mind.

In order to become successful, Hill says you must position your Chief Aim in your mind's eye.

And obsessively pursue it.

7/ The Almanack of Naval Ravikant
 
Curated wisdom from Silicon Valley investor & founder of AngelList, Naval Ravikant, this is one of my favorite "business" books ever.

It is a guide for success while also acknowledging the flaws of the game.

8/ The Psychology of Money
 
Finally, before you graduate and enter "the real world," you should probably know...

Getting rich is easy. 

Staying rich, and not behaving poorly with money, is the hard part.

9/ BONUS: The Art & Business of Online Writing

The single most valuable skill you can have in the world today is the ability to communicate clearly, at scale.

Aka: writing online.

I can't stop thinking about a TikTok I saw the other day.

It said:

"Procrastination is self-preservation. You're avoiding doing the work to protect yourself from rejection."

This is so true.

But even after we choose a goal, we sabotage ourselves:

• We pick something.
• The first 3 reps feel great.
• Then it gets hard, and we quit.

Quitting is also self-preservation.

But realize this:

It's just hours. 

Anything you want in life, unless constrained by biology, is a fixed number of hours away. 

The problem?

The number of hours is unknown, and it's usually much higher than you want it to be.

So... you quit. 

You aren't making progress because you keep starting over.

Because you don't "escape" from life and THEN move toward your goal. 

The skill to build is being able to pursue a goal despite being surrounded by:

• Chaos
• Responsibilities
• And notifications

If you can build this skill, you can achieve anything. 

And if you don't, you won't.

So pick 1 thing now, and fully COMMIT.

And realize you can do this 3-4 times in your life, but no more.

For me, I've committed fully to writing.

And my skill as a writer accelerated the fastest when I became a ghostwriter.

I ghostwrote for 300+ industry leaders (including CEOs, Silicon Valley founders, and NYT best-selling authors).

outliers.hook: This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon:
outliers.text:
This writer was Steve Jobs's secret weapon:

• He helped save Apple from bankruptcy
• Turned 'Think Different' into $3 trillion
• And crafted the iMac story

When Ken Segall joined Apple's ad agency in 1997, Apple was:

• 90 days from bankruptcy
• Its brand message was a mess
• And sales were plummeting 

Jobs needed someone who could turn complex tech into simple stories.

Enter Ken Segall, the creative director who'd write the words to define Apple.

His first task? 

Create a campaign to tell the world Apple was back.

Segall's team presented several campaigns to Jobs—but one stood above the rest: 

"Think Different"

Two simple words that would become the foundation of Apple's revival. 

Segall used 5 writing rules to bring Apple back from the brink:

1. Simplicity is power. 

• Remove every unnecessary word
• Cut complex explanations
• Focus on emotional impact

The "Think Different" tagline is just 2 words, and the campaign was full of black & white photos.

2. The name tells the story.

Before Segall, Apple's computers had names like Performa 6400, PowerBook 5300, & Macintosh Quadra 800.

Segall convinced Jobs to simplify everything to "i" products:

• iMac
• iPad
• iPhone

Steve Jobs wanted to call Apple’s new computer "MacMan," even though it sounded like a toy. 

Segall pitched "iMac," with a focus on simplicity and the internet—but Jobs hated it.

Yet, after rejecting other names, Jobs quietly approved it.

Segall later joked, "Without it, we’d have PhoneMans and PadMans today."

3. Write like you talk.

Before Segall, Apple's marketing was filled with technical jargon and specs.

He transformed it into copy that was:

• Simple
• Conversational
• Easy to understand

4. Find the drama.

Every product needed a story:

• iPod: 1,000 songs in your pocket
• iPhone: A revolutionary mobile phone
• iMac: The internet computer for everyone

This would capture the customer's imagination.

5. Follow proven frameworks.

His writing framework was simple:

• Start with the problem (make it personal)
• Build emotional tension (highlight the stakes)
• Introduce the solution (make it irresistible)
• Show the transformation (paint the "after")
• End with a clear CTA (drive action)

The power of great writing transformed Apple from near bankruptcy to the world's most valuable company.

But the modern-day Segalls aren't writing for Apple. 

They're ghostwriters scaling the message of founders, CEOs, and businesses.

Because every founder & business leader needs a Ken Segall to scale their message online.

But founders are too busy building to write—creating massive demand for skilled ghostwriters.

I know because I've made $3,000,000+ as a ghostwriter.

It's how I learned from industry leaders & got paid to practice my writing.

📌 I created this free Premium Ghostwriting Blueprint to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/eRCDv66F

outliers.hook: The easiest way to 10x your writing:
outliers.text:
The easiest way to 10x your writing:

(Without spending hours editing & rewriting)

Formatting.

But why is it important?

Because it makes your writing:

• Easier to skim
• Easier to quote & share
• And easier for readers to “remember” and repeat to their friends

Here's a dead-simple, 4-step method to format your writing:

Step 1: Start every section with a single-sentence opener.

When you open each area of your work with a single sentence, you:

• Keep readers interested
• Make things easier to read
• Subconsciously encourage them to continue

Here are the 6 most effective openers:

• Open with a moment in time
• Open with a vulnerable statement
• Open with a controversial opinion
• Open with a weird, unique insight
• Open with a thought-provoking question
• Open with 1 strong, declarative sentence

These are my go-to proven ways of hooking the reader.

--
Step 2: Always look for opportunities to turn long paragraphs into bulleted lists.

If you are:

• Listing anything, ever, or,
• Rambling off a dozen quick examples, or,
• Making a series of distinct points to drive home a point

...make it into a bulleted list.

(Like this!)

--
Step 3: Where are your subheads?

Subheads are bolded sentences that help the reader more easily follow your line of thinking.

• They split the page up evenly
• They offer big, bolded “milestones”
• They help the reader identify where they’re going

Subheads give clarity.

--
Step 4: Use the 1/3/1 writing rhythm.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

You can also use:

• 1/4/1
• 1/5/1
• 1/2/5/2/1
• Etc.

The key to injecting skimmability into your writing is alternating the length of sentences and sections.

--
In all your formatting choices, your primary concern should always be:

"How can I make this easier for the reader?"

• Is a bulleted list easiest? Do that.
• Is a step-by-step guide easiest? Do that.
• Is a series of small, rapid-fire sections easiest? Do that.

Your North Star is the reader's enjoyment.

---

They should have been blog posts.

outliers.hook: I got married this past week in Cartagena, Colombia!
outliers.text:
I got married this past week in Cartagena, Colombia!

Here's something I wrote for my wife, and shared during our ceremony, about what it means to find your life partner:

~
One of my favorite writers, Charles Bukowski,
said the problem is,
we look for someone to grow old together,
When the secret is to find someone to stay a child with.

When I was a child,
I asked my mother for her definition of love.
She said love is the ability to grow together.
That life is hard, people will change,
But your love for each other should always remain.

I am not the same person I was when we met.
And in the best way, neither are you.
I had never been on a safari in South Africa.
You had never been to a Chicago Blackhawks hockey game.
I hadn’t lived anywhere other than the Midwest.
And you had lived all over the world, but weren’t sure which place to call Home.

In the beginning of our relationship we used to say,
“Home isn’t a place, it’s a feeling.”
Because we lived a thousand miles apart, and getting to know each other wasn’t convenient.
Being together meant choosing each other.
Getting on planes. Meeting up in different cities.
And sad goodbyes in airports,
Until the next time we’d see each other,
And finally feel at home again.

We’re getting married today, but we committed to each other a long time ago.
From the day we met, it felt like everything else in our life was negotiable—except for our relationship.
We could live anywhere.
We could do anything.
As long as we were together.

I know, and you know, that I wouldn’t be the man I am today without you.
You are my mirror,
My greatest motivator, my battery pack, my passport to the world.
You have taught me more about myself than I could have ever learned on my own.
You have made me stronger.
You have made me softer.
But most of all, you have taught me what it means to take care of someone.
To be selfless. To love without conditions.
And to meet each other halfway,
And not just love the things we share in common,
But love what makes us different.

So if love is the ability to grow together,
And the secret is to find someone to stay a child with,
Then I know you are my partner for life.
Because years have passed,
We’ve grown and changed,
I am not the same person I was when we met,
And in the best way, neither are you.
But I still feel like we’re kids
and our love has always remained.

outliers.hook: 7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":
outliers.text:
7 hard lessons I've learned on the journey to "success":

When I graduated from college, I moved into a cramped studio apartment.

8 years later, I moved into my dream condo on the beach.

Here's what I learned:

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟏: 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐡𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐝𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐞.

I wrote every day in my studio. Even in 100-degree heat.

I'd sit at my desk in my underwear & take cold showers to cool off.

Only difference today? AC.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟐: 𝐀𝐥𝐥 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐧𝐞𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐬 𝐚 𝐛𝐞𝐝 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚 𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐤.

For the first 3 years after college, I slept on a $79 air mattress. My desk was $20 from Goodwill.

Today, the 2 things that mean the most to me are my bed & desk.

It's all you need.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟑: 𝐃𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐨𝐟 𝐬𝐮𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬.

In my early 20s, I was so determined to become a successful writer, I deprived myself of internet for 4 years.

My friends all told me I was crazy.

But 4 years later, I was an author.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟒: 𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐟𝐢𝐱 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠.

I was very insecure about my apt. I was ashamed of how poor I was.

And I didn't date for years because of it.

It took me years to realize those insecurities were my own and money wouldn't fix them.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟓: 𝐈𝐟 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐜𝐚𝐧'𝐭 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞, 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐰𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐝𝐨 𝐢𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞.

After college, I listened to a lot of my peers say:

"Once I get to X, THEN I'll do Z."

But the goalpost kept moving. And they never got "there."

Success means doing what isn't convenient.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟔: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐯𝐞, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐲 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐝.

I am surrounded by 10x more distractions today than I was 10 years ago:

• TV
• Travel
• Disposable income

In my 20s, I didn't have these options.

This was a gift.

𝐋𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟕: "𝐖𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠" 𝐢𝐬 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐟𝐮𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 "𝐇𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠".

Two days after I moved into my dream condo, I had my weekly therapy session.

"Now what?" I asked.

I'd spent a decade pounding the pavement, trying to get "here."

I got "here" and didn't know how to enjoy it.

Final story:

A few years ago, I ghostwrote for a Grammy-winning producer.

He flew me out to his home: a ranch with a home music studio.

In awe, I said, "Your creative process must be so different. You have so many options!"

He shook his head, "Actually, it's killing me."

He planned to rent a city studio apt to make his next album.

We think the journey will get easier once we've achieved our dreams or made a certain amount of money.

It's the opposite.

It gets harder.

So, enjoy wherever you are today.

Everything might change.

But the work won't.

-
📌 Ghostwriting completely changed my life.

Here's how to land $5k-$10k clients & get paid to learn & build your network:

https://lnkd.in/eGAyHceM

outliers.hook: The bottleneck to your personal growth isn't "working harder."
outliers.text:
The bottleneck to your personal growth isn't "working harder."

It's having the courage to:

• Work on something different
• Outside your comfort zone
• To acquire new skills

Which means transforming yourself into a different person.

People only change when the discomfort of not-changing exceeds their current comfort levels.

This is why so many people, once they get settled in a career, struggle so much to change directions.

And why people who have "nothing to lose" tend to be the ones who succeed.

So, how can you make being "uncomfortable," and moving forward, your new comfort zone?

That's something to journal on this morning.

outliers.text:
In 4 years, I went from being a broke freelance writer to generating over $3,000,000 as a ghostwriter.

And I did it without these tools:

• AI
• A fancy website
• Or a complex funnel

But if I had to start from zero today, these are the 7 no-code tools I'd use as a ghostwriter:

1. Hemingway Editor

Gives your writing a grade and shows you where your content is:

• Hard to read
• Could be simplified
• Written in a passive voice

Now includes AI to rewrite content with your chosen tone.

2. Mem AI

AI-enhanced notes app that organizes client interviews:

• Summarizes notes automatically
• Writes content based on transcripts
• Recalls specific details across all notes

3. Wordable

Publishes from Google Docs to client websites with one click.

Works with WordPress, HubSpot, and Medium.

No more manual formatting or CMS headaches.

4. Notion

Your all-in-one workspace for:

• Client CRM tracking
• Content storage and proposals
• Project plans (with client access)

The Free plan works perfectly.

5. Loom

Record yourself and screen for:

• Client pitches
• Project explanations
• Content edit walkthroughs

The best way to delight your clients and reduce meetings.

6. Fathom

AI notetaker for Zoom, Google Meet, and Teams.

Auto-transcribes, summarizes, and creates action points.

Ask the AI chatbot questions about any meeting.

7. Kit (ConvertKit)

This is the perfect place to:

• Hold your own email list
• Build sample automations
• Send one-off emails and announcements to your list

As you grow as a ghostwriter, building your own email list is how to land more clients.

Then, later, use it to diversify your income with digital products.

These tools will completely change how you run your ghostwriting business.

—

But what service should you start with?

I recommend LinkedIn Ghostwriting.

So, I put together a 30-day roadmap to help you land your first (or next) LinkedIn ghostwriting client—with 7 AI prompts to make it 10x easier.

outliers.hook: I have been in weekly therapy for over 10 years.
outliers.text:
I have been in weekly therapy for over 10 years.

$100 per session
1x per week
4x per month
10 years

= $50,000

But here are 7 ways therapy has changed my life, and why I'd gladly pay 2x, 3x, 5x, 10x more all over again: 

-

Therapy gave me permission to explore how I felt.

The first 3+ years, all I did was rant and ramble about how I saw the world. 

And as a kid who didn't get this "space," being able to just talk and talk was all I needed.

And slowly, I started to be able to hear myself.

-

Therapy created distance between me and my worst feelings.

By being able to talk with someone else, consistently, every week, I started to see how I felt "objectively."

This gave me the opportunity to see them as separate from "myself," question them, & ultimately change them.

-

Therapy was a forcing function for accountability.

The moment I started, I knew I needed to go weekly.

Otherwise, I'd fall off.

And sure enough, when therapy rolled around every week, I was forced to rise to the occasion.

Slow steps, compounded over a long period of time.

-

Therapy allowed me to move from Reactive to Proactive.

When I first started therapy, it was to "work through" past pain.

But as I learned about myself and grew, therapy became a proactive exercise in anticipating future struggles.

And getting ahead of them.

-

Therapy taught me compassion.

"You can only meet others as far as you've met yourself."

The more I did this inner work, the more I understood other people and why they were the way they were.

I could see they were afraid to change.

B/c I had been afraid too.

-

Therapy gave me a foundation of first principles emotional skills.

Despite years of therapy, life still challenges me (and all of us) often.

But when things happen, at least now I have tools I can use to reconnect within myself again.

And operate from a place of clarity.

-

Therapy has made me a more effective, driven individual.

I didn't know this would be an outcome—and most people I talk to don't either.

But I attribute the MAJORITY of my success in life to my years in therapy.

B/c now I understand where the choices I make come from in myself.

I now pay $500/hr for weekly therapy, and have continued seeking out more and more knowledgeable people to work with and learn from.

And as I make more and achieve more in life, I will likely pay even more.

It's a non-negotiable cost.

Even when I was poor, I still paid for it.

One of my goals in life is to help de-stigmatize therapy and inner work, especially for men.

Because especially in business, most men I know ignore this work completely.

They achieve external success, but remain emotionally stunted.

Which limits what they do with their $$$.

outliers.hook: I studied fiction writing in college.
outliers.text:
I studied fiction writing in college.

But I always tell people I didn't learn how to write in college.

I learned how to read, and I learned the importance of reading my work out loud.

Here's a mini-masterclass in what to HEAR in your own writing:

-

1/ Listen for tone. 

As soon as the words start to come out of your mouth, you will feel what tone you were writing in.

The big question is: was this tone your intention?

If yes, you'll hear what gives this tone its quality. Do more of that.

If no, you'll hear why not.

-

2/ Listen for wordiness.

"And so it was then that..."

Anytime you hear lots of tiny words next to hear other, you'll feel your mouth fumble over them.

Don't ignore this.

Cut them. Compress them.

Your writing will be sharper.

-

3/ Listen for off-brand language.

"I looked at her with glimmering eyes..."

The moment an off-brand description, word, or phrase falls out of your mouth, you will cringe in pain.

It'll be so potently apparent that isn't you, or isn't the real voice of the character.

Cut it.

-

4/ Listen for pace.

Long descriptions seem great when they're in your head.

But read them out loud and you'll feel yourself getting bored of yourself.

Don't discount this feeling.

The moment you start zoning out, the description has died.

Compress it. Get to the point.

-

5/ Listen for your own emotions.

Reading your writing out loud makes it real.

In emotional moments, you'll feel your chest tighten.

In vulnerable moments, you'll feel your throat close.

In hilarious moments, you'll let out a laugh.

These = your BEST writing.

-

Reading your writing out loud is the most important part of becoming a great writer.

You will learn much more about yourself and how to improve just by speaking the words.

All inadequacies reveal themselves, in the best way.

Now you know what to fix.

outliers.hook: I wrote a 70,000-word novel with ChatGPT.
outliers.text:
Nicolas Cole 🚢👻 reposted this

I wrote a 70,000-word novel with ChatGPT.

Parts were great, parts were horrible.

Here's what I learned:

***

I had two questions I wanted to answer?

1. Can AI help me write faster? 

2. Can AI write a quality book? 

The first got a Yes, the second a definitive No.

But my biggest takeaway after countless hours testing:

AI is an accelerant, not a replacement.

outliers.hook: Drinking my coffee & reflecting this morning.
outliers.text:
Drinking my coffee & reflecting this morning.

A few thoughts on cultivating an abundance mindset:

-

I feel least creative when I am "competing" with others.

I do my worst work when I am trying to "beat" someone else.

-

A mentor of mine once asked me:

"Do you love to win? Or hate to lose?"

How you answer this question reveals how you see the world.

An abundance mindset = running your own race.

• If you "love to win," that means others can too.

• But if you "hate to lose," that means life is a zero-sum game.

There can only be 1 winner.

This is a scarcity mindset.

-

In a scarcity mindset, the world has a fixed number of:

• Opportunities
• Resources
• Friendships/relationships
• Money
• Fame/attention

Your job is to then "compete" over the above.

Which means your entire headset is rooted in being "better" than the next person.

But in an abundance mindset, there is no "lack."

• You can create net-new opportunities
• You can share friendships
• You can generate more money together
• You can compound fame/attention
• You can invent resources

Your "competition" becomes your collaborators.

-

When you live your life with an abundance mindset: 

1+1 = 3

It's not *just* that you can create/achieve more together.

It's that you can unlock opportunities you otherwise would never be able to alone.

Life is not a 1-player game.

Some rewards require multiple players.

-

How do you cultivate an abundance mindset?

When you compare, consider how you can collaborate.

When you are jealous, consider what you can learn.

When you want to fight, consider how you can be friends.

When you want respect, consider how you can give it first.

When you want to prove how smart you are, consider the benefits of being "the dumbest person in the room."

When you are let down or betrayed, consider the lesson you can share with others.

When you are succeeding, consider how you can throw down a rope to the next person.

-

Those who measure their success in "things," titles, trophies, or achievements, tend to live life through a scarcity mindset.

They love they won—but they love even more that someone else lost.

But those who measure their success in impact, and how many people they've helped achieve their own personal transformation, tend to live life through an abundance mindset.

They love they won.

But they love that others won even more.

outliers.hook: 13 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:
outliers.text:
13 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

2. Successful people are neither smarter nor more creative than you. 

They are simply executing better.

3. Success comes from doing somewhat-monotonous things every day for years – but no one wants to hear that.

4. You can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation.

5. 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity.

6. You said you would start months ago.

7. You can complain about the game or you can learn the rules.

8. The market rewards not the best product, but the product who best positions themselves as the obvious solution.

9. If you did everything you said you'd do, your results would be 10x better than they are. 

10. 99% of people would rather be uncomfortable and unhappy than uncertain.

11. No one on Earth thinks about you as much as you think they do.

12. You know exactly what you should be doing, so stop chasing shiny objects. 

Instead, figure out why you aren't doing what you know you should be. 

13. If you spent half as many hours taking action as you did thinking about taking action, you would be in a better spot.

outliers.hook: The single most powerful habit for personal growth:
outliers.text:
The single most powerful habit for personal growth:

Journaling.

Over the past 5 years, I've journaled every single morning.

And I always return to these 5 prompts:

Prompt 1: The Morning Kickstart

No one has time to write a novel every morning—so here's what I do instead:

I start with 5 questions: 

• What's 1 thing I'm grateful for? 
• What's 1 thing I'm excited about? 
• What's 1 virtue I want to exhibit? 
• What's 1 thing I'm avoiding? 
• What's the 1 thing I need to do? 

 I set a 5-minute timer and each question gets 1 sentence, max.

This prompt covers:

• Present moment gratitude
• Future excitement for the day ahead
• Reminders about the type of person I'm trying to become (and avoid becoming)

And it ends with a reminder of the 1 most important thing I need to today.

Prompt 2: The Evening Shutdown

Just like the Morning Kickstart, this one has to be quick.

So I set a 5-minute timer and answer:

• What were my biggest wins of the day?
• Did I have any major realizations?
• What's on the agenda for tomorrow?

By recapping my biggest wins & realizations, I keep a ledger of:

• The good things happening in my life
• The things I'm learning about myself & the world

And by brain dumping bullets on the day ahead I let my subconscious work overnight.

Prompt 3: The Bottleneck Analysis

This prompt has accelerated my personal growth.

And it has 2 questions:

1. What's the biggest bottleneck to achieving my next goal?
2. Why aren't I working on it today?

The truth is, you know exactly what you should be doing.

So—stop looking for new things and do the things you know you should be doing.

Prompt 4: The 80/20 Life Audit

First start with 2 lists:

• Where am I feeling satisfied?
• Where am I feeling dissatisfied?

Brain dump anything and everything—health, finances, relationships, career, spirituality.

Then, take each list and find the 20% of:

• Places
• Habits
• People
• Beliefs

That are leading to 80% of the positive & negative results.

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

During this reflection, patterns will emerge.

The same people, habits, and beliefs—all leading to your best and worst results.

From there, your goal is to:

• Double down on the 20% positive
• Ruthlessly eliminate the 20% negative

The result?

An instant life upgrade.

Prompt 5: The Compounding Projection

It's cliché, but all successful outcomes come from compounding.

Our success (or failure) is a result of tiny, daily actions compounded over time.

But in the moment, it can be hard to gauge your progress.

So ask yourself these 2 questions:

1. If I repeated every action I took today, every day for a year, where would I end up?
2. Is this the place I want to be?

If you're confident in your current path, this prompt will help you be more patient.

But if you're on the wrong path, it will help you course correct.

Boom, that's it!

These are the 5 prompts I come back to time and time again and they always leave me with a feeling of *elite* clarity.

outliers.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
outliers.text:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting—and before they know it, years have gone by and it's too late to change.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

The goal of the monthly review:

Block 60 minutes to slowly process the month.

It's easy to get caught up in the day to day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

And after trying 1000+ reflection questions, I chose each question for a specific reason.

Let's start with the first:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things that you will look back on with pride & fond memories at the end of the year.

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, our focus is on the negative. But this questions helps us celebrate all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

From there, you ask a more important question:

---
2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—because something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

And by constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By constantly iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

---
3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page in 2 columns and brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

The goal here is to identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

---
4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive *and* negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to *double down* on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to *stop doing entirely* that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

---
5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a quick list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

---
And just like that, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things throughout the month.

• Wins & milestones

• Realizations & decisions

• Things going well & things to improve

Then, at the end of the year, you collect these monthly reviews and distill the entire year.

So ask these 2 questions:

• If I repeated every action I took today, every day for a year, where would I end up?
• Is this the place I want to be?

If you're confident in your current path, this will help you be more patient.

But if you're on the wrong path, it will help you course correct.

Boom, that's it!

These 5 prompts always leave me with a feeling of *elite* clarity.

📌 I used these prompts to start writing online & make my first $10k as a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting helped me see a world beyond Wall Street so I could ultimately quit my 9 to 5.

Want to get paid to write & work with industry leaders?

outliers.hook: I used to have one severe addiction:
outliers.text:
I used to have one severe addiction:

Planning.

My brain loved the cheap dopamine of gathering:

• Books to read
• Tasks to complete
• And videos to watch

But when it came to taking action, the euphoric rush faded.

What ended my addiction?

This story of two men learning to fish:

The first fisherman heads straight to the marina to buy the best fishing boat money can buy.

He specs out a custom, carbon fiber rod.

And he spends time reading up on everything there is to know about fishing:

• Casting techniques
• Baiting techniques
• Reeling techniques

You name it, he's bought the book on it.

And of course, he's watching the fishing channel every night for hours, learning from the best of the best.

A few months later, he finally feels ready to fish.

The next day, he heads out on the lake in his new boat with his fancy rod and wealth of fishing "experience" built up over the last few months.

But six hours in, nothing. Not even a nibble.

What happened?

Turns out, the second fisherman had already sucked the lake dry.

On day one, the second fisherman grabbed a shitty rod, sliced up a hot dog, and went out to the lake.

• The first day, nothing
• Second day, nothing
• Third day, nothing
• One week, nothing

But on the eighth day? His first nibble.

And every day after, he honed his technique.

He found a baiting technique that worked a bit better. Or a new spot with a few more fish.

And after three weeks of iteration, he found the winning combination.

Before long, he was hauling in hundreds of fish every day. To keep up with his daily haul, he hired a small team to help him. And from there, he opened a restaurant with fat margins to sell his fresh fish.

And after that, he opened a fishing school to teach others his craft.

He did all of this while the first fisherman sat around gathering gear.

Sure, the first fisherman felt productive. He was "learning" after all, right?

But there was one difference: feedback.

Every day the second fisherman had an idea of something that might work better. And he went right to the market to validate his assumption. If it worked, his strategy improved. And if it failed, he learned something new.

The moral of this story?

Get to action as quickly as possible.

When you find yourself gathering gear, grab a shitty rod and start fishing.

outliers.hook: 7 signs your job is about to be replaced by ChatGPT-4:
outliers.text:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

7 signs your job is about to be replaced by ChatGPT-4:

~

1) You get paid per hour (for digital work)

"Hourly" is a signal you are getting paid for your time & effort.

Not your value.

The age of AI is going to (painfully) reveal how many people have been getting paid to "sit there" vs getting paid to generate an outcome.

~

2) You are a "digital laborer."

These are the new blue-collar jobs:

• SEO writers
• Website optimizers
• Back-end developers
• Landing page builders
• Google AdWords optimizers
• Etc.

If you currently get paid to build "piping" for the Internet, bye bye.

~

3) Your task list = "doing," not "thinking."

All the highest-paid people, in any industry, don't get paid to "do."

They get paid to "think"—and then other human beings "do the doing."

So if AI replaces a lot of the "doing..."

Then you need to stop "doing" & start "thinking."

~

4) Your work requires zero creative decision-making.

• Taking notes on calls
• Writing company press releases
• Reporting "the facts" for a news publication
• Etc.

All these jobs are just different versions of manual data retrieval & synopsis.

Going to be replaced by AI.

~

5) Your creative decision-making is arbitrary.

Ever sit in a meeting where 8 people debate whether the logo should be dark blue or light blue?

0 value. (No unique frameworks here.)

AI replaces baseline subjectivity because it can generate infinite options to choose from.

~

6) You get paid to create commodity projects.

• Decks
• Proposals
• Holiday emails
• Website mockups
• Etc.

Even before AI, all these "digital assets" were being turned into beautiful, no-design-required templates.

With AI? Game over.

1-Click. Generate. Download.

~

7) Your content/POV is easily substitutable. 

It doesn't matter if you think you're "talented."

If you write, design, build, or create things people can easily find elsewhere... your economic value is lower than you think.

Being King of your Category should be the goal.

~

10 years ago, everyone thought the physical laborers (plumbers, electricians, etc.) would be first to get "automated away."

Turns out, that's wrong.

It's the digital "do-ers" that will be first to go.

~

Thriving in The Age of AI is going to be all about leverage.

Either compete with humans & technology over the "doing."

Or ascend, create new & unique frameworks, and get paid to "think" (and let AI "do the doing").

outliers.hook: This is Jeff Bezos’s favorite book.
outliers.text:
This is Jeff Bezos’s favorite book. 

He rereads it every year—and he's been doing this for 25 years straight.

Here are 7 lessons from "The Remains of the Day" that helped him build his $200B+ Amazon empire:

In 1987, Bezos found "The Remains of the Day" by Kazuo Ishiguro.

The book follows Stevens, an English butler who sacrificed everything for "professional dignity."

But in his final years, he realizes a devastating truth:

• His dignity was a prison
• He never lived for himself
• His whole life was spent in fear

This revelation shook Bezos to his core.

At 30, he was the youngest Senior VP at D.E. Shaw:

• Making $1M/year
• On track to be partner
• Living the Wall Street dream

That's when Bezos noticed internet usage growing 2,300%/year.

He faced a choice:

1. Stay in his golden handcuffs 
2. Or risk everything on the internet

The butler's story of regret haunted his decision.

This led Bezos to create his famous "Regret Minimization Framework":

When faced with a decision, imagine yourself at 80, looking back at life.

Ask yourself, "Would you regret this decision?"

This insight became the 7 principles that would transform Amazon:

1. Make decisions through the lens of your 80-year-old self.

Most optimize for 2-3 years ahead. 

But Bezos optimized for 40 years out: 

• AWS took 10 years to profit 
• Prime was mocked for years 
• Kindle was called "Amazon's iPod of reading"

2. Professional dignity is a trap.

Stevens wasted his life maintaining an image. 

But Bezos embraced being misunderstood by leaving Wall Street, ignoring quarterly profits, and reinvesting everything into growth.

3. Move with urgency and intensity.

Stevens realized too late that time was his scarcest resource.

This birthed Amazon's "Day 1" philosophy:

• Make quick decisions 
• High-velocity experimentation 
• Never waste a day

4. Emotion drives the biggest breakthroughs. 

Stevens suppressed his emotions.

But Bezos channels emotion into customer obsession, long-term thinking, and building trust.

5. The "safe path" is often the riskiest. 

Stevens never questioned his role as a butler.

But Bezos questions everything:

• Traditional retail
• Book publishing
• Cloud computing

Growth happens outside your comfort zone.

6. Build a reflection system.

Stevens only reflected at life's end.

But Bezos built reflection into Amazon's DNA with silent reading in meetings, a December book rereading ritual, and 6-page memos, not PowerPoints.

7. Study timeless principles.

Every December, Bezos rereads "The Remains of the Day" to remember:

• Move fast
• Think long-term
• Embrace emotion
• Question everything
• Don't live with regret
• Make time for reflection

If you want to write stories like this for other people, then become a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I made my first $10,000 on the internet.

Here's a free, 5-day crash course to help you get started: https://lnkd.in/ep3kMFAA

outliers.hook: Writers:
outliers.text:
Writers:

Stick these 19 powerful writing tips to your desk:

1. Compress what you're saying into bullets.

If you don't know the bullet points of what you're writing, you don't have clarity in what you're saying.

2. Delete the word “that.”

99% of the time, you don’t need it.

3. Make the last word the most important word in the sentence.

"You knew who the first President was."

Rewrite:

"You knew the name of the first President."

4. Increase engagement by increasing your Rate of Revelation (how quickly you introduce new information to the reader).

5. Prep the page. Write a headline and outline your subheads first. Then fill in the gaps.

6. Don't use semicolons. Most readers don't know what they mean.

7. Make your first sentence a short, strong declarative statement.

8. Write (and edit) in different interfaces—analog & digital.

9. Cut all tiny word chunks (e.g. "It is for," "If it is," etc.)

10. Delete opening "i" phrases (e.g. "I think," "I believe," "I have learned")

11. Match language to the audience (niche audience vs. general audience).

12. "The Tequila Test"

When differentiating your content, make a list of all the things most people say about your topic. Now, don't use anything on that list.

13. The 1/3/1 sequence

Open your essays/articles with the 1/3/1 sequence.

1/3/1 is a cadence that makes it easy for readers to fall into your writing. The first and last sentences read like candy. But the middle paragraph is where the value is.

Just like this point.

14. Ignore word count. Instead, focus on saying what needs to be said.

15. Volume wins. If you want to be a successful writer, publish a lot.

16. Redraft from zero. 

Instead of trying to tweak your existing draft, put it aside and rewrite it 100% from scratch.

Trust the best parts of V1 will make their way into V2.

17. Write to change the reader's mind.

18. Delete adverbs

• Excitedly
• Delicately
• Finely
• Carefully

Removing them makes your writing more declarative.

19. Before you start "intentional writing," do some unintentional writing.

That's it!

📌 I used these writing tips to start writing online and make my first $10,000 as a ghostwriter.

Want help landing your first ghostwriting client?

Find out how to write for creators, founders, and top executives here: https://lnkd.in/ep3kMFAA
