outliers.hook: I spent over 100 hours studying legendary coaches & CEOs.
outliers.text:
I spent over 100 hours studying legendary coaches & CEOs.

Turns out, world-class leadership is simple.

The key is to master these 7 principles:

1. Lead From the Front

Most "leaders" are actually bosses, "telling" people what to do.

But this creates a toxic culture:

• Shaky operations
• Resentment
• Poor results

Instead, great leaders dictate the tempo.

They lead by example—bringing more volume & intensity than anyone else.

This forces their team to rise to meet that level of energy (or get left behind).

The result?

Tremendous output.

Which brings us to the 2nd principle:

2. Preach the Score Takes Care of Itself

Poor leaders fixate on outcomes.

• The stock price
• The sales number
• The user milestone

But great leaders understand:

Outcomes are not a result—they are a byproduct.

So instead of obsessing over short-term metrics, they stress the importance of:

• Inputs
• Details
• Process

They focus on building the system that makes results inevitable.

And the key to building that system?

The 3rd principle:

3. Take Extreme Ownership

Great leaders understand everything that happens in their organization is 100% their responsibility.

• No blaming
• No defending
• No complaining

Instead, they realize everything is their fault—and they act this way:

4. Reinforce Tight Feedback Loops

"Power is the speed at which you can go from idea to execution"—Alex Hormozi

So the most powerful person would be someone who:

• Thinks about something
• It instantly appears in the real world

Most companies have slow feedback loops.

They begin working on something, then "circle back" at the end of the week.

Instead, make the default the end of the day (or hour!)

You'll get 7x more done than everyone else (at least).

To do this, you need the 5th principle:

5. Deliver Radical Candor

Poor communication kills culture.

And most organizations communicate with either:

1. No sincerity & tons of gossip
2. Brutal honesty & complete lack of empathy

Both are recipes for long-term disaster.

Here's what to do instead:

Kim Scott defines Radical Candor as striking the balance between:

• Caring personally
• Challenging directly

The leader who communicates well strikes the perfect mix of:

• Honesty
• Empathy
• Directness

Develop this with our 6th principle:

6. Overcommunicate.

Most "leaders" expect their team to be mind readers. 

• Vague instructions
• Unclear decision-making rules

Then, like clockwork, something is done incorrectly.

From there, this happens:

The "leader":

1. Blames the other person
2. Complains about their incompetence

Lack of Extreme Ownership & Radical Candor.

Then the vicious cycle continues.

Instead, communicate expectations clearly, concisely, & repeatedly.

Otherwise, our last principle is impossible:

7. Expect Competitive Greatness

Combining the first 6 principles create a culture of:

• Positive-sum collaboration
• Efficient communication
• Operational excellence
• Rapid iteration loops

The result?

Winning.

outliers.hook: In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff titled "How To Write."
outliers.text:
In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff titled "How To Write."

Here's a breakdown of the memo (a masterclass in effective writing):

The memo starts with a clear why.

"The better you write, the higher you will go in Ogilvy & Mather. People who think well, write well. Good writing is not a natural gift. You have to learn to write well."

Replace "Ogilvy & Mather" with any company and this holds true.

1/ Read the Roman-Raphelson book on writing. Read it 3 times.

Every company on Earth would be a better place if this book was required reading.

If you are still sending emails with Walls of Text, order this.

2/ Write the way you talk. Naturally.

"Finding your writing voice" is a waste of time.

You already have your voice—the one you use every day.

Here's ow to start using it in your writing:

• Choose a topic

• Record yourself talking about it

Then, transcribe it and start there.

3/ Use short words, short sentences, and short paragraphs.

This one takes practice.

So read everything aloud before you publish it.

When you find yourself getting caught up, it's a sign you need to simplify.

4/ Never use jargon words like "reconceptualizes, demassification, attitudinally, judgementally."

When you see someone using jargon, they're hiding their lack of understanding.

An easy solution: pretend you are writing to an 8th grader.

5/ Never write more than two pages on any subject.

99% of books should be blog posts.

And 99% of blog posts should be tweets.

I would preface this by saying: never *publish* more than two pages on any subject.

If it can't fit in two pages, it should be simpler.

6/ Check your quotations.

This one is simple enough. Misquotes are unforced errors.

7/ Never send a letter or memo on the day you write it. Read it aloud the next morning, then edit it.

This is the number one piece of writing advice I give people.

If you are publishing something important, always, always, give it room to breathe.

And always read it aloud.

8/ If it is something important, get a colleague to improve it.

This pairs nicely with point number 7. If it's something really important, write it, give it a day, edit it, then send it to a colleague.

9/ Before you send your letter or memo, make sure it is crystal clear what you want the recipient to do.

Put yourself in the reader's shoes and identify exactly the next step they should take after reading.

Then, articulate those steps for them.

10/ If you want ACTION, don't write. Go tell the guy what you want.

Last and most importantly, writing is never a replacement for a targeted conversation.

Most messages should be conversations, especially ones that require action.

Boom that's it!
-

outliers.hook: 6 weeks ago, I quit my full-time job.
outliers.text:
6 weeks ago, I quit my full-time job.

And on that first day, I bursted awake with a strong feeling of freedom & excitement.

"Woah – I can do literally anything I want today."

But as someone who prefers routine & structure, this freedom & excitement soon melted into discomfort. (Turns out freedom isn't necessarily liberating!)

I spent the first few days trying to make progress in 100 different directions. I was trying to do everything (and as a result, I didn't do anything).

Then, I stumbled across a little quote inside Derek Sivers' latest book How To Live – and since reading it, I can't stop repeating it.

"You can have anything you want, but not everything."

Right away, I recognized how this applied in every area of my life (and was the exact saying I needed to internalize).

• Health. You can eat anything you want, but not everything.
• Writing. You can write anything you want, but not everything.
• Travel. You can go anywhere you want, but not everywhere.
• Business. Your business can solve any problem it wants, but not every problem.
• Relationships. You can be friends with anyone you want, but not everyone.

Now, anytime I feel like I'm spinning my wheels or pulling myself in a dozen different directions, I pause and repeat these 9 words.

This cures my overwhelm by forcing me to eliminate choices & excess decisions.

But, reminding myself I can have anything gives me the confidence & excitement to go and pursue it – which is the whole point of having personal freedom in the first place!

outliers.hook: This family runs one of the world's most successful companies—but no one talks about them.
outliers.text:
This family runs one of the world's most successful companies—but no one talks about them.

With $17.7 billion turnover in 2023, Hershey, Cadbury, and Lindt envy their success.

Here's the full story behind Ferrero Rocher—the most powerful chocolate brand in the world:

It started in a small Italian bakery in 1946.

Pietro Ferrero, a master pastry chef in Alba, Italy, had an obsession: 

Finding the perfect balance between cocoa and hazelnuts.

The breakthrough came during wartime:

Chocolate was scarce and expensive so Pietro crafted 'Giandujot'—a hazelnut chocolate paste using local ingredients.

It was an instant regional success.

But tragedy struck early:

• Pietro died at just 50
• Left the business to his brother Giovanni & son Michele
• Then Giovanni passed too, so Michele stepped up to lead

Michele's first innovation transformed the industry when he introduced 'SuperCrema'—a smoother, spreadable version of Giandujot.

He turned premium chocolate into an everyday pleasure by pricing it for the average Italian family.

His marketing was revolutionary:

• Branded trucks touring Italy with free samples
• Strategic factory placement in growing markets
• Bold expansion into post-war Germany

The company began its global ascent.

In 1964, Michele rebranded SuperCrema as 'Nutella,' blending 'nut' with the Italian suffix '-ella.'

With a refined, secret recipe, it became one of the industry's most closely guarded formulas.

His product strategy was brilliant:

• Nutella for everyday consumers 
• Kinder (launched 1968) for children
• Ferrero Rocher (1982) for luxury markets

Each brand has its own distinct identity and market.

The launch of Ferrero Rocher in 1982 was particularly inspired and influenced by Michele's visits to Lourdes, France.

The multi-layered creation became synonymous with premium gifting: 

• A wafer shell
• Whole hazelnut center
• Signature gold wrapper

But Michele's true legacy was building a complete business ecosystem:

• Healthcare facilities
• The Ferrero Foundation
• Worker housing programs
• Community infrastructure
• Employee education programs

This created remarkable stability with deep community ties, high employee retention, and a multi-generational workforce.

Then, in 2018 Michele acquired Nestlé's U.S. candy business for $2.8B. 

They became:

• An industry leader 
• A multi-brand powerhouse 
• A major hazelnut buyer globally

What I loved most is how success isn't just about product quality.

It's about building an ecosystem of loyalty—from your team to your customers.

This philosophy turned a local Italian paste into a $44.5B empire.

-
📌 I study legendary companies as I build my digital business.

But 5 years ago, I was stuck in a corporate job.

Then I made my first $10k online as a ghostwriter—which ultimately helped me escape Wall Street.

outliers.hook: This weekend I kickstarted my "Yearly Review."
outliers.text:
This weekend I kickstarted my "Yearly Review."

 I begin with a simple exercise to prime my brain into a state of reflection.                                                                             

It's called the "Proof of Growth" prompt—here's how it works:

The goal of my Yearly Review is simple:

• Recap the past year
• Extract lessons & realizations
• Set my intentions for the year ahead

So to get the process going, I set the frame to give me tangible evidence of where I've progressed and where I stayed the same.

I start my listing out the 6 major areas of my life:

1. Health
2. Wealth
3. Relationships
4. Experiences
5. Operations
6. Business

Then, I dig into each one of them by brain dumping where I was a year ago versus where I am now.

Here's a list of examples indicators you can compare & contrast:

(I'll take a look at each of these and confront the truth about my growth for each of them.)

Health

• Sleep
• Weight
• Strength
• Flexibility
• Cardio ability
• Combat training
• Nutritional habits
• Meditation practice

Wealth

• Income
• Spending
• Net worth
• Investment allocation
• Attitude towards money

Relationships

• Family ties
• Close friends
• Romantic situation
• Casual acquaintances
• Charisma & confidence

Experiences

• Trips taken
• Number of flights
• Sporting events attended
• Number of nice restaurants
• Current apartment / housing situation

Business

• Team size
• Current job
• Audience size
• Business revenue
• Product offerings
• Level of excitement
• Meeting cadences

Operations

• Journaling habits
• Deep work habits
• Average screen time
• Productivity systems
• Core values & beliefs 

Now, this is just the beginning of the review. 

But right away, you will start to notice areas you've excelled and areas you've either regressed or stayed the same.

From here, you can start to look for patterns on what propelled you forward and what held you back.

And in the next part of the review, I'll show you how to process that information using an "80:20 life audit."

Lastly, one personal note on this process:

If you're serious about personal progress, you should be in a completely different spot in every one of these areas than you were a year ago.

I do this every 6 months because that's my goal—I'm 27 years old and in a period of accelerated personal growth. My life today looks dramatically different than it did 6 months ago, let alone a year.

So, use this reflection to confront the brutal truth of your situation & performance during the last year.

• Are you in a different spot?
• Or are you in the exact same spot?
• Or worse, have you moved backward?

Having that awareness will set the foundation for the year ahead.

Good luck ✊✊✊

outliers.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
outliers.text:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

(I've done this every month for 5 years)

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting.

And before they know it, it's too late to change.

It's easy to get caught up in the day-to-day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things you will look back on with pride at the end of the year:

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, we focus on the negative. But this question celebrates all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

Now, you ask a more important question:

2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

By constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page into 2 columns & brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

Identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive and negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to double down on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to stop doing entirely that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

Boom, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things.

Then, at the end of the year, you collect all these reviews to distill the year.

📌 These reflection prompts helped me start writing online & make my first $10k as a ghostwriter.

Ghostwriting is how I saw a world beyond Wall Street so I could ultimately quit my 9 to 5.

Want to get paid to write & work with industry leaders?

Get started with this free, 5-day email course: https://lnkd.in/eBDNhqsi

outliers.textPreview:
The highest-paid copywriter in history: Gary Halbert. 

His legendary sales letters generated over $1,000,000,000 in revenue.

And in one of his very last letters, he shared 12 time-tested strategies for writing better copy, faster.

Here's a breakdown of each one:

outliers.hook: In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff.
outliers.text:
In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff.

Inside was a writing masterclass:

The memo is titled "How To Write" and it starts with a clear why:

"The better you write, the higher you will go in Ogilvy & Mather. People who think well, write well. Good writing is not a natural gift. You have to learn to write well."

Replace "Ogilvy & Mather" with any company and this holds true.

Here's a breakdown of the 10 points:

1. Read the Roman-Raphelson book on writing. Read it 3 times.

Every company on Earth would be a better place if this book was required reading.

If you are still sending emails with Walls of Text, order this.

2. Write the way you talk. Naturally.

"Finding your writing voice" is a waste of time.

You already have your voice—the one you use every day.

Here's ow to start using it in your writing:

• Choose a topic
• Record yourself talking about it

Then, transcribe it and start there.

3. Use short words, short sentences, and short paragraphs.

This one takes practice.

So read everything aloud before you publish it.

When you find yourself getting caught up, it's a sign you need to simplify.

4. Never use jargon words like "reconceptualizes, demassification, attitudinally, judgementally."

When you see someone using jargon, they're hiding their lack of understanding.

An easy solution: pretend you are writing to an 8th grader.

5. Never write more than two pages on any subject.

99% of books should be blog posts.

And 99% of blog posts should be tweets.

I would preface this by saying: never *publish* more than two pages on any subject.

If it can't fit in two pages, it should be simpler.

6. Check your quotations.

This one is simple enough. Misquotes are unforced errors.

7. Never send a letter or memo on the day you write it. Read it aloud the next morning, then edit it.

This is the number one piece of writing advice I give people.

If you are publishing something important, always, always, give it room to breathe.

And always read it aloud.

8. If it is something important, get a colleague to improve it.

This pairs nicely with point number 7. If it's something really important, write it, give it a day, edit it, then send it to a colleague.

9. Before you send your letter or memo, make sure it is crystal clear what you want the recipient to do.

Put yourself in the reader's shoes and identify exactly the next step they should take after reading.

Then, articulate those steps for them.

10. If you want ACTION, don't write. Go tell the guy what you want.

Last and most importantly, writing is never a replacement for a targeted conversation.

Most messages should be conversations, especially ones that require action.

Boom that's it!

-
📌 Becoming a writer changed my life.

Writing on social media every day for 30 days helped me build a 7-figure writing business.

Want to get started?

Here's a free, 5-day email course: https://lnkd.in/eQiV9ae7

outliers.hook: I've been lifting weights for 14 years.
outliers.text:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

I've been lifting weights for 14 years.

But 8 weeks ago, I made a life-changing $21 purchase.

1-pound weight plates.

And this *tiny*  helped me make more progress in 2 months than I had in 2 years.

Here's the quick story (and 1 reflection question that will change your life):

--
So I said I've been lifting weights for 14 years – which is half true.

These 14 years has been riddled with ups & downs.

Seasons of beaming motivation and seasons of burnout.

(Chances are you've felt this too)

And after some reflection, I traced these waves back to 1 feeling:

--

The difference between motivation & burnout?

Clear evidence of progress.

When I saw myself improving, I was motivated to keep going.

If weeks went by without any gains, I would burn out.

And I realized, current gym setups encourage this cycle.

--

Here's why:

At most gyms, the smallest jump you can make is 5 pounds.

So if one of your lifts is 100 pounds, that's a 5% jump!

And if the goal is to make weekly progress, you simply can't sustain 5% jumps every week.

So here's where things go wrong:

--

With this setup, you have to do the same weight for weeks in a row.

During this time, you're getting stronger – but your progress is invisible.

You have no feedback loop – so it can feel like you're spinning your wheels.

But here's where the 1-pound plates changed everything:

--

Over the last 8 weeks, I've made progress *every week*

Because instead of jumping 5% per week, these tiny plates let me jump 1%.

And these weekly, 1% micro-gains have me *addicted* to the process – because I see *actual* improvement every week.

Here's why that's important:

--

If I've learned anything in the last 15 years, it's all good things come from compounding.

And compounding comes from doing:

• The absolute basics
• With monotonous consistency
• For a long period of time

Said another way:

• Simplicity
• Consistency
• Patience

--

And with these pillars in mind, I set goals with 2 steps:

1. Pick an outcome I want to achieve

2. Change the goal from "I want to achieve X" to "I want to enjoy every second on the journey to achieving X."

And here's the key to enjoying every second on the journey:

--

95% of success is learning to cherish the journey on the path of monotonous consistency.

And to fall in love with this path, we need to see progress along the way.

Because more tangible progress we see:

• The more momentum we gain
• The easier it becomes to keep going

Now, these last 8 week gave me 1 big realization.

This idea goes far beyond lifting weights.

In any area you're looking to progress, the key is tiny, continuous, tangible progress over the long term.

Said another way, you need to find your micro plates.

So here's the reflection question for you:

• What are you trying to progress with right now?
• How can you find a way to measure tiny progress consistently?

Whether it's 1% per day, per week, or month, or per year!

Find a way to celebrate your progress.

outliers.hook: In my 20s, I built a $3,500,000 writing agency.
outliers.text:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

In my 20s, I built a $3,500,000 writing agency.

The entire business was built on ONE skill (steal this):

Cold outreach.

• No ads
• No huge audience
• No fancy website or funnel

80% of the 300+ clients I worked with came from cold outreach.

And I wrote for:

• Olympic athletes
• Silicon Valley founders
• Grammy award-winning musicians 

Learning how to prospect was 100x more valuable than actually being able to write!

And after teaching 1,500+ writers this system inside Premium Ghostwriting Academy...

I've just packaged everything up into The Writer Prospecting Blueprint.

Why?

Because I see too many writers (copywriters, ghostwriters, content writers, freelance writers) struggling to land high-ticket deals.

So I want to get this into as many hands as possible.

Inside you'll find:

• 4 ways to land clients (and why 3 of them waste your time)

• My complete 3-step outreach system from DM to sale

• Real examples of terrible vs excellent outreach messages

• The exact bottleneck analysis system to fix your outreach

• Scripts for booking calls and closing deals

And 3 videos breaking down every single framework.

Want access?

Comment "outreach" and I'll send it across (for free!)

And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "outreach" and I'll send it to you.

outliers.hook: Writing online changed my life.
outliers.text:
Writing online changed my life.

This simple habit has helped me:

• Make $10,000 as a ghostwriter
• Quit my corporate job on Wall Street
• And build an 8-figure digital business

It's why I'm convinced writing on the internet is the single most effective habit for personal growth and business building.

So, I'm on a mission to help 1,000,000 people start writing online.

To help you get started, I'm giving away 2 of my books for FREE:

1. The 5 Pillars of Digital Writing
2. The 22 Laws of Digital Writing

The frameworks packed inside these books are everything you need to start writing online.

They will help you:

• Create a daily writing habit
• Generate endless writing ideas
• Write irresistible hooks and headlines

And tons more!

And as an extra bonus, I'm including Nicolas Cole 🚢👻's The Art & Business of Ghostwriting.

Why?

Because ghostwriting is how I made my first $10,000 writing online.

And I want to help you use your Digital Writing skills to monetize as a ghostwriter.

Want access?

Like this post & comment "books"

I'll send them to you over DM.

(And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "books" and I'll DM them to you.)

outliers.hook: "Sorry, we're out of cups."
outliers.text:
"Sorry, we're out of cups."

This was my interaction at Starbucks today.

And it left me with an important life & business reminder:

"Like, coffee cups?" I asked back to the barista.

"Yeah, we forgot to order more last week."

As I walked out without my caffeine fix, I couldn't help but think to myself:

How could Starbucks possibly run out of coffee cups?

Behind coffee *beans* the cups have to be the second most important thing on the supplies list, no?

Now, I wasn't totally surprised, because this Starbucks is usually a bit all over the place.

• Long lines
• Messy store
• Tons of employee turnover

But despite this, this morning they had a beautiful and well-organized "fall flavor" promotion.

Pumpkin spice this. Cinnamon latte that. Tons of big balloons and colors. 

Hours of prep work from the entire staff to turn the entire place into an orange Fall Frenzy.  

However, this grand promotion fell flat—but not for lack of excitement or interest from customers.

But because there were no cups—which meant no sales!

And that's what reminded me the importance of focusing on the absolute basics.

The shiny object of all of the balloons and decor.

The big promotion of all the pumpkin flavors.

All of this clearly took their attention away from the completion of the basics—ordering supplies. 

And without this strong foundation of the basics, all of the fancy add-ons fail.

And on my uncaffeinated, somber walk back to my apartment, I realized the importance of this reminder. 

Fitness, business, relationships—all of them flourish on top of a rock-solid, basic foundation. 

In fitness, you need to move more and eat less before worrying about advanced lifting techniques.

In business, you need to sell something people want before worrying about complex marketing funnels.

In relationships, you need to listen to other people and practice basic empathy before worrying about complex psychology. 

• Do the basics
• Do them every day
• Do them longer than the average person is willing

The rest takes care of itself.

(But it sure helps to be caffeinated...!)

I asked 430,000 people for their favorite book of 2025.

These 12 books showed up the most often:

(So they must be worth reading over the holidays!)

1. Grit by Angela Duckworth

2. The Art of Spending Money by Morgan Housel 

https://a.co/d/0IBjluN

3. 5 Types Of Wealth by Sahil Bloom

https://a.co/d/8gvw7iR

4. Demon Copperhead by Barbara Kingsolver

https://a.co/d/isJmVOK

Follow me so you know exactly when it drops!

outliers.hook: At 24, I made my first $10,000 writing on the internet.
outliers.text:
At 24, I made my first $10,000 writing on the internet.

The crazy part?

I landed my first writing client by mistake.

But I'd actually just stumbled across an insanely lucrative business anyone can start:

Ghostwriting.

Everyone should have writing clients who pay $5,000+ for a project.

(This is ultimately how I quit my job on Wall Street so I could travel and work from anywhere in the world.)

But most people are stuck figuring out:

• What service to sell
• Which clients to target
• How to charge premium rates

So, I put together the Writer Positioning Playbook to help you land high-paying clients in ANY niche.

Inside, you'll find:

• The 1 simple shift to 10x your rates overnight
• A 3-step framework to upgrade your Positioning
• An AI prompt to find 5 high-paying clients for YOUR specific background

Plus, you'll see real examples from a $3M ghostwriting agency.

Want access?

Comment "playbook" and I'll send it across (for free).

And if we're not connected, send me a connection request with the word "playbook" and I'll DM it to you.

outliers.hook: The simple habit I used to lose 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and build a 7-figure writing business:
outliers.text:
The simple habit I used to lose 100 pounds, quit my 9 to 5, and build a 7-figure writing business:

Journaling.

Over the last 7 years, I have:

• Journaled every day
• Tried over 1,000 questions
• Tested every app, pen, & notebook

I can point every good thing in my life back to this simple, daily practice.

With it, I have:

• Started writing online
• Made my first $10,000 on the internet as a ghostwriter
• Quit my full-time job on Wall Street to go all in on entrepreneurship

And over the last few years, I've narrowed down these 1,000 questions to 12 journal prompts I use on a daily, weekly, and monthly basis.

And now I've put them together in my Ultimate Guide To Journaling.

Inside, you'll find:

• 12 tried-and-test journal prompts
• Prompts to get you unstuck and motivated
• 2 simple prompts I use to generate 100s of content ideas

And tons more!

Want access?

✅ Like this post
✅ Comment "JOURNAL"
✅ And send me a connection request if you haven’t yet (so I can DM you)

outliers.hook: Your single greatest 21st-century superpower:
outliers.text:
Dickie Bush 🚢 reposted this

Your single greatest 21st-century superpower:

Pursuing the inconvenient truth over the comforting lie.

So here are 19 inconvenient truths I don't want to forget:

--

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

--

2. No one on Earth thinks about you as much as you think they do.

--

3. Successful people are neither smarter nor more creative than you. They are simply executing better.

--

4. Success comes from doing somewhat-monotonous things every day for years – but no one wants to hear that.

--

5. You know exactly what you should be doing, so stop chasing shiny objects. Instead, figure out why you aren't doing what you know you should be.

--

6. You can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation.

--

7. If you spent half as many hours taking action as you did thinking about taking action, you would be in a better spot.

--

8. 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity.

--

9. If you are reading productivity blogs, you are working on the wrong project.

--

10. You can't deposit the number of books you've read or followers you have in a bank account.

--

11. You said you would start months ago – the perfect time will never come. 

--

12. You have three finite resources: time, energy, and attention.Every time you waste them, they're gone forever.

--

13. You can complain about the game or you can learn the rules.

--

14. The market rewards not the best product, but the product who best positions themselves as the obvious solution.

--

15. If you're afraid to publish things online, you have an ego problem.

--

16. The people you look up to are not that special and are no more talented than you are.

-- 

17. If you did everything you said you'd do, your results would be 10x better than they are – so why aren't you doing them?

--

18. Being "busy" is a sign you lack leverage and are delegating poorly.

--

19. 99% of people would rather be uncomfortable and unhappy than uncertain.

-- 


That's it. Most people spend their lives avoiding these truths (and end up in a spot they don't want to be because of it).

Start pursuing these truths and watch your results accelerate overnight.

outliers.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
outliers.text:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting.

And before they know it, years have gone by and it's too late to change.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

The goal of The Monthly Review:

Block 60 minutes to slowly process the month.

It's easy to get caught up in the day to day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

And after trying 1000+ reflection questions, I chose each question for a specific reason.

Let's start with the first:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things that you will look back on with pride & fond memories at the end of the year.

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, our focus is on the negative. But this questions helps us celebrate all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

From there, you ask a more important question:

---
2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—because something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

And by constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By constantly iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

---
3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page in 2 columns and brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

The goal here is to identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

---
4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive *and* negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to *double down* on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to *stop doing entirely* that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

---
5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a quick list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

---
And just like that, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things throughout the month.

• Wins & milestones
• Realizations & decisions
• Things going well & things to improve

Then, at the end of the year, you collect these monthly reviews and distill the entire year.

outliers.hook: I wasted years as a horrible writer.
outliers.text:
I wasted years as a horrible writer.

Then I heard Naval Ravikant mention a 250-word essay full of the best writing mental models he swears by.

On The Tim Ferriss Show, Naval said this is his go-to resource for writing is:

"The Day You Became A Better Writer" by Scott Adams.

Here are the 4 most important mental models:

1. Keep it simple.

That's the #1 rule of concise and persuasive writing, according to Adams. 

• Remove adverbs
• Use shorter words
• Instead of "very happy" say "happy"

This is similar to Hemingway's rules for writing.

2. Hook the reader from the very first sentence.

Curiosity is the crack cocaine of the writing world.

Hook your readers, and they'll follow you anywhere.

• Study David Ogilvy: https://lnkd.in/eyB4Ahcq

• And use these 6 proven sentence openers: https://lnkd.in/eqrAEmT9

3. Use short sentences.

"Readers aren't as smart as you'd think."

Just one thought per sentence. Readers process info better when it's bite-sized and easily digestible.

Avoid:

 • Jargon
 • Flowery language
 • Complex sentences

Be clear, and you'll be clever.

4. Subject before action.

"The boy hit the ball" not "The ball was hit by the boy."

It's easier to comprehend the first sentence. So, make sure you're always putting the subject of the sentence before the action of the sentence.

Naval Ravikant swears by Scott Adams' writing tips. 

But for me, it's Ravikant's ideas that make his writing truly irresistible.

According to Naval, this is why writing is such a superpower:

"Ideas are the new oil."  

Modern society requires ideas to disrupt ways of thinking. Just as oil needs to be extracted and refined to be useful, raw ideas need molding. 

And writing is a powerful tool to gain clarity and shape ideas.

That's it!

Here are 4 writing mental models for great writing by Scott Adams:

1. Simplicity wins
2. Hook them early
3. Keep sentences short
4. Subject before action

-
📌 I've been writing online since 2020.

But most people don't know that I made my first $10,000 on the internet as a ghostwriter—which ultimately led me to escape the Wall Street rat race.

Here's a 5-day crash course on how to get started: https://lnkd.in/ep3kMFAA

outliers.hook: Want to start writing but can't overcome the blank page?
outliers.text:
Want to start writing but can't overcome the blank page?

Use these 21 templates for the next 21 days (to make writing every day for the first 3 weeks of 2024 effortless):

Before we start, a quick breakdown.

Everything you write falls into 4 buckets:

• Actionable (here's how)
• Analytical (here's what)
• Aspirational (yes, you can)
• Anthropological (here's why)

Every 1 of these 21 ideas falls into 1 of these buckets.

Let's dive in:

These are Actionable:

1. Share 1 underrated tip for solving a certain problem.

2. Share 1 framework that helps you get something done in less time or with less stress.

3. Share the 1 piece of advice to you would give to a beginner in your field.

4. Share 1 software tool that helps you the most (that most people know nothing about).

5. Share 1 helpful website (that you think is rarely used).

6. Share a step-by-step guide for accomplishing 1 specific task.

7. Share the 1 book that most helped you in learning about your topic.

8. Share the 1 podcast you listen to religiously – and what others will learn from it.

9. Share the 1 Twitter thread you've bookmarked and reread multiple times – and what you learned from it.

10. Share the 1 blog post that once you read, permanently changed the way you see the world.

Now, let's dig into Aspirational:

11. Share 1 mistake you made as a beginner – and what you learned from it.

12. Share the single most valuable thing you learned from a mentor.

13. Share your personal story – where you used to be, where you are now, and how you got here.

14. Share 1 piece of advice to your 5-years-ago self.

15. Share 1 common trait of the people you look up to.

16. Share 1 quote you live by – where you found it, and how it impacts you.

17. Share your personal playbook – how you achieved some result and the steps you took to get there.

Finally, onto Anthropological:

18. Share 1 thing you believe that everyone else doesn’t – and why you’re correct

19. Share 1 life-changing realization you made in your 20s that accelerated your success

20. Share 1 bad habit you broke in the last 12 months - and why it was holding you back

21. Share 1 common trait people who fail in your industry have—and how the reader can avoid it

Boom!

Now you have content ideas for the rest of the year and into 2024.

outliers.hook: The *ONLY* shortcut in life:
outliers.text:
The *ONLY* shortcut in life:

Learning from those ahead of you on a path you want to take.

So to accelerate your career, ask your mentors these 7 questions:

1. What is something most people think is important that I can skip entirely?

As a beginner, everything looks important.

But as you progress, you realize most things aren't.

So instead of wasting your effort in dozens of directions, ask this to figure out what to avoid.

---

2. What's something important to your daily routine you wish you started sooner?

The people ahead of you got to where they are with simple daily actions compounded over time.

So figure out what those actions are & build them into your days.

Then, stick to them for years.

--

3. What channels led to the building of your highest-quality relationships?

It's cliché, but you are the average of the 5 people you surround yourself with.

But finding the right people isn't easy—unless you know where to look.

So use this question to get some directions.

--


4. What is something you did differently than your peers 5 years ago that led to pushback, but served you in the long run?

If you do what everyone else does, you can expect to achieve what everyone else achieves (mediocrity).

Use this question to identify behaviors to shed.

--

5. What can I expect to struggle with along the way?

Growth is having problems today you would have begged to have had years ago.

So if you know which struggles are coming, you can:

• Prepare for them
• Conquer them
• Continue to grow

Which means unlocking new problems.

---

6. What is something you believed 5 years ago you had to "unlearn" to take the next step?

Progress comes from the constant cycle of:

• Identifying a bottleneck
• Removing that bottleneck

And most of the time, that bottleneck is a behavior or belief you have to "unlearn."

--

7. What's something you didn't pay enough attention to early on, then had to learn the hard way?

Early on, you're flying blind.

You don't even know what you don't know.

So the faster you can uncover these blind spots, the faster you can improve.

--

Boom—that's it.

This is your toolkit to ask high-quality questions when given the chance to talk to a mentor.

Don't waste their time "jumping on a quick call to pick their brain."


Instead, ask targeted questions they will be interested in thinking about.

Instant progress.

If you enjoyed this, I'd appreciate if you liked & reposted it so the algorithm shows it to more people ✊

Also, which one was most useful? 

Hit reply with your favorite question to let me know.

14. If you are reading productivity books, you are working on the wrong project.

15. You can't deposit the number of books you've read or followers you have in a bank account.

16. You have three finite resources: time, energy, and attention.

Every time you waste them, they're gone forever.

17. The people you look up to are not that special and are no more talented than you are.

18. If you're afraid to publish things online, you have an ego problem.

19. Being "busy" is a sign you lack leverage and are delegating poorly.
---
📌 If you enjoyed this, you'll love my newsletter.

In it, I share no-fluff frameworks for making personal progress: dickiesdigest.com

outliers.hook: The most powerful reflection you can do:
outliers.text:
The most powerful reflection you can do:

The Monthly Review.

Most people go their *entire* life without reflecting.

And before they know it, years have gone by and it's too late to change.

So don't let this happen—grab a pen, crack open a notebook, and answer these 5 questions:

The goal of the monthly review:

Block 60 minutes to slowly process the month.

It's easy to get caught up in the day to day & lose sight of the bigger picture.

And after trying 1000+ reflection questions, I chose each question for a specific reason.

Let's start with the first:

1. What were my biggest wins & milestones?

These are things that you will look back on with pride & fond memories at the end of the year.

• Health wins
• Business wins
• New relationships
• Goals accomplished
• Fun memories & events

Recapping wins & milestones kicks the review off on a positive note.

So often, our focus is on the negative. But this questions helps us celebrate all the things going well! And it also creates a "ledger" of wins from the year.

From there, you ask a more important question:

---
2. What were my biggest realizations?

I used to call this my "biggest losses."

But I reframed this—because something is either a win, or it teaches you about the world.

And by constantly collecting these realizations, you are always learning.

Most of my realizations are about my:

• Health
• Beliefs
• Business
• Lack of skills
• Relationships

By constantly iterating and distilling these lessons, you will level up month after month after month.

From here, you go from looking backward to looking at the present:

---
3. What areas am I most satisfied? Least satisfied?

Split the page in 2 columns and brain dump bullet points of everything that comes to mind.

The goal here is to identify in the present moment what's working and what's not working.

From there, you ask another question:

---
4. Based on these areas, what am I going to do more of? Less of?

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

20% of your:

• Habits
• Beliefs
• Friends
• Actions

Are leading to 80% of both your positive *and* negative results.

Now you should have 2 lists:

• Things to *double down* on that are bringing you the positive results
• Things to *stop doing entirely* that are bringing you the negative results

Put those lists somewhere you can see them every morning (for me, that's on my bathroom mirror).

Now, it's time to think about the future:

---
5. What am I thinking about for the month ahead?

This is a quick list of things you're:

• Excited about
• Thinking about
• Uncertain about

This turns into a letter to your future self you can read at the end of the month.

---
And just like that, the review is complete!

The beauty of this process is you can constantly capture these things throughout the month.

• Wins & milestones
• Realizations & decisions
• Things going well & things to improve

Then, at the end of the year, you collect these monthly reviews and distill the entire year.

outliers.hook: The single most powerful habit for personal growth:
outliers.text:
The single most powerful habit for personal growth:

Journaling.

Over the past 5 years, I've journaled every single morning.

And I always return to these 5 prompts:

Prompt 1: The Morning Kickstart

No one has time to write a novel every morning—so here's what I do instead:

I start with 5 questions: 

• What's 1 thing I'm grateful for? 
• What's 1 thing I'm excited about? 
• What's 1 virtue I want to exhibit? 
• What's 1 thing I'm avoiding? 
• What's the 1 thing I need to do? 

I set a 5-minute timer and each question gets 1 sentence, max.

This prompt covers:

• Present moment gratitude
• Future excitement for the day ahead
• Reminders about the type of person I'm trying to become (and avoid becoming)

And it ends with a reminder of the 1 most important thing I need to today.

Prompt 2: The Evening Shutdown

Just like the Morning Kickstart, this one has to be quick.

So I set a 5-minute timer and answer:

• What were my biggest wins of the day?
• Did I have any major realizations?
• What's on the agenda for tomorrow?

By recapping my biggest wins & realizations, I keep a ledger of:

• The good things happening in my life
• The things I'm learning about myself & the world

And by brain dumping bullets on the day ahead, I let my subconscious work overnight.

Prompt 3: The Bottleneck Analysis

This prompt has accelerated my personal growth.

And it has 2 questions:

1. What's the biggest bottleneck to achieving my next goal?
2. Why aren't I working on it today?

The truth is, you know exactly what you should be doing.

So—stop looking for new things and do the things you know you should be doing.

Prompt 4: The 80/20 Life Audit

First start with 2 lists:

• Where am I feeling satisfied?
• Where am I feeling dissatisfied?

Brain dump anything and everything—health, finances, relationships, career, spirituality.

Then, take each list and find the 20% of:

• Places
• Habits
• People
• Beliefs

That are leading to 80% of the positive & negative results.

This is the 80/20 rule in action.

During this reflection, patterns will emerge.

The same people, habits, and beliefs—all leading to your best and worst results.

From there, your goal is to:

• Double down on the 20% positive
• Ruthlessly eliminate the 20% negative

The result?

An instant life upgrade.

Prompt 5: The Compounding Projection

It's cliché, but all successful outcomes come from compounding.

Our success (or failure) is a result of tiny, daily actions compounded over time.

But in the moment, it can be hard to gauge your progress.

So ask yourself these 2 questions:

1. If I repeated every action I took today, every day for a year, where would I end up?
2. Is this the place I want to be?

If you're confident in your current path, this prompt will help you be more patient.

But if you're on the wrong path, it will help you course correct.

Boom, that's it!

These are the 5 prompts I come back to time and time again and they always leave me with a feeling of *elite* clarity.

outliers.hook: In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff titled "How to write."
outliers.text:
In 1982, the legendary marketer David Ogilvy sent a memo to his staff titled "How to write."

Here's a breakdown of the memo (it's a masterclass in effective writing):

The memo starts with a clear *why*.

"The better you write, the higher you will go in Ogilvy & Mather. People who think well, write well. Good writing is not a natural gift. You have to learn to write well."

Replace "Ogilvy & Mather" with any company and this holds true.

1/ Read the Roman-Raphelson book on writing. Read it three times. 

Every company on Earth would be a better place if this book was required reading before email permissions were given.

If you are still sending emails with Walls of Text, order this.

2/ Write the way you talk. Naturally. 

"Finding your writing voice" is a waste of time. 

You already have your voice - the one you use every day.

How to start using it in your writing?

• Choose a topic
• Record yourself talking about it

Then, transcribe it and start there.

3/ Use short words, short sentences, and short paragraphs.

This one takes practice. 

But the easiest way to find when you're being too wordy? 

Read everything aloud before you publish it. 

When you find yourself getting caught up, it's a sign you need to simplify.

4/ Never use jargon words like "reconceptualizes, demassification, attitudinally, judgementally." 

When you see someone using jargon, they're hiding their lack of understanding. 

An easy solution: pretend you are writing to an 8th grader.

5/ Never write more than two pages on any subject. 

99% of books should be blog posts. 

And 99% of blog posts should be tweets. 

I would preface this by saying: never *publish* more than two pages on any subject. 

If it can't fit in two pages, it should be simpler.

6/ Check your quotations.

This one is simple enough. Misquotes are unforced errors.

7/ Never send a letter or memo on the day you write it. Read it aloud the next morning, then edit it.

This is the number one piece of writing advice I give people.

If you are publishing something important, always, always, give it room to breathe.

And always read it aloud.

8/ If it something important, get a colleague to improve it.

This pairs nicely with point number 7. If it's something really important, write it, give it a day, edit it, then send it to a colleague.

9/ Before you send your letter or memo, make sure it is crystal clear what you want the recipient to do.

Put yourself in the reader's shoes and identify exactly the next step they should take after reading.

Then, articulate those steps for them.

10/ If you want ACTION, don't write. Go tell the guy what you want. 

Last and most importantly, writing is never a replacement for a targeted conversation. 

Most messages should be conversations, especially ones that require action.

Boom that's it!

And if you're looking for more tips to upgrade your writing, then check out this free 13,000-word Ultimate Guide To Start Writing Online (link is in the comments).

outliers.hook: Gary Halbert's sales letters generated over $1,000,000,000 in revenue.
outliers.text:
Gary Halbert's sales letters generated over $1,000,000,000 in revenue.

Here's his dead-simple, 7-step writing process:
--

1. Set up a proper writing space

Gary was a big fan of writing anywhere.

• His desk
• Local bar
• Bookstore
• Coffee shop

The location didn't matter.

But he made sure to stick with the same one for long stretches.
The goal was to signal to his brain "it's time to write."
--

2. Set up a writing routine

“I write when I’m inspired, so I see to it I’m inspired every morning at nine o’clock.” ~ Peter De Vries

Time: the first thing you need to write well.

But the best writers don't find time to write. They make time to write.

Gary was big on writing first thing in the morning.

So before bed, he would scribble down notes on four questions:

• What's the big idea here?
• What's my product's biggest benefit?
• What's most exciting about this product?
• What emotion am I trying to create.

Then, he went to bed and let his subconscious do the work for him.
Like clockwork, he would wake up with a clear answer to each question.

--

3. Write. A LOT.

Gary says there's no shortcut. And he's right.

The more you write, and the more often you right, the better you will become.

Because no one has 100 bad pieces of writing.

They either have 10 and quit, or do it long enough to figure it out.
--

4. Write for set time periods.

Gary was big on the Pomodoro technique.

He set a timer for 33:33.

And his rule was simple: he could do anything he wanted during that 33:33.

Except get out of his chair.

And this took the pressure off writing.

Most times he'd stare at the wall for the first few minutes. Sip his coffee. Think about how many other things he'd rather be doing.
But then, someway or another, he'd start writing.

And the timer let him know the torture would end soon.

During his breaks, he would walk around, have a quick snack, and do anything else but write.

And during this time, inspiration always struck.
--
5. Write your first draft fast and furious with no editing.

When writing, rip out your backspace button.

When editing, but it back and rip out everything else.

Every piece of great writing started as a crappy piece of writing.

So just get started.
--

6.Write something easy, first (and give yourself permission to create junk).

For every great sales letter, Gary wrote 20 crappy sales letters.

His reminder: "In this history of written communication, nothing has ever been published that is 100% perfect."
--
In summary:

Gary Halbert's writing system:
1. Write, A LOT
2. Build a writing routine
3. Set up a writing space
4. Write for set time periods
5. Write your first draft without judgement
6. Write something easy, first (and allow yourself to create junk)

Give this simple system a try and watch your writing ability 10x overnight!

---

If you enjoyed this post, here's two things you can do:

1. Follow me here on LinkedIn Dickie Bush 🚢
2. Leave a comment with your biggest takeaway!

outliers.hook: I used to have one crippling addiction:
outliers.text:
I used to have one crippling addiction:

Planning.

My brain loved the cheap dopamine of gathering:

• Books to read
• Tasks to complete
• And videos to watch

But when it came to taking action, the euphoric rush faded.

What ended my addiction?

This story of two men learning to fish:

The first fisherman heads straight to the marina to buy the best fishing boat money can buy.

He specs out a custom, carbon fiber rod.

And he spends time reading up on everything there is to know about fishing:

• Casting techniques
• Baiting techniques
• Reeling techniques

You name it, he's bought the book on it.

And of course, he's watching the fishing channel every night for hours, learning from the best of the best.

A few months later, he finally feels ready to fish.

The next day, he heads out on the lake in his new boat with his fancy rod and wealth of fishing "experience" built up over the last few months.

But six hours in, nothing. Not even a nibble.

What happened?

Turns out, the second fisherman had already sucked the lake dry.

On day one, the second fisherman grabbed a shitty rod, sliced up a hot dog, and went out to the lake.

• The first day, nothing
• Second day, nothing
• Third day, nothing
• One week, nothing

But on the eighth day? His first nibble.

And every day after, he honed his technique.

He found a baiting technique that worked a bit better. Or a new spot with a few more fish.

And after three weeks of iteration, he found the winning combination.

Before long, he was hauling in hundreds of fish every day. To keep up with his daily haul, he hired a small team to help him. And from there, he opened a restaurant with fat margins to sell his fresh fish.

And after that, he opened a fishing school to teach others his craft.

He did all of this while the first fisherman sat around gathering gear.

Sure, the first fisherman felt productive. He was "learning" after all, right?

But there was one difference: feedback.

Every day the second fisherman had an idea of something that might work better. And he went right to the market to validate his assumption. If it worked, his strategy improved. And if it failed, he learned something new.

The moral of this story?

Get to action as quickly as possible.

When you find yourself gathering gear, grab a shitty rod and start fishing.

Find this useful?

Then repost this to share it with more people and help them take action ✊

outliers.hook: In 5 years, my little business has made $15,000,000.
outliers.text:
In 5 years, my little business has made $15,000,000.

It runs on just $8,215/month.

Here are the 7 most powerful no-code tools in my tech stack:

In 2020, I was making $180k at BlackRock.

But I had no:

• Freedom
• Flexibility
• Or growth potential

I took a chance and built a writing education business that today is doing 7-figs a year.

Here are the top 7 no-code tools that made it all possible:

1. ConvertKit ($1,179/mo)

The heart of our operation. Handles all email marketing:

• Automations
• Broadcasts
• CRM

We used this to grow from 0 to 125k subscribers, which scaled perfectly with us.

One of the most important tools in our arsenal.

2. Stripe (2.9% + $.30 per transaction)

By far the best payment system for digital writers.

• $15M+ processed
• Zero issues

It just works. No reason to use anything else.

For beginners, this is essential—start collecting payments from day one.

3. Carrd ($19/yr)

Every landing page we create starts here.

We buy stand-alone domains and host opt-ins that convert like crazy.

• It's fast
• Cheap
• And looks clean

No coding required.

It's what got us to $1M before we even needed more complex solutions.

4. Airtable ($1,134/mo)

The single most important database in our business.

It's our internal CRM and stores data in one central hub.

Every product, lead, and customer lives here.

If ConvertKit is the heart, Airtable is the brain of our operation.

5. Zapier (~$749/mo)

The ultimate glue that holds everything together.

Connects Stripe → Airtable → ConvertKit → Everything else.

• Easy to use
• Automates 100k+ tasks/month
• Replaces at least 5+ full-time assistants

This is how we scale without hiring an army.

6. ChatGPT ($20/mo)

Our go-to AI writing assistant.

• Analysis 
• Research
• Brainstorming

It's my best thought partner that I constantly spar with.

Every team member uses it daily to enhance their writing, not replace it.

7. Substack (Free or 10% of paid subs)

Hosts my personal + Write with AI paid newsletter (~$400k/yr).

The built-in referral engine is a massive growth lever.

For simple newsletters with monetization potential, nothing beats it.

This is the perfect platform for beginners.

That's it!

At scale, seemingly expensive tools become incredibly cheap relative to the revenue they generate.

📌 If I were starting from zero today, I'd use just 3 tools:

• ConvertKit for email
• Carrd for landing pages
• Stripe for payments

Make your first $1. Everything else comes after that.

I made my first $10,000 on the internet as a ghostwriter—and it's what ultimately helped me escape my job on Wall Street.

It's the easiest online business to start (and way faster than building an audience & selling digital products).

Want help landing your first high-paying client?

Get started for free here: https://lnkd.in/erhz6zSW

outliers.hook: A legendary writer you've never heard of:
outliers.text:
A legendary writer you've never heard of:

Henry Miller.

He wrote books so good (and so controversial) the US banned them from publication for 30 years.

In 1930, Henry made a list of 11 Commandments that are a must-read for any aspiring creative:

1. Work on one thing at a time until finished

Henry's biggest superpower: focus. 

If you give something 100% of your attention (versus giving it 90%) - it's not 10% better, it's 10 times better.

2. Start no more new books, add no more new material to "Black Spring."

At the time of writing these rules, Henry was working on his book "Black Spring."

So this was a two-fold reminder:

• Stop chasing shiny objects
• Edit your work with subtraction, not addition

3. Don't be nervous. Work calmly, joyously, recklessly, on what is in hand.

Calmly, joyously, recklessly - it's worth rereading those words.

You can *feel* what it's like to work in a state like that just from reading them.

4. Work according to Program and not according to mood. Stop at the appointed time!

The crux of Henry's Program was two-fold:

• Consistency
• Constraint

He wrote every day - but not all day. 

5. When you can't create, then you can work.

To Henry, "creating" and "working" were two separate things.

Today, these are the meetings, tasks, emails, and other things that gnaw at our precious attention.

Henry did those things when he could no longer create.

6. Cement a little every day, rather than add new fertilizers. 

"Cementing" means to finish something. 

"Fertilizing" is planning, "researching" and preparing, which are really procrastinating.

More cement, less fertilizer.

7. Keep human! See people, go places, drink if you feel like it.

As much as Henry enjoyed the creative writing process, he knew one thing:

The best writing is not a result. It's a byproduct of a life well-lived.

8. Don't be a draught-horse! Work with pleasure only.

Henry had to remind himself: 

Work every day but enjoy the journey as well.

9. Discard the Program when you feel like it—but go back to it the next day. Concentrate. Narrow. Exclude.

A man way before his time, Henry pioneered the "2 Day Rule."

On the rare days where he could not get it going, he would give it up.

But he made sure to never miss twice.

10. Forget the books you want to write. Think only of the book you are writing.

As if he forgot the very first commandment, Henry reminds himself to stop chasing shiny new objects and focus on the book at hand.

There's a lesson here that he had to repeat this rule to himself.

11. Write first and always. Painting, music, friends, cinema, all these come afterward.

And to round it out, Henry finishes with the foundation of his Program:

Write first and always.

-

📌 And if you want more writing tips, here's a free 13,000-word Ultimate Guide on how to start writing online. 

Join over 150,000 people who finally started writing after reading it: https://lnkd.in/eeNFKWP7

outliers.hook: Over the last 5 years, I lost 100 pounds and wrote every morning for 90 minutes.
outliers.text:
Over the last 5 years, I lost 100 pounds and wrote every morning for 90 minutes.

These 3 small mindset shifts made this easy (despite wanting to give up hundreds of times):

99% of people know exactly what they *should* be doing. 

And it's following a simple, boring process. 

But no one wants to hear that. 

So they spend time (and waste money) on complex "solutions." 

Luckily, this 2-minute read will help you avoid that trap:

Everything good in life comes from compounding.

And compounding comes from doing:

• The basics
• Every single day
• For a long period of time

Said differently:

• Simplicity
• Consistency
• Patience

But this is hard to stick to.

Compounding takes time.

So most people give up before seeing results.

(Maybe you've felt this – starting something with a ton of motivation but quitting when you didn't see immediate progress.)

Luckily, there's a solution:

People quit things for 1 reason:

Dopamine.

Well, it's a *lack* of dopamine.

You see, most people set a goal and think:

"I'll be happy once I achieve this goal."

And this leaves our simple human brains *starving* for dopamine along the way.

Here's what to do instead:

We stick to things we enjoy.

And we enjoy things that give us dopamine.

So we need to *somehow* get dopamine from the monotonous consistency that leads to compounding.

I call this Process Dopamine.

And here's a 3-step system for unlocking it (so you can achieve any goal):

First, stop setting goals – at least the way you're currently setting them.

Instead, choose an outcome.

Then, switch the goal to:

"I want to enjoy every second on the journey to achieving X."

Here's why this works:

It puts the focus on the *process* instead of the *outcome*.

With this as your focus, you can spend time figuring out how to make the *process* enjoyable, which makes achieving the *outcome* inevitable.

And here are 2 ways to love the process:

First, optimize for progress.

Find a way to make:

• Tiny, constant gains
• Every day or every week
• For long periods of time

Trust me – you will get addicted to tiny bits of progress (and stick with it forever).

This is the key to unlocking Process Dopamine.

By optimizing for progress, it's easy to stay motivated.

But, motivation won't last forever.

Along the way, there will be plenty of times where you don't feel like doing the things you know you need to do.

Which is where the last mindset shift comes in – the Daily Leapfrog.

Every day you take another step on the path of monotonous consistency, picture this:

There are thousands of people who pursued a similar goal, but quit on *this exact day* on their journey.

So picture yourself *leapfrogging* all of those people.

(Silly, but it works!)

To recap the Process Dopamine system:

1. Stop setting outcome goals. Instead, set goals to *enjoy every second* on the path to achieving the outcome

2. Optimize your path for consistent progress

3. Use the Daily Leapfrog to stay motivated on the most boring days

outliers.hook: At Steve Jobs's memorial service, all 500 guests were given one book:
outliers.text:
At Steve Jobs's memorial service, all 500 guests were given one book:

Jobs had reread it every year for 38 years.

In 1972, while in high school, Steve Jobs discovered the "Autobiography of a Yogi" by Paramahansa Yogananda.

In Yogananda's teachings, Jobs found the secret to channeling consciousness into creativity:

• He meditated daily
• Became a vegetarian
• Studied Zen Buddhism

And these 5 ideas from "Autobiography of a Yogi" helped him build the $1 trillion Apple empire:

1. Self-realization.

Steve Jobs was drawn to the spiritual disciplines of Kriya Yoga:

• Transcend limitations
• Access intuitive wisdom
• Manifest your fullest potential

These routines unlocked Jobs's inner genius.

2. Yogananda's "no limitations" message became Jobs's leadership mantra.

For Jobs, Apple products must:

• Be different 
• Push boundaries
• Disrupt industries

Macs, iPods, and iPhones all came from a mind expanded by meditation.

3. Yogananda helped Jobs tap into his intuition.

He described it as "more powerful than intellect" and credited it for his biggest breakthroughs.

Through daily meditation, Jobs strengthened his intuitive muscles.

4. Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication.

Yogananda once said: 

"Be as simple as you can be; you will be astonished to see how uncomplicated and happy your life can become."

Jobs's obsession with simplicity came from his pursuit of inner clarity.

5. Passion is born of a deep sense of purpose.

Jobs had a relentless drive to put a "dent in the universe."

And to do that, he needed to be passionate. Because if he didn't have passion for what he was doing, he wouldn't have been able to continue building Apple, even when times got tough.

Jobs always saw himself as part of the spiritual lineage of innovators like Yogananda.

And his dying wish for the world echoes the core message of the yogi whose words shaped his life.

"You can't connect the dots looking forward," he famously said. "Only backwards."

Yogananda's memoir was his map.

-

Ghostwriting is how I made my first $10,000 on the internet.

outliers.hook: In 1991, Warren Buffett gifted Bill Gates his favorite book.
outliers.text:
In 1991, Warren Buffett gifted Bill Gates his favorite book.

Gates read it and said:

"This is the best business book I've ever read."

Business Adventures was written in 1969 by John Brooks.

It’s a collection of 12 real-life stories about ambition, failure, greed, and resilience.

Warren Buffett read Business Adventures in the 1960s, and it stuck with him for decades.

Each story in the book is a masterclass in:

• Psychology
• Leadership
• And decision-making

These are my 3 favorite stories & lessons:

1. Markets Are Psychological, Not Logical

On May 28, 1962, a stock market crash sent Wall Street into chaos.

The NYSE ticker ran 82 minutes behind. Trading volume hit 9.35M shares.

Men like Keith Funston, NYSE president, had no answers when reporters asked what was happening:

• No war had started
• No banks had failed
• No pandemic had broken out

Just pure fear spreading faster than the facts.

The S&P 500 dropped 22.5% over six months, and the Dow fell 5.7% on May 28 alone.

The market only recovered after the end of the Cuban Missile Crisis eased investor fears.

2. Vision Beats Validation

In 1957, Ford launched the Edsel after spending $250 million on development.

• 18 consumer research studies
• 2 years of focus groups
• Custom styling

Ford was sure they had created the perfect car.

But by 1960, Ford had discontinued the Edsel after losing $350 million.

Their market research missed something obvious:

Americans' tastes had shifted to smaller cars.

The data told them what customers *said*, not what they'd actually *buy*.

The lesson? 

Bold vision beats excessive validation when markets are changing.

3. Experience Transforms Skeptics Into Believers

In 1959, Xerox launched the 914 copier:

• IBM had rejected the technology as unmarketable in 1955
• Banks refused loans, calling it "too complicated" for offices
• It cost $27,500—the same as 5 cars

So Xerox got creative: 

Leasing cost of $95/month for 2,000 copies + 5-cent per copy.

Office managers quickly found countless uses for the machine. By 1965, Xerox copiers were producing millions of copies daily nationwide.

Now you know why "try before you buy" works.

In Business Adventures, Brooks' storytelling made complex business ideas unforgettable.

And this is why leaders need writers to turn their insights into relatable stories.

I experienced this firsthand when I made my first $10,000 online as a ghostwriter.

My clients needed someone to tell their stories the way Brooks explained these lessons.

Why?

Because industry leaders want to scale online but don't have the time to write.

This is why ghostwriting is such a lucrative writing opportunity.

Want to land high-paying ghostwriting clients?

This free, 5-day email course will show you how (so you can get paid to write & work with leaders in your niche).

Get instant access here: https://lnkd.in/eFEwtp_C

outliers.hook: Over the last 5 years, I've read 100+ books on writing.
outliers.text:
Over the last 5 years, I've read 100+ books on writing.

But 90% of them were a waste of time.

Read these and throw the rest away:

1. The Adweek Copywriting Handbook by Eugene Schwartz

The only copywriting book you'll ever need – full stop. 

Learn the art of capturing & keeping attention.

2. Writing That Works by Kenneth Roman and Joel Raphaelson

This is the only business writing book you need.

• Emails
• Resumes
• Letters
• Messages
• Speeches

This book will teach you how to craft anything business-related (in a way people will actually read).

3. The War of Art by Steven Pressfield

Writing is a daily battle with the Resistance.

But until you're aware of it, the Resistance will dominate you.

This book single-handled changed my life – once you read it, you'll never see creative work the same way.

4. On Writing Well by William Zinsser

The no-BS guide to writing effective non-fiction.

Filled with examples of classic mistakes that once you see, you'll never make again.

5. Atomic Habits by James Clear

This book has nothing to do with writing.

But you can use the four principles in this book to build your writing habit:

• Make it obvious
• Make it attractive
• Make it easy
• Make it satisfying

6. Show Your Work by Austin Kleon

The idea of the writer slaving away for months in solitude is no longer true.

The best writers are sharing all of their ideas in public, getting feedback, and iterating.

7. The Elements of Style by William Strunk

The most tactical book on this list. 

• Active vs. passive voice
• Positive vs. negative form
• Concise vs. verbose writing
• Lists of words to never use again

Keep this one on your desk.

8. The Boron Letters by Gary Halbert

Read this book for 1 reason:

To study Gary Halbert's incredible writing style.

This guy has an incredible way with words – a voice, rhythm, and tone that keeps your eyes glued to the page.

9. THE book that completely broke my brain about what it meant to write online:

The Art and Business of Online Writing by Nicolas Cole

• Why you shouldn't start a blog
• Generating an endless amount of ideas
• The "Golden Intersection" of telling personal stories

Truly epic.

That's it!

What would you add to this list? Let me know in the replies.

Here's exactly how I would build my business back:

 1.⁠ ⁠I'd identify what I call a 'fast advantage'. 

•⁠  ⁠A skillset that makes me stand out (writing)
•⁠  ⁠An experience that makes me relatable (failing)
•⁠  ⁠Social proof I could leverage (Forbes features)

This is how I'd go from 0 followers to 10k in a month.

 2.⁠ ⁠I'd create highly actionable content to get clients.

•⁠  ⁠Find the main pain points 1 person has.
•⁠  ⁠Create content on how I'd solve them.
•⁠  ⁠Repeat until I get my first lead. 

I.e: How to grow an email list using LinkedIn. 
I.e: How to tell viral stories on LinkedIn in 3 steps. 

 3.⁠ ⁠I'd have 2 main offers ready from day 1 to cash in fast. 

•⁠  ⁠A done-for-you offer ($3k+ service)
•⁠  ⁠A do-it-yourself offer (A $99 digital product)

Fastest way to win clients through content.

 4.⁠ ⁠I'd split content 50/50 stories and education. 

Stories will make me relatable to a broad audience.
Education will make me interesting to a niche audience. 

Using both, I'd build a profitable personal brand. 

 5.⁠ ⁠I'd write broad hooks but narrow and niche content.

Broad hook: "Here's a career hack that saved me"
Narrow re-hook: "After beating burnout at 27"
Niche content: "Start writing on LinkedIn"

Hits the spot for multiple audiences maximing my reach. 
I get to be in front of 10x the people with the same effort.

 6.⁠ ⁠I'd use social proof in 90% of my posts. 
 7.⁠ ⁠I'd use validated keywords in every hook. 

I.e: I worked with 100+ founders this year.
Here's how I taught them how to use AI to write.

→ "Worked with 100+ founders" is the social proof
→ "How to" + AI to write" are the right keywords

(I'll drop a list of them in the comments too). 

 8.⁠ ⁠I'd launch weekly lead magnets. 

•⁠  ⁠Solving a specific problem.
•⁠  ⁠Getting them to join my newsletter to get it.
•⁠  ⁠Emailing people who downloaded it with an offer. 

I.e: Breaking down Justin welsh's $1M funnel.

Helps gain followers and leads at the same time.
Win-win.

 9.⁠ ⁠I'd work for free for 3x people with massive personal brands on LinkedIn and use them as case studies. 

This is a career accelerator (if you do good work). 

10.⁠ ⁠I'd be in the DMs for 2 hours a day. 

•⁠  ⁠I'd message those who like and comment on my post.
•⁠  ⁠I'd message those who look at my profile. 

Wouldn't pitch, just ask the right leading questions and see if they are on the market for my service. 

That's literally it. 10 simple steps to making $$$ on LI. 

Fame is good, cash is great but both combined... 

Make you unstoppable. Trust me, I did it.

outliers.hook: You don't need algorithm hacks to grow on LinkedIn.
outliers.text:
You don't need algorithm hacks to grow on LinkedIn.

I spent the last 90 days testing every growth hack I could find. Here's what actually works:

(Based on analyzing content in different niches)

1. Hyper-specific content (3x a week)

• Solve one exact problem per post
• Use industry language your clients use
• Speak directly to one person

Adam Robinson has mastered this for B2B. 
Nausheen I. Chen is killing it for B2C. 

Address their problems directly. 

i.e "Stop losing credibility with your body language."

2. Proof-First Content

80% tactical step-by-step frameworks and screenshots.
20% stories on how you're doing it (use stories)

The 80% builds trust, the 20% engages them more. 

Educate and storytell. Always both. 

3. LinkedIn Video (let me explain...)

Video reach might be down right now.
But it's the fastest way to prove authority. 

LinkedIn video is ok.
Linkedin Lives? They're a goldmine.

We had a LinkedIn live with 200 people.
Made $10,000 from it. 

People buy from people they trust. Video accelerates this! 

I'd limit these to 2 a month, though, no pressure. 

I left a checklist for you in the image below for you to script your first Linkedin video too btw.

This is the no-bs blueprint for entrepreneurs. 

It's not about more volume, it's about more insights.

Dominating LinkedIn isn't just about virality right now.
It's about building a brand that earns trust.

Keep your focus on your 1 thing. 

The rest will follow.

I've just recorded a private video on how to dominate "storytelling" on LinkedIn like an expert. 

If you want it ♻️ repost this post and I'll send it to you (20 people max). 

PS: Which step do you struggle with the most? 

--

outliers.hook: I've read many books, but this one changed my life:
outliers.text:
I've read many books, but this one changed my life:

(And it’s not Rich Dad Poor Dad)

→ Influence by Robert Cialdini. 

Here's how to apply his 6 principles to win LinkedIn.

1. Reciprocity: Your free content? better than their paid
2. Consistency: Post and engage consistently
3. Social Proof: Your transformation is your best asset
4. Authority: Master one topic, become the go-to
5. Liking: Share your story, make yourself relatable
6. Scarcity: Become your own niche, be unique.
   
This book taught me more than my marketing degree.

I re-read it every 3 months. Its lessons are timeless. 

Master these principles → you'll become unstoppable.

Educate and storytell. Always both.
